philosophy: Dimir Tempo seeks to disrupt the opponent's early game with efficient discard and counterspells while deploying resilient, high-impact threats that outvalue the opponent over time. It wins by controlling the board state, dictating the pace of the game, and out-resourcing slower or combo-based strategies through superior card quality and tempo swings.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetchable sources), a turn-1 discard outlet or cantrip, and at least one efficient one-drop threat. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early interaction, or hands consisting solely of high-cost spells with no protection or cantrips to enable them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1 typically involves casting a cantrip or Thoughtseize to dig for threats and strip key early pieces. Turn 2 focuses on deploying a resilient threat or using counterspells to protect it while disrupting the opponent's plan. Land sequencing should prioritize fetching and dual-land bases to ensure consistent access to both colors, allowing you to cast interaction and threats on the same turn.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro and combo (Sneak and Show, Izzet Delver), bring in graveyard hate, additional discard, and spot removal. Against control and midrange (Tron, Ocelot Pride), bring in more counterspells, card advantage engines, and late-game finishers. Against the mirror, sideboard in additional disruption and threat-overlap cards to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a weapon; never let it sit empty if you can disrupt or develop. Manage hand size by using cantrips to filter into answers or threats. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection. Use the graveyard as a resource for discard outlets and recursion, but be mindful of opponent hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent lacks removal. Hold back counterspells for game-ending threats or to protect your own win conditions. Trade efficiently: use spot removal on early threats, save sweepers for board-stomps, and always prioritize protecting your own board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana bases and land drops to predict combo or ramp turns. Track the opponent's graveyard for recursion or flashback threats. Note when they start casting their second land drop on turn 2, as it often signals a midrange or control plan. Pay attention to when they hold up mana past turn 3, indicating a counterspell or protection spell.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact mid-game threats that demand an answer, combined with card advantage engines that out-value the opponent. Win by out-resourcing them in extended turns or closing out with a protected threat they cannot remove.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage secured, the opponent has no removal in hand, or you've established board dominance with protected threats. Commit when you can safely cast your win condition knowing you can protect it or trade up.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you're consistently one-sidedly removed, lack interaction against a locked board, or face a combo opponent who has assembled their win condition with no way to disrupt it. Also concede on dead hands, such as only high-cost spells against aggro or only removal against control.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig for answers or threats that are hard to counter. Employ layered interaction, such as discarding into a threat and then protecting it. Avoid overcommitting to the stack; wait for them to use their counterspell on a less important spell before playing your win condition.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to punish their slow start. Use discard to strip key ramp pieces or finishers. Prioritize fast interaction to disrupt their mana development before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early counterspells and spot removal. Use discard to strip key early threats. Transition to a slightly more controlling game plan, then close out with resilient threats once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats that demand immediate answers. Use discard to strip their counterspells and card advantage. Out-value them by playing around their removal with layered threats and cantrips.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by playing more efficiently. Prioritize card advantage and disruption. Trade up on the stack, and be willing to concede a minor board position to secure a key interaction. Sideboard in cards that beat the mirror's specific threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction density based on the metagame. Against combo and aggro, increase early interaction and hate cards. Against control and midrange, add more card advantage and late-game finishers. Always keep a flexible hand of sideboard cards that can address multiple archetypes, and remember to adjust your land count if you're bringing in significant non-basic cards.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without protection, hoarding counterspells until it's too late, and failing to use cantrips effectively. Dimir Tempo thrives on information and tempo; always prioritize playing around key opponent threats rather than reacting to them. Remember that discard is both a tool and a resource—use it to dig and disrupt simultaneously.

philosophy: Tron is a fast combo-ramp deck that wins by assembling three specific artifacts to generate massive amounts of mana, then casting a high-impact threat like Ugin, the Spirit Dragon. It aims to outpace midrange, control, and combo decks by accelerating into game-ending threats before opponents can stabilize or interact effectively.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2+ lands and a mana acceleration piece (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, Chalice of the Void) or a Tron piece. Keep hands with 3+ lands if you have no acceleration but have fetch lands. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, or 3+ lands with no acceleration/Tron pieces. Against combo, keep hands with 2+ lands and interaction (Surgical Strike, Dismember).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet/Chalice, or fetch a dual land. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a Tron piece or acceleration, or fetch another land. Prioritize untapped lands when possible, but never skip casting acceleration for a tapped land. Sequence fetches to find Shivan Reef or Ancient Tomb for early mana, then play Tron pieces. If you hit Tron on turn 2 or 3, cast your threat immediately.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Midrange (Ocelot Pride, Mono-Red): Bring in 2x Engineered Explosives, 2x Cursed Totem, 2x Surgical Strike, 2x Dismember. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Izzet Delver): Bring in 2x Engineered Explosives, 2x Cursed Totem, 2x Surgical Strike, 2x Dismember, 1x Rest in Peace. vs Combo (Sneak and Show): Bring in 2x Engineered Explosives, 2x Cursed Totem, 2x Surgical Strike, 2x Dismember, 1x Rest in Peace. vs Mirror: Bring in 2x Engineered Explosives, 2x Cursed Totem, 2x Surgical Strike, 2x Dismember, 1x Rest in Peace.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; never hoard mana past turn 3 unless you're holding up counterspells or waiting for a specific threat. Use Candelabra of Tawnos to recycle lands for extra mana, but be mindful of the 2-mana cost. Protect your Tron pieces with Chalice of the Void (set to 0 or 1) and countermagic. Graveyard is generally irrelevant unless facing Rest in Peace; otherwise, focus on board presence.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit your threat as soon as you have the mana and a reasonable expectation of surviving the opponent's response window. If you have a Ugin, you can often afford to be slightly more aggressive. If you're facing a control deck, hold back a Tron piece or two to bait out removal/counterspells before committing your finisher.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Mana Leak, Spell Pierce, Daze), targeted removal (Surgical Strike, Dismember), and sweepers (Engineered Explosives, Cursed Totem). Pay attention to how many lands they play; control decks often play extra lands to stabilize. Note if they're holding up mana for multiple turns, indicating a counterspell or sweeper.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ugin, the Spirit Dragon is the primary win condition, providing card advantage and board wipe. If Ugin is dead or you're ahead, cast a second Ugin or use your mana to attack with creatures if applicable. In the mirror, winning the resource war and out-resourcing with Ugin or a second Tron turn is key.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have assembled Tron, have a threat in hand, and your opponent has no clear answer in hand or on board. If you're ahead on board, commit to attacking and casting your threat to close the game. If you're behind, commit to casting your threat immediately to stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo deck and you have no interaction or win condition, or if you're in a bad mirror match with a dead hand (no lands, no Tron pieces, no answers). Also concede if you're facing a control deck that has locked you out with multiple sweepers and you have no way to break through.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Candelabra of Tawnos to generate mana for multiple threats or to cast your threat in response to a counterspell. Set Chalice to 0 to counter their cheap counterspells. Use fetch lands to find untapped lands to cast threats in response to counterspells. If they have multiple counters, consider casting your threat at end step or using a cheap threat like Ugin (which is a planeswalker, not a spell, so it bypasses counterspells).
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-accelerate them by prioritizing Tron assembly. Use Cursed Totem to destroy their mana rocks. If they go wide, use Engineered Explosives set to 2 or 3. Ugin's -3 loyalty ability can remove key threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize survival. Cast Chalice to 0 or 1 to disrupt their cheap spells. Use Surgical Strike/Dismember to remove key threats. Cast Ugin to wipe the board and draw cards. If you can't stabilize, consider mulliganing aggressively pre-game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure them with early threats. Use Candelabra to generate mana for multiple turns. Bait out their counterspells with cheap threats before committing your finisher. If they try to sweep, have a backup threat or use Ugin's card draw to out-value them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize land drops and Tron assembly. Use countermagic to protect your threats and disrupt theirs. Ugin is the key; winning the planeswalker war usually decides the match. Be willing to trade Tron pieces for theirs if it gives you a tempo advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, you become more reactive. You'll have more interaction, so you can afford to play more conservatively early. Focus on surviving the early game, then out-value opponents with Ugin and card draw. Adjust your mulligan strategy to keep hands with interaction if you're facing combo or aggro. In the mirror, prioritize hands with countermagic and Tron pieces.
notes: Tron is highly sensitive to land drops; always prioritize playing lands over casting spells if you're behind on mana. Candelabra of Tawnos is incredibly powerful but requires careful mana management; don't overextend into sweepers. The One Ring is a powerful card advantage engine but can be a liability against Rest in Peace; consider mulliganing if you expect heavy graveyard hate. Common mistake: holding up mana too long waiting for the 'perfect' turn to cast your threat; in Tron, tempo is everything, so cast your threat as soon as you can.

philosophy: Sneak and Show is a fast, combo-oriented deck that leverages mana acceleration and cheap spells to cheat massive threats onto the battlefield via Show and Tell or Sneak Attack. It aims to win before opponents can stabilize or interact, excelling against slower, resource-heavy decks while relying on precise sequencing and discard to navigate interactive matchups.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands plus at least one acceleration source (Lotus Petal, Dark Ritual, Chrome Mox, etc.) and a win condition (Show and Tell, Sneak Attack, or a creature to cheat). Keep hands with Thoughtseize/Liliana of the Veil if paired with a land or acceleration. Mulligan pure land draws, hands with only threats and no acceleration, hands with multiple copies of the same key spell without mana support, or hands with zero interaction against interactive formats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lotus Petal/Dark Ritual, activate for mana, cast Show and Tell or Sneak Attack, and put a threat onto the battlefield. If holding discard, use it on turn 1 to strip key answers or combo pieces. Turn 2: Often the win turn if turn 1 was disrupted, or continue accelerating/attacking with Sneak Attack creatures. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast acceleration spells without flooding, and prioritize fetching basic lands if your deck runs duals that require specific colors for protection or interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride): Bring in discard (Thoughtseize, Inquisition of the Mind), counterspells (Force of Will, Daze, Spell Pierce), and fast interaction (Lightning Bolt, Go for the Throat). Against control/ramp (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in additional discard, graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Grafdigger's Cage), and alternative win conditions (Thassa's Oracle, Emrakul, the Aeons Torn). Against mirror (Sneak and Show): Bring in discard, counters, and hate for key threats (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Flusterstorm).

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your most critical resource; always prioritize keeping open mana for acceleration and key spells. Manage hand by using discard early to disrupt, but don't overcommit if you have no win condition. Graveyard is a resource for recursion (Liliana, Fetchlands) but also a vulnerability; be mindful of graveyard hate and avoid overloading it with dead cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can protect them or win immediately. If you cheat in a fragile threat, follow up with protection or combo pieces. Hold back if the opponent has clear interaction (counters, removal) and you lack backup. Never attack with a Sneak Attack creature if you cannot close the game or have no way to protect it from removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Force of Will, Daze, Spell Pierce), removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Bolt), and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for interaction versus when they hold up mana for protection. Track if they are playing a control shell (holding multiple answers) or a combo shell (going for the win). Pay attention to whether they are playing fetchlands or shock lands, as this indicates their mana base and potential for quick interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Progenitus or Emrakul for board wipes and protection, Kiki-Jiki, Niavar's Genius with Mirror Image for infinite combat, Thassa's Oracle with Tendrils of Agony for a combo finish, or Sneak Attack creatures attacking twice. Get there by accelerating into Show and Tell/Sneak Attack, or by setting up the combo pieces in hand/graveyard through discard or recursion.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have the full combo package in hand/graveyard, when the opponent has no interaction left, or when you can win before they can respond. Also commit if you are ahead on board and can close out the game, or if the opponent is tapped out for a key answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are behind on board with no acceleration or win conditions, if your key spells are countered/hated, or if you are in a dead matchup with no sideboard answers. Also concede if you are forced to trade your only win condition for a minor tempo gain or if the opponent has stabilized with multiple board wipes and you lack recursion.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to strip counterspells, cast acceleration spells that are hard to counter, sequence Show and Tell/Sneak Attack when they are tapped out, or use uncounterable threats. If they have multiple counters, consider going for a cheaper win condition or using free spells (like Force of Will if in your list) to protect your combo.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with your own acceleration, use discard to strip their mana rocks, or bring in graveyard hate and board wipes. If they are ahead, use counterspells and discard to disrupt their key spells, and look for ways to win before they can cast their massive threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use discard to strip early threats, counterspells to stall, and your own win condition to race. Do not overextend; stabilize with interaction and close the game quickly. If you cannot stabilize, consider going for a faster win condition or using your discard to disrupt their key turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use discard to strip answers, and win before they can stabilize. If they have multiple counters, sequence your spells carefully or use uncounterable threats. Consider going for a combo finish if they are locked out of interaction, or use your discard to remove their key answers before committing to the win.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards, using discard effectively, and sideboarding in hate for their key threats. Prioritize keeping hands with acceleration and win conditions, and do not overcommit if they have backup interaction. Track their graveyard and hand to anticipate their win condition, and be ready to disrupt it with discard or counters.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your game plan based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, prioritize discard and graveyard hate to disrupt their strategy. Against aggro, lean into counterspells and fast interaction to stall until you can win. In the mirror, side in more counters and discard to out-resource your opponent. Always ensure your sideboard does not dilute your core win conditions; keep at least two copies of Show and Tell/Sneak Attack and your primary threats, and avoid bringing in too many single-use cards that may not see play.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to a single win condition without backup, mulliganing too aggressively (you need acceleration plus win condition), ignoring the opponent's interaction, and failing to sequence lands properly. Unique interactions include using fetchlands to tutor for lands or shuffle away unwanted cards, and activating Lotus Petal for mana even if you have no spells to cast immediately (though it is dead if not used). Always be mindful of the stack and priority when casting Show and Tell/Sneak Attack, and practice your turn sequences to ensure you can execute under pressure.

philosophy: Apply early pressure with Delver of Secrets while disrupting the opponent’s game plan with cheap instant-speed interaction. Win by racing midrange/control decks, comboing out with Flow State, or out-resourcing tempo matchups through card advantage and efficient removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Delver of Secrets, and at least two cheap instants (Opt, Lightning Bolt, Spell Pierce, or Thought Scorn). Prioritize hands that can cast two instants by turn 2 to flip Delver. Mulligan flood, pure removal, or hands missing Delver/instants unless they contain multiple answers to specific meta threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Opt or Spell Pierce into a land, or Bolt a potential threat. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a second instant, and flip Delver into Inverter of Truth. If holding Delver, play it on turn 1 or 2 and cast an instant to flip it. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast two instants by turn 2 without tapping out completely if you expect disruption.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control/tempo: Bring in additional counterspells (Flusterstorm, Spell Pierce), discard (Thoughtseize), and Chalice of the Void. Against aggro: Add more removal (Lightning Bolt, Go for the Throat if in list), counters, and Chalice. Against combo (Sneak & Show): Bring in Chalice, Flusterstorm, and discard to disrupt the package. Against mirror: Keep the core, add more card advantage (Thought Scorn, Opt) and hatebears to protect your Delver.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource for both interaction and threat development. Never tap out for Delver if you have no way to protect it against hard counters. Use Opt/Thought Scorn to dig for answers or finishers. Preserve graveyard for Flow State or avoid it if opponent runs graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Delver when you have at least one counter/removal to protect it, or when you can immediately follow up with Flow State. Hold back interaction if the opponent is on a combo that requires specific pieces; save counters for their key spells. Don't overextend into Tron or Ocelot Pride without a clear path to combo or removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for hard counters (Counterspell, Mana Leak, Flusterstorm) and sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods). Track opponent’s hand for combo pieces (Colossus, Aetherling, Ocelot Pride) or ramp targets. Note when they tap out for threats vs. when they hold up interaction. Pay attention to whether they’re trying to stabilize or go off.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Flow State + Inverter of Truth is the primary win condition, dealing infinite damage. Secondary wins come from Inverter + Lightning Bolt/Spell Pierce, or out-racing with Delver pressure and efficient removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal or counterspells in hand, when you can safely cast Flow State on your turn, or when you’re ahead on board and can close out the game before they stabilize. Commit to the combo even if it means tapping out, provided you’ve verified their hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re behind on board, opponent has multiple hard counters or sweepers, you have no way to protect Delver or cast Flow State, and your hand lacks interaction or finishers. Also concede if you’re facing a locked combo that you cannot disrupt and have no win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Chalice of the Void on 1 or 2, Flusterstorm/Spell Pierce to protect your instants, and Opt/Thought Scorn to dig around counters. Overcommit with Delver when you suspect they’re out of counters, or use Flow State to win before they can respond.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Chalice on 1, Flusterstorm, and discard to disrupt their mana rocks. Pressure with Delver to race them before they assemble Tron or Ocelot Pride. Use Flow State to win before they stabilize or combo off.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Counterspell their early threats, Bolt/Scorn their creatures, and Chalice on 1. Delver pressure forces them to trade, while Flow State can close the game if they overcommit. Don’t let them stabilize; keep pressure high.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with Delver and cheap instants. Use Chalice to disrupt their mana or key spells. Dig for Flow State and combo when they tap out for sweepers or counters. Avoid overextending; trade efficiently and wait for the right moment to go off.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through card advantage (Opt, Thought Scorn) and better sequencing. Chalice on 1 or 2 to disrupt their Delver/instants. Flusterstorm to protect your flips or disrupt theirs. Race with Delver, protect your Inverter, and use Flow State timing to out-combo them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific matchup. Against control, prioritize counters and discard to protect your combo. Against aggro, add removal and counters to stabilize. Against combo, bring in hatebears and disruption to break their package. In the mirror, focus on card advantage and Chalice to control the board. Always keep at least one copy of Chalice and Flusterstorm unless the meta dictates otherwise.
notes: Flow State must be cast on your turn to trigger infinite damage with Inverter. Don’t tap out for Delver if you suspect a hard counter unless you have a way to protect it or win immediately. Chalice of the Void is often the most impactful sideboard card; prioritize it. Use Opt/Thought Scorn to dig for answers, not just to flip Delver. Be mindful of graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) which can shut down Flow State or Delver’s recursion.

philosophy: Establish a resilient board of Nacatl tokens and use Ocelot Pride as a scalable combat pump engine to out-value opponents. The deck wins by leveraging efficient trades, combat tricks, and card advantage to close out games faster than control can answer and more resiliently than aggro can race.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 3+ lands (prioritize fetch/shock-compatible or untap-entering lands), plus a turn-2 play (Ocelot Pride, Nacatl Pariah, or a combat trick). Keep 2 lands only if paired with Ocelot Pride, Ajani, or two cheap cantrips/removal. Mulligan on 2 lands with no early plays, or 3+ lands with zero interactive/early threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cantrip or hold for turn 2. Turn 2: Cast Nacatl Pariah or Ocelot Pride. If Pariah, attack immediately, pump it, and look to trade or establish board presence. Sequence lands to guarantee 2-drop and 3-drop turns. Use Ocelot Pride on the turn it resolves if you have a token to pump, or hold it for a favorable combat phase.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Dimir Tempo, Sneak & Show): +2/1 Board Wipes, +1/2 Hard Removal, +1 Combat Trick. Vs Control/Ramp (Tron, Izzet Delver): +2/1 Additional Threats, +1 Card Draw, +1 Protection. Vs Mirror: +1/2 Targeted Removal, +1 Efficient Pump, +1 Hate Artifact/Enchantment if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Avoid overcommitting mana to pumps without board presence or card advantage. Use Ocelot Pride to trade up (pump to kill a larger creature, then sacrifice a token to refill). Cantrip into answers to maintain hand size. Graveyard is generally irrelevant; focus on board and hand resources.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no sweepers or has blown interaction. Hold back if they have untapped mana for removal or a sweeper in hand. Use Ocelot Pride to force unfavorable trades for the opponent, preserving your board state. Don't overextend into known sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Anger of the Gods, Wrath of God), hard removal (Path to Exile, Doom Blade), and combo finishers. Note when opponents tap out for interaction versus holding up mana. Track how many removal spells they've seen to calculate your risk of overcommitting.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ocelot Pride pumped to lethal, Ajani's ultimate, or a combat trick on a large threat. Win by trading efficiently, pushing damage through unblocked, and closing in 1-2 combat phases once you have card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can mathematically close the game before they can stabilize. Commit when Ocelot Pride can trade into their best removal and still leave you ahead.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single unprotectable threat against a deck with multiple sweepers/hard removal, or if you're facing a combo deck that has already assembled its win condition. Also concede if you're consistently drawing dead lands and no interactive spells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast Ocelot Pride with a cheap cantrip to ensure it resolves, or play multiple threats in a row to force counter-sacrifice. Use it as a late-game finisher when they've exhausted interaction. Don't rely on it resolving early if they have heavy counter support.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to force answers before they hit their big spells. Use efficient removal on their mana sources or key threats. Don't let them stabilize; trade aggressively and close the game before their late-game threats come online.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and combat tricks. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and close the game quickly once stabilized. Use Ocelot Pride to trade into their largest threats and regain board control.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats to force them to use multiple answers. Use Ocelot Pride to trade into their removal and out-value them with card draw. Force them to make difficult choices between answering your board and developing their own.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, efficient trades, and timing your pumps to maximize value. Sideboard in targeted removal and protection. Out-value them by trading up and closing the game before they can stabilize.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Generally, bring in more interaction against control/combo, more threats against aggro/midrange, and specific hate pieces. Keep the core game plan intact but optimize for matchup-specific threats. Prioritize cards that either stabilize early or close games quickly.
notes: Common mistake: Over-pumping Ocelot Pride without board presence or card advantage. Unique interaction: Ocelot Pride's ability triggers on combat damage, so timing pumps matters. Don't forget to use Ajani's +1 to generate tokens or his -2 to draw cards. Watch out for sacrifice outlets in the meta, as they can disrupt your token strategy. Always track opponent's removal count to calculate risk.

philosophy: Oops! All Spells is a fast combo deck that aims to cast an overwhelming number of cheap spells in a single turn to draw cards, generate tokens, and win via combat or board state. It thrives on outpacing control and combo decks while using discard and spot interaction to protect its fragile but explosive game plan.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ mana sources (including cantrips/artifacts like Lotus Petal, Mishra's Bauble, or Mox Opal) and at least one key piece (Agadeem's Awakening, Karn, Cauldron Familiar, or Balustrade Spy). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no spells, or hands with only one mana source and no draw/disruption spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lotus Petal/Mishra's Bauble for mana, or cast Thoughtseize/Inquisition to disrupt. Turn 2: Cast Mox Opal/Balustrade Spy, draw cards, and start assembling the spell engine. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast multiple instant-speed spells by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Control: Bring in Flusterstorm, Spell Pierce, Daze, Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Tormod's Crypt. vs Combo/Graveyard: Bring in Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Tormod's Crypt, Surgical Extraction, Grafdigger's Cage. vs Mirror: Bring in Flusterstorm, Spell Pierce, Thoughtseize, Duress, Abrupt Decay, Tormod's Crypt.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping cheap artifacts and discard in hand for protection. Use Cauldron Familiar to flashback spells from the graveyard to fuel Balustrade Spy and Agadeem's Awakening. Don't overcommit to the graveyard if facing Rest in Peace; pivot to hand disruption.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting Agadeem's Awakening until you have enough cheap spells in hand/graveyard to draw cards and generate tokens safely. Use discard and spot interaction to clear threats before going off.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Flusterstorm, Spell Pierce, Force of Will), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Tormod's Crypt), and board wipes. Note when opponents tap out for interaction vs. when they're holding up mana for protection.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Agadeem's Awakening is the primary finisher, drawing cards and creating Saproling tokens to win via combat or overwhelming board state. Karn, the Great Creator can also close games by making artifacts cost less or tutoring for pieces.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ mana sources, at least one cheap artifact or discard, and a clear path to casting Agadeem's Awakening without being countered or disrupted.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard-lock combo (e.g., Sneak and Show with no interaction), have a dead hand against Rest in Peace/Leyline of the Void, or are down to 1 life with no way to protect your combo pieces.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap spells to out-value counters, cast Agadeem's Awakening as the last spell in a chain, or use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Flusterstorm can also protect your key spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and cheap artifacts. Use Tormod's Crypt or Surgical Extraction to remove their mana rocks. Combo off quickly before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Thoughtseize/Inquisition to remove key threats, Flusterstorm/Spell Pierce to protect your combo, and Tormod's Crypt for graveyard decks. Stabilize and go off on turn 3-4.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with discard to remove counterspells and protection. Use Flusterstorm to protect your combo pieces. Agadeem's Awakening is hard to answer if you have enough spells to draw into it and win.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war with Flusterstorm, Spell Pierce, and Thoughtseize. Prioritize graveyard hate (Tormod's Crypt) to disrupt their recursion. Out-value them with better sequencing and card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize interaction and hate cards against the metagame. Against control, lean into Flusterstorm and Spell Pierce to protect your combo. Against graveyard/combo, bring in Rest in Peace and Leyline of the Void. Against midrange/aggro, keep discard and spot removal. Adjust your land base if needed, but keep the mana fast.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the graveyard against Rest in Peace. Always have a backup plan with hand disruption. Balustrade Spy is crucial for card advantage, so protect it. Agadeem's Awakening wins via combat if you have enough Saprolings, so don't forget to attack. Watch for Leyline of the Void in opening hand; if opponent has it, consider mulliganing or playing around it with discard.

philosophy: Eldrazi is a midrange-control archetype that leverages mana acceleration and targeted disruption to out-value opponents, aiming to stabilize the early game and overwhelm them with unblockable, game-ending threats. It excels against combo and control decks by disrupting their resources while out-resolving them with card advantage engines.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with two or more mana sources plus a Lotus Petal, Chalice of the Void, or Kozilek's Command. Mulligan on three or more lands with no early plays, or hands with only one land and no disruption or acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then Lotus Petal -> Kozilek's Command (discard a threat or bounce an opponent's spell) or Chalice of the Void (X=1 or 2). Turn 2: Play a land, cast Thought-Knot Seer or Reality Smasher. If you have three Arcane Signs, assemble Tron for massive mana acceleration.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride), bring in Surgical Extraction, Rest in Peace, and additional Kozilek's Command for bounce. Against control (Dimir Tempo, Tron), bring in more Kozilek's Command, Thought-Knot Seer, and discard effects. Against combo (Sneak and Show), bring in Surgical Extraction, Rest in Peace, and graveyard hate. In the mirror, bring in Kozilek's Command and Surgical Extraction to disrupt their graveyard and bounce threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for interaction; avoid overcommitting to Chalice without a follow-up threat. Use Kozilek's Command to protect your graveyard or bounce key threats, and prioritize card advantage from Ulamog/Emrakul to out-resource opponents.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or bounce ready for their interaction. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy graveyard hate, and prioritize killing small threats before deploying massive Eldrazi.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponents holding up mana on turns 2-3, which often indicates counterspells or discard. Track their graveyard for combo pieces or graveyard hate, and note when they sacrifice lands for Tron to anticipate a massive mana turn.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ulamog, the Ceaseless Hunger and Emrakul, the Promised End win the game by exiling permanents, drawing cards, and shuffling the opponent's graveyard. Assemble Tron or use Lotus Petal/Kozilek's Command to generate the 7+ mana needed to cast them.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and they lack sweepers or graveyard hate. Commit when you can protect your finisher or win before they can respond.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, the opponent has a sweeper or Rest in Peace, and you have no way to break through. Also concede against combo decks that have already assembled their wincon and you lack interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek's Command to bounce counterspells or threats, cast threats in response to counterspells, and leverage Tron for massive mana to overwhelm interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Deploy Chalice of the Void (X=1 or 2) to shut down mana rocks, use Thought-Knot Seer to discard key pieces, and Kozilek's Command to bounce ramp accelerants.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play Chalice (X=1) to shut down cheap spells, Thought-Knot Seer to discard threats, and Kozilek's Command to bounce early attackers. Deploy Reality Smasher to block and apply pressure.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply early pressure with Seer/Smasher, use Kozilek's Command to disrupt their hand, and win through card advantage from Ulamog/Emrakul. Avoid overextending into sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race to card advantage. Prioritize Kozilek's Command to bounce their threats or exile their graveyard, use Surgical Extraction to remove their graveyard hate, and win by deploying Emrakul/Ulamog first.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from early disruption to removal and graveyard hate against control and combo. Increase card advantage pieces if facing discard-heavy decks, and adjust Chalice targets based on the metagame. Generally, bring in more interaction against control, more hate against combo, and more pressure against aggro.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Chalice without follow-up, misusing Lotus Petal on non-critical turns, and failing to track the opponent's graveyard. Unique interactions: Kozilek's Command can bounce your own Chalice to reuse it, or bounce your own Petal for extra mana. Emrakul's shuffle effect is crucial against graveyard combos.

philosophy: Out-value opponents by generating disproportionate mana and card advantage through land acceleration and digging, then overwhelm with late-game threats or combo finishes. Excels against slow, resource-heavy decks but struggles against fast, disruptive aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 4-5 lands (especially fetches, duals, or signets), Mox Diamond, Life from the Loam, Exploration, or early threats like Tectonic Giant/Thrun. Mulligan to hands that can play a land or rock on turn 1, or dig for consistency. Keep any hand with Mox Diamond + fetch land, or Loam + 2 lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Diamond if possible, or fetch a dual. Turn 2: Cast Life from the Loam to dig 2-3 cards, play a second land, then cast another mana rock or Exploration. Sequence: Fetch -> Land -> Dig/Rock -> Land -> Rock/Exploration. Prioritize acceleration over threats until turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Delver/Ocelot): +2 Tectonic Giant, +2 Thrun, +1 Breaker of Silence, -2 Exploration, -2 Mox Diamond. Vs Control/Tron: +1 Rest in Peace, +2 Thrun, -2 Mox Diamond, -1 Exploration. Vs Sneak & Show: +2 Rest in Peace, +1 Breaker of Silence, -2 Exploration. Vs Mirror: Keep mainboard, +1 Thrun, -2 Mox Diamond.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana acceleration and digging over card draw. Use Life from the Loam to dig 2-3 cards, but avoid digging into lands when you need threats. Manage your graveyard carefully against hate, and sequence mana rocks to ensure you can cast Exploration or threats when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back early threats until you have 5+ mana or a clear path to victory. Commit when you can protect your board with multiple threats or have enough mana to answer removal. Avoid trading creatures prematurely; focus on out-valuing the opponent.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard, and graveyard hate. Note if the opponent is playing Tron (watch for Urza's lands) or Sneak & Show (watch for graveyard recursion). Track their mana base to predict when they can answer your threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Tectonic Giant, Thrun, Breaker of Silence, or casting Exploration with 4+ lands in play to win on the spot. Secure these by protecting them with multiple threats or using mana rocks to cast around removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ mana, a threat on board, and enough pressure to close the game. Commit to Exploration only if you have 4+ lands and a guaranteed win, or if the opponent has no answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against fast aggro with no early threats or dig spells, or if your graveyard is completely shut down and you cannot cast Exploration. Also concede if you are out-valued in card advantage and have no path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast multiple low-cost threats to force counter trade-offs, use Life from the Loam to dig for answers, and sequence mana rocks to cast around counters. If possible, cast Exploration in response to counterspells or use it from the graveyard if supported.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with Life from the Loam and Exploration. Do not trade creatures; focus on generating more mana and card advantage to win the late game. Use Thrun or Breaker of Silence to protect your board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Mulligan aggressively for early threats like Tectonic Giant or Thrun. Use Life from the Loam to dig for answers and stabilize. Apply early pressure to force the opponent to answer you, then close the game with a massive threat.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with threats and use Life from the Loam to out-value their draw spells. Win by generating more card advantage and mana than they can answer. Protect your threats with multiple board presence or removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It is a Life from the Loam and Exploration race. Win by having better sequencing, more threats, and graveyard hate. Prioritize digging for threats over lands, and use Thrun or Breaker of Silence to protect your board while out-valuing your opponent.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust threat density based on the metagame. Against control and combo, increase threat count and add graveyard hate. Against aggro, prioritize early threats and discard. Generally, sideboard in more threats and hate for specific strategies, while reducing mana rocks and Exploration if they are being targeted.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Exploration without a finisher, mismanaging Life from the Loam by digging into lands when you need threats, and failing to mulligan aggressively for early plays. Unique interactions: Mox Diamond can be sacrificed for 2 mana, Life from the Loam can fetch any land, and Exploration can be cast from the graveyard if supported by effects like Reanimate or Undying Evil (though rare in Modern). Always track the opponent's graveyard and mana base to predict their answers.

philosophy: A fragile but explosive control-combo deck that uses targeted disruption and card advantage to assemble a precise five-card hand, then leverages Doomsday to instantly win or establish an unbreakable board state. It wins by out-resourcing disruption-heavy midrange and control decks while outpacing faster combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands containing Doomsday plus two or more enablers/tutors (Tamiyo, Flow State, Thoughtseize, Duress) and two or more basic lands. Keep hands with three or more enablers/tutors and two lands if you can reliably find Doomsday. Mulligan hands with fewer than two lands, Doomsday with zero enablers, or hands with only enablers and no reliable way to fetch Doomsday.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize or Duress to remove opponent's combo pieces, counterspells, or ramp sources. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Flow State or Tamiyo to tutor for Doomsday or additional enablers. Sequence mana to ensure you can disrupt on turn 1 and assemble on turn 2 or 3. If opening with multiple lands, consider holding a land drop to guarantee you can cast Doomsday when ready.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Dimir Tempo and Ocelot Pride Midrange: Bring in Force of Will, Mana Leak, Rest in Peace, and additional disruption. Against Sneak and Show and Izzet Delver: Bring in Force of Will, Lightning Helix, Surgical Extraction, and extra enablers. Against Tron: Bring in artifact/enchantment removal, additional disruption, and sideboard hate for mana bases. Against mirror: Bring in Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and extra disruption to protect your combo while disrupting theirs.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for Doomsday's five mana cost and protect your hand size, as Doomsday requires exactly five cards. Use Tamiyo to mill or search efficiently, and leverage Flow State's graveyard recursion to tutor without losing card advantage. Avoid drawing extra cards once you have the full five-card combo assembled.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing threats until you have the complete five-card hand. Use disruption to clear the path for Doomsday. If the opponent has a clear win condition, prioritize disruption over combo assembly. If you have a lethal setup, commit immediately without overthinking card advantage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Force of Will, Counterspell), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and fast combo threats. Note when opponents play lands suggesting ramp or control. Track their hand size and graveyard contents to anticipate their next move and time your Doomsday accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Doomsday plus four other cards in your graveyard and one in hand. Common UB/G finishers include Mindslaver, Time Spiral, or Empty Library setups. Once Doomsday resolves, arrange the four graveyard cards on top of your library to win instantly or set up an unbeatable state, then play the top card if possible.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have exactly five cards in hand, including Doomsday and four enablers/wincons. When the opponent has no clear answer to Doomsday or has already used their interaction. When you can protect the combo with disruption, counters, or graveyard protection.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: When you have no way to assemble the combo (e.g., Doomsday milled, no tutors), the opponent has established an unbreakable board (Tron, Ocelot Pride), or you are facing graveyard hate that completely shuts you down with no sideboard answers. Also concede if you are forced to draw extra cards before casting Doomsday.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Force of Will, Flash, or split your combo assembly to avoid single-target counters. Cast Doomsday in response to their counterspells if you have protection. Use Tamiyo and Flow State to set up multiple threats or win conditions that are harder to counter, and prioritize disrupting their hand early.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Disrupt their mana base early with Thoughtseize and Duress. Use Force of Will to protect your combo. If they get ahead, use Tamiyo to mill or search for a faster wincon. Bring in sideboard hate and artifact/enchantment removal to slow their engine.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early disruption to remove key threats. Use Force of Will to protect your combo. If you can't stabilize, concede early. Tamiyo can mill to find Doomsday faster, but only if you can survive the initial turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage (Tamiyo, Flow State). Use disruption to remove their key answers. Set up a win condition that is hard to interact with, and avoid overcommitting to the board. Force them to spend resources on your combo while you assemble it.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Prioritize disruption to remove their Doomsday or enablers. Use Force of Will to protect your combo. Track their graveyard and hand size. If they have Doomsday, use Surgical Extraction or Rest in Peace. Win by going first, having better disruption, or out-resolving their Doomsday setup.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo assembly to a more disruptive and resilient game plan. Bring in graveyard hate against combo and midrange, additional disruption against control, and fast interaction against aggro. Adjust your land base if the metagame demands more basics or duals. Focus on protecting your combo while disrupting theirs, and be willing to concede early if the matchup is completely shut down.
notes: Doomsday requires exactly five cards in hand; drawing extra cards before casting it will break the combo. Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student can mill or search, but be careful not to mill Doomsday into your graveyard unless you have a way to retrieve it. Flow State can be cast from the graveyard, making it resilient to discard. Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board, drawing too many cards before casting Doomsday, and failing to track opponent's hand size. Always prioritize disruption and hand integrity over tempo.

philosophy: The EPIC Storm is a fast, explosive combo deck that aims to assemble ephemeral mana and card draw to cast Tendrils of Agony or alternative win conditions by turn 3-4. It wins by out-pacing control, combo, and midrange archetypes through hand disruption, graveyard recursion, and overwhelming card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2+ fast mana sources (Mox Opal, Lotus Petal, Chrome Mox, Mana Crypt) and a land, or 3+ fast mana with a cantrip/discard. Keep hands with Burning Wish + 2+ fast mana + a land. Mulligan hands with 0 fast mana, 3+ lands, or only one land and no cantrips/discard. Prioritize hands that can sequence mana into draws on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, fetch a fast mana source (e.g., Polluted Delta for Mox Opal), or cast a cantrip/discard if you have a free mana source. Turn 2: Assemble additional mana, draw cards with Brainstorm/Ponder/Preordain, and begin sequencing toward a Tendrils win or set up a Burning Wish target. Prioritize fetching dual lands that enter untapped only if you need to hold priority; otherwise, fast mana dictates aggressive fetching.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Control: Bring in Tormod's Crypt, Pyroclasm, and/or Rest in Peace. vs Combo/Ramp: Bring in Tormod's Crypt, Pyroclasm, and additional discard. vs Mirror: Bring in extra Burning Wish targets, Tormod's Crypt, and consider additional discard. Generally, sideboard in graveyard hate and board wipes, removing redundant fast mana or cantrips based on metagame density.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a one-time explosive resource; sequence draws to maximize value. Keep the graveyard stocked with Tendrils, Fire/Ice, and Wish targets. Don't overcommit to drawing if you're at lethal; prioritize casting win conditions. Manage hand size carefully—discard is a resource, but don't blow it all on non-essential threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: TES rarely interacts with the board early. Commit to winning as fast as possible. If you draw a board wipe (Pyroclasm) or hate artifact (Tormod's), hold it until you can safely cast it or win. Don't overextend into sweepers unless you're at lethal or facing a stable board that threatens to stall your combo.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up mana for Brainstorm/Preordain or discard), graveyard hate (Tormod's, Rest in Peace), and board wipes. Note if opponents are playing discard-heavy decks (hold up Inquisition/Thoughtseize for their key pieces). Track how many cards they've drawn to gauge if they're comboing or controlling.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Tendril of Agony (primary), Fire/Ice (secondary/versatile), or Burning Wish targets like Pyroclasm, Tormod's Crypt, or a second Tendrils. Win by assembling 7+ mana and casting Tendrils, or using Fire/Ice to deal 5 damage and draw 2.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast your win condition and draw enough cards to ensure it resolves or deals lethal. If you're at 5+ mana and have a Tendrils in hand, cast it. If you're racing, commit to your win condition even if it means leaving yourself vulnerable.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1 card, have no fast mana, no win conditions, and the opponent has a stable board or graveyard hate. Also concede if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no way to break through and you've exhausted your draw spells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrip density to out-draw and find answers. Hold up mana for Brainstorm/Preordain to find around counters. Use Thoughtseize to discard their counterspells. If they have heavy counter suites, switch to Fire/Ice or Wish for Tormod's/Pyroclasm.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Outpace them with faster mana and win conditions. Use Thoughtseize to discard their ramp pieces. Hold Tormod's Crypt for their mana rocks. Win before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early discard to disrupt their curve. If they survive, stabilize with Pyroclasm or Tormod's. Your win conditions are fast enough to race if you assemble mana quickly.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cantrips and discard to thin your deck and find answers. Out-value them with card draw. If they lock down the board, Wish for Tormod's Crypt or Pyroclasm. Don't overcommit to draws if you're at lethal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Out-draw and out-wish. Prioritize graveyard protection and hate. Win condition selection is key—Tendril wins if they're low, Fire/Ice if they're drawing, Pyroclasm if they're aggressive. Manage your hand carefully and don't overdraw into your own hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your game plan to the metagame. Against control and combo, prioritize graveyard hate and board wipes. Against aggro and midrange, lean into discard and early disruption. Adjust your main deck by removing redundant fast mana or cantrips if you're bringing in multiple sideboard cards. Always keep at least one Burning Wish target flexible enough to handle multiple threats.
notes: TES is highly skill-intensive; practice sequencing mana and cantrips to avoid dead draws. Remember that Mox Opal requires a permanent to enter untapped, so plan your land drops accordingly. Don't forget that Fire/Ice can be used as a 5-damage spell even if you don't need to draw cards. In mirrors, graveyard hate often decides the game—track both players' graveyards carefully. Common mistake: over-drawing past lethal or failing to sequence cantrips properly, leading to dead mana.

philosophy: Painter is a fast combo deck that assembles an infinite mana and combat engine using Painter's Servant, a 0-cost creature/artifact, and Goblin Welder. It aims to win by overwhelming opponents with unblockable damage or infinite resources before tempo and control decks can stabilize or answer the threat.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand containing Painter's Servant plus at least two of the other combo pieces (Goblin Welder or a 0-cost creature like Tangle Wire/Myr Retriever/Arcbound Ravager). Keep hands with 2-3 lands that can produce red mana or fetch, plus at least one protection spell (Pyroblast/Flusterstorm) if missing a piece. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple non-fetching lands, or hands with only one combo piece and no protection/mana fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast a cantrip or protection spell if holding combo pieces. Turn 2: Cast Goblin Welder or the 0-cost creature, then use Painter's Servant to paint it. If all three pieces are in play, cast Painter's Servant, paint the 0-cost creature, then use Goblin Welder to bounce it to hand and cast it again for free, repeating to generate infinite mana/combat. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast the non-0-cost pieces on time.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Dimir Tempo/Tron: Bring in additional protection (Flusterstorm, Red Elemental Blast) and artifact hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void if format-legal, or Ensnaring Bridge). Against Sneak and Show/Izzet Delver: Bring in faster interaction (Lightning Bolt, Pyroblast) and artifact destruction. Against Ocelot Pride Midrange: Bring in more targeted removal, protection, and graveyard hate if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is largely irrelevant once the combo is online, but before that, conserve lands for casting the combo pieces. Use cantrips to dig for pieces and avoid clogging your hand. Do not overextend with non-combo threats; the engine is your primary board presence.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on attacking with the combo until you have infinite power or a clear win condition. If you have infinite mana, use it to cast win-cons or pump creatures. Protect the combo pieces at all costs against sweepers and removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Flusterstorm, Mana Leak, Force of Will), artifact hate (Ensnaring Bridge, Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment). If they have a sweeper, try to win before it resolves or protect the combo. Track their hand for disruption and adjust sequencing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Infinite combat damage via Tangle Wire/Myr Retriever/Arcbound Ravager, or infinite mana to cast a win-con like Tangle Wire or a pump spell. Sometimes simply attacking with infinite power is sufficient.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have the full combo online and no immediate threat can stop it. If you have infinite mana, use it to win immediately. If facing a slow deck, commit to the combo and ignore minor threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard lock (Ensnaring Bridge, Rest in Peace with no way to remove it) and have no way to break it. Also concede if you are down to a dead hand with no combo pieces and the opponent is ahead on board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use protection spells (Pyroblast, Flusterstorm) to counter their counters. Sequence the combo so the final piece is cast in response to their counter, or use cantrips to dig around it. Sometimes you can win before they can respond.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply pressure quickly. Do not let them get to their big threats. Use Pyroblast to counter their ramp spells or finish them off with Lightning Bolt.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Pyroblast/Flusterstorm to counter key threats. If you cannot stabilize, try to combo off quickly. Sometimes you can win before they kill you.
threat_responses.against_control: Protect the combo at all costs. Use protection spells to counter their removal. If they have a sweeper, try to win before it resolves or use protection to save the combo.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It is a race. Try to combo off faster. Use protection to counter their combo pieces. If they go first, try to disrupt their early plays. If you go first, try to combo off before they can respond.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize hate cards against the metagame. Against control, bring in more protection and artifact hate. Against aggro/combo, bring in faster interaction and artifact hate. Against midrange, bring in more removal and protection. Always keep at least one copy of Pyroblast for key threats.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board before the combo is online, failing to protect the combo pieces, not sequencing the combo correctly (e.g., painting the wrong target), and ignoring hate cards. Unique interaction: Goblin Welder can bounce the painted creature to hand and cast it again for free, triggering Painter's Servant's ability again to create infinite mana/combat. Always check for artifact hate before committing.

philosophy: A control-midrange archetype that leverages tax effects, efficient removal, and card advantage to out-value opponents. It wins by stabilizing early with Yorion, applying incremental pressure with resilient threats, and closing games with equipment or Yorion's body.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep 4+ land hands with a curve of 1-2 mana spells (Solitude, Noble Hierarch, Birds of Paradise, Thoughtseize). Keep 5+ land hands with a turn-1 play (Thalia, Tarmogoyf, Scavenging Ooze) or a turn-2 play (Yorion, Stoneforge Mystic). Mulligan pure land, all removal, or hands missing basics if the format demands them. Prioritize hands that can deploy a tax effect or card draw by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Noble Hierarch or Birds of Paradise for mana acceleration, or Thoughtseize/Duress to disrupt. If opening Stoneforge Mystic, play land, cast SFM, fetch land, search for Batterskull or Sword of Feast and Famine. Turn 2: Deploy Thalia to tax opponent's non-creature spells, or cast Yorion to stabilize. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for Solitude/Thoughtseize by turn 2.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo (Sneak and Show, Izzet Delver): In Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction, Ghost Quarter, Dismember. Out Birds, Noble Hierarch, and redundant removal. vs Control/Ramp (Dimir Tempo, Tron, Ocelot Pride): In Field of the Dead, additional Thalia, threat protection, and graveyard hate. Out land destruction and extra hate. vs Mirror: In additional tax effects, graveyard hate, and extra removal. Out land ramp.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Solitude for key threats (counters, finishers, combo pieces). Use Thoughtseize/Duress to disrupt opponent's hand before committing threats. Manage the graveyard with Rest in Peace/Surgical Extraction to prevent recursion. Avoid overcommitting to the board if the opponent has sweepers or counterspells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if they have potential sweepers or counters. Use Yorion to trade efficiently or stabilize against aggressive boards. Tax effects should be prioritized on the board to disrupt the opponent's game plan and force inefficient trades.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), counters (Force of Will, Counterspell), combo pieces (Inverter of Truth, Storm targets), and graveyard recursion. Note when the opponent taps out for a big spell or when they start casting non-basic lands for ramp. Track their hand size after Thoughtseize/Duress to predict their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Yorion, Sky Nomad (body + flying + lifelink), Batterskull (on a resilient threat or Yorion), Sword of Feast and Famine (for card advantage and removal). Field of the Dead can close games if the graveyard is clear and you have enough 1/1 tokens.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can trade Yorion into a key threat. Commit when you can protect your finisher with removal or tax effects, and when you have card advantage to sustain the push.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to a single vulnerable threat against a deck with multiple sweepers or combo pieces, or if your hand is dead against their strategy (e.g., no removal vs Tron, no threats vs Sneak and Show). Also concede if graveyard hate is fully countered and you have no way to clear it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Solitude to remove counterspellers. Cast threats in response to counterspells if possible. Use Thoughtseize to remove key counters. Do not overcommit to a single threat; apply incremental pressure and force them to use multiple answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use Solitude on key ramp creatures. Deploy Thalia to tax their non-creature spells. Graveyard hate to stop recursion. Force them to spend resources on interaction rather than developing their board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Yorion and efficient removal (Solitude, Fatal Push, Go for the Throat). Use Ghost Quarter to disrupt their mana. Do not overextend; trade efficiently and use tax effects to slow their curve. Win the resource war by out-grinding them.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply incremental pressure with resilient threats. Use Thoughtseize to remove key answers. Force them to use resources on multiple threats. Close games with equipment or Yorion's body. Do not let them stabilize with sweepers by applying pressure before they can cast them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Thoughtseize/Duress to disrupt their hand. Use Solitude to remove key threats. Deploy Thalia to tax their non-creature spells. Win the graveyard war with Rest in Peace/Surgical Extraction. Control the board with tax effects and efficient trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the metagame. Against control, prioritize card advantage and threat protection. Against aggro/combo, prioritize hate cards and early interaction. Always keep a balance of removal, tax effects, and finishers. Adjust land counts if needed (e.g., more basics vs control, more fetches vs aggro). Keep your sideboard plan flexible to adapt to mid-game developments.
notes: Yorion is both a win condition and a stabilizer; use him flexibly. Solitude is your most powerful tool—save it for game-ending threats or key combo pieces. Stoneforge Mystic finds Batterskull for a massive body or Sword of Feast and Famine for card advantage and removal. Watch for graveyard recursion; Rest in Peace is often a game-winning sideboard card. Avoid overcommitting to the board without removal; D&T wins by out-resourcing, not trading blindly.

philosophy: A resilient control-midrange hybrid that leverages cheap, efficient threats protected by Cradle of Safety and Green Sun's Zenith to out-value opponents. It aims to disrupt early, stabilize with protection and removal, and close out games with unblockable or tax-inducing creatures.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Ignoble Hierarch, Cradle of Safety, Green Sun's Zenith, or early interaction (Fatal Push, Lightning Helix). Prioritize hands that can play a turn-1 Hierarch or turn-2 GSY/creature. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only three-drops, or hands lacking early interaction/creatures.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Ignoble Hierarch. If you have a fetch and a basic, fetch into a black source for Thoughtseize/Fatal Push. Turn 2: Cast Green Sun's Zenith to find Badgermole Cub or Thalia, or play a land + spell. Sequence lands to ensure double black for hand disruption or green for GSY, while maintaining flexibility for fetches.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Delver, Ocelot Pride): +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Go for the Throat, +2 Lightning Helix, -2 Green Sun's Zenith, -1 Thalia. Against control (Dimir Tempo, Tron): +2 Thoughtseize, +2 Duress, +2 Rest in Peace, -2 Cradle of Safety, -1 Badgermole Cub. Against combo (Sneak and Show): +3 Rest in Peace, +2 Leyline of the Void, -2 Green Sun's Zenith, -1 Ignoble Hierarch. Mirror: +2 Thoughtseize, +2 Go for the Throat, -2 Cradle of Safety.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use fetch lands to cycle and find duals, keeping hand size up with Thoughtseize/Duress. Manage your graveyard proactively; if the opponent is running graveyard strategies, deploy Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void early. Avoid overcommitting mana to Cradle without a clear threat to protect.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats like Badgermole Cub or Thalia when the opponent has no spot removal or sweepers. Hold back if they have unanswered interaction or a potential board wipe. Use Cradle of Safety defensively to survive combat or targeted removal, but don't waste it on creatures that can be traded favorably.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), spot removal (Lightning Helix, Go for the Throat), and graveyard strategies. Note when opponents tap out for counters or removal, as this is your window to cast Green Sun's Zenith or commit multiple threats. Track their graveyard size to anticipate Rest in Peace needs.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Badgermole Cub (unblockable), Thalia, Guardian of Thraben (taxes), and Knight of the Reliquary (grind). Secure victory by establishing a protected board, then attacking with unblockable or tax-inducing creatures while using removal to clear blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, their graveyard is empty, or they've tapped out for a non-essential spell. Also commit when you have multiple threats on board and they lack sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has a locked board with no answers, you have a dead hand with no interaction, or you're facing a combo that has already resolved its win condition. Also concede if you're down to one life and facing a sweepers + removal lock.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast Green Sun's Zenith when the opponent is tapped out or has no counterspells in hand. Use cheap creatures like Ignoble Hierarch that are hard to counter, and sequence spells to force counters on less critical plays. If they counter GSY, follow up with a creature and Cradle to stabilize.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use Thoughtseize and Duress to hit mana dorks and key ramp spells. Deploy removal on early threats, and use Cradle of Safety to protect your own board from being overrun. If they run graveyard ramp, bring in Rest in Peace.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early interaction (Fatal Push, Lightning Helix), then deploy Cradle of Safety to protect your board from combat damage. Use Thalia to tax their attacks, and Rest in Peace to stop graveyard-based aggro. Trade efficiently and close out games with unblockable threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap threats and hand disruption to disrupt their hand. Use Green Sun's Zenith to find value creatures, and Cradle to protect your board from spot removal. Win the attrition war by out-grinding them with Knight of the Reliquary and graveyard recursion if applicable.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Out-value with better sequencing and hand disruption. Use Thoughtseize to disrupt their Green Sun's Zenith or early threats, and Cradle to protect your board. Win the resource war by trading efficiently and leveraging graveyard hate if they run it.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your game plan based on the metagame. Against control and combo, prioritize graveyard hate and hand disruption to disrupt their strategy. Against aggro, focus on spot removal and tax effects to slow them down. Keep Cradle of Safety and Green Sun's Zenith in most games, but be willing to cut them against decks that can easily answer them. Manage your sideboard slots carefully to maintain a balanced curve and interaction count.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Cradle of Safety without a threat, mismanaging fetch lands, and failing to sideboard in Rest in Peace/Leyline against combo. Unique interactions: Ignoble Hierarch can be sacrificed to pay for Cradle of Safety or other costs, and Green Sun's Zenith is a sorcery so must be cast on your turn when the opponent lacks counters. Always track the opponent's graveyard and removal count to make informed decisions.

philosophy: Omni-Tell is a five-color combo-control deck that aims to assemble free-casting engines like Omniscience or Flow State, then win via Show and Tell or massive free spells. It wins by out-resourcing and out-swinging control, midrange, and combo decks through explosive turns and disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (preferably fetchable/shockable), 1+ acceleration (Lotus Field, Chrome Mox, Mox Opal, Arcane Signet), and 1+ combo piece (Omniscience, Flow State, Show and Tell) or draw/spell (Brainstorm, Ponder, Preordain, Thoughtseize). Keep hands with 3+ lands if no acceleration/combo. Mulligan pure lands, pure removal, or hands with only one land and no acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox/Chrome Mox, or cast Thoughtseize/Lightning Bolt. If Lotus Field is in hand, play it untapped if possible (via fetch/shock) or hold for acceleration. Turn 2: Cast draw spells (Brainstorm/Ponder), cast Omniscience/Flow State if mana allows, or play Show and Tell. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for Omniscience (WUBRG) or Flow State (U).
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver, Ocelot): +1 Spell Pierce, +1 Lightning Bolt, +1 Dismember/Go Blank, -1 Omniscience/Flow State, -1 extra draw. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir): +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Dismember, +1 Negate/Flusterstorm, -1 extra lands, -1 Show and Tell. vs Combo (Sneak & Show): +1 Dismember, +1 Negate, +1 Rest in Peace/Scavenging Ooze, -1 extra draw, -1 Flow State. vs Tron/Ramp: +1 Dismember, +1 Negate, +1 Rest in Peace, -1 extra lands, -1 Show and Tell.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping hand size high; Omni-Tell thrives on card advantage. Use fetch lands to find shock lands for colored mana when casting Omniscience. Don't overcommit to one win condition; keep options open. Manage graveyard carefully if facing Rest in Peace or Graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting Show and Tell if the opponent has sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods) or removal. Use disruption (Thoughtseize, Spell Pierce) to protect your combo pieces. Only commit threats when you can follow up or have protection.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Spell Pierce, Flusterstorm, Negate), sweepers, and graveyard hate. Note if the opponent is playing fast lands or ramp spells. Track their hand size; if they have no interaction, go all-in.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Omniscience + Show and Tell (Thassa's Oracle/Green Sun's Zenith), Omniscience + Emrakul, the Aeons Torn, or Flow State + Brainstorm/Ponder into a win.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have Omniscience/Flow State in play, enough mana to cast it, and a win condition in hand or library. Also commit when the opponent has no interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're on the draw against a fast combo with no disruption, or if you're locked out by Rest in Peace/Scavenging Ooze with no way to interact. Also if you're facing a Tron/Control deck with a locked board and you have no draw spells or acceleration.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Spell Pierce to protect your own spells. Cast Omniscience/Flow State in response to counterspells if you have priority. Use cheap draw spells to dig around. Don't overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early disruption (Thoughtseize, Lightning Bolt) to hit their mana rocks or key spells. Use Dismember/Negate to remove threats. Play around their win conditions by holding up interaction.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Lightning Bolt, Spell Pierce, and Dismember. Use Thoughtseize to remove key threats. Once stabilized, pivot to your combo. Don't overcommit to the combo if you're at low life.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with early disruption and cheap threats. Use Omniscience to cast multiple spells in a turn, overwhelming their counterspells. Dig for your win condition with Ponder/Brainstorm.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race to assemble your combo first. Use Thoughtseize to steal their Omniscience/Flow State/Show and Tell. Prioritize keeping hands with acceleration and draw. Win by going first and assembling faster, or by out-resolving their counterspells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your mana base if needed (though Omni-Tell typically runs a stable 5-color base). Focus on bringing in interaction against control/combo, and removal against aggro. Trim redundant draw spells or lands based on the matchup. Always keep at least one copy of your primary win condition.
notes: Omni-Tell is highly sensitive to graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze) and counterspells. Practice sequencing fetch lands and shock lands to cast Omniscience on turn 2 or 3. Don't forget that Omniscience lets you cast spells from hand for free, so you can cast Show and Tell, then cast a creature from your hand (like Thassa's Oracle) using Omniscience's effect. Flow State works similarly but only for instants and sorceries. Be mindful of the stack when casting multiple spells; use priority wisely.

philosophy: Jeskai Control stabilizes the early game through efficient interaction and card filtering, then out-values opponents in the late game using Jace, the Mind Sculptor and Flow State to close out games against combo, midrange, and control archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing basics for shock lands), Ponder, Jace, or early interaction (Lightning Helix, Path to Exile, Spell Snare). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no spells, 0-1 lands with spells, or hands lacking early answers to aggro/combo threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Ponder if possible to dig for lands or interaction, then play a land. Turn 2: Play a land, then cast Jace, Flow State, or interaction depending on board state. Sequence lands to maximize shock effects and Flow State triggers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo (Sneak & Show, Delver): +4 Spell Queller, +2 Surgical Extraction, +2 Rest in Peace, -2 Flow State, -2 Anger of the Gods. vs Control/Ramp (Dimir Tempo, Tron): +2 Anger of the Gods, +2 Surgical Extraction, +2 Jace, the Mind Sculptor, -2 Flow State, -2 Lightning Helix.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Ponder to filter for answers or threats; never cast it when you can't afford to mulligan or when mana is critical. Manage shock lands to preserve life total and enable Flow State. Track graveyard to prevent recursion.
