midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing Jace or Flow State if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Commit threats when you have card advantage, opponent is tapped out, or you can protect your board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron’s land count, Sneak & Show’s graveyard, Delver’s flip progress, and Dimir’s mana holding. Key tells include opponent tapping for multiple colors, holding up mana on your turn, or graveyard interactions.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Jace’s +1 loyalty to draw into game-winning cards, Flow State’s card draw and combat damage, or Anger of the Gods for board wipes. Secure victory by out-resourcing or closing with a protected threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, opponent has no sweepers, or you can protect your finisher. Cast Flow State when you can attack safely or when you need to close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re at 1 life with no answers, opponent has a guaranteed win condition (e.g., Sneak & Show with multiple creatures, Tron with 5 lands and a finisher), or you’re dead to the board (e.g., Anger of the Gods in a control mirror).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Dig through counters with Ponder, cast spells in response to counterspells, or sideboard in uncounterable threats. Use Flow State to out-value and pressure.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with interaction, use Surgical Extraction/Rest in Peace to disrupt, and leverage Jace to dig through their threats. Flow State can out-draw their ramp.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal (Lightning Helix, Path to Exile), deploy Spell Queller for protection, and use Surgical Extraction to remove key threats. Transition to Jace/Flow State to close out.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with Jace and Flow State, sideboard in Anger of the Gods or additional removal, and pressure with late-game threats. Force them to spend resources on your threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through card advantage and sideboard positioning. Prioritize Ponder, Jace, and Flow State. Bring in Anger of the Gods, Surgical Extraction, and Rest in Peace to disrupt their game plan and protect yours.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to the specific matchup by prioritizing hate cards for combo/ramp, early interaction for aggro, and card advantage for control/mirror. Avoid over-indexing on one strategy; keep a balanced mix of removal, disruption, and finishers to handle shifting metagame demands.
notes: Ponder is the deck’s backbone—cast it aggressively to find answers or threats, but avoid it when you’re at 1 life or need to protect a board. Flow State is a double-edged sword; only cast it when you can protect it or when you’re ready to attack. Jace is your primary engine; protect him with removal or by playing around sweepers. Never overextend into known sweepers, and always track the opponent’s graveyard and mana.

philosophy: Aluren is an explosive combo deck designed to win on turn two or three by casting massive, game-ending creatures for free using Aluren and Show and Tell. It aims to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize, leveraging fast mana and card advantage to outpace tempo, control, and midrange strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with two or more sources of fast mana (Lotus Petal, Mox, Dark Ritual, etc.) paired with Aluren, Show and Tell, or a draw spell. Keep 4+ lands if you have zero fast mana. Mulligan hands with only lands, only non-mana non-combo cards, or a broken mana base that can't cast your key spells by turn two.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast fast mana, and either cast Aluren or draw spells to dig for your combo. Turn 2: Cast Show and Tell, fetch a massive creature like Griselbrand or Emrakul, and cast it for free via Aluren. If Aluren isn't in play, use fast mana to cast Show and Tell and target a creature in your graveyard. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetches and shocks to generate fast mana, then play basics to ensure you can cast Aluren and Show and Tell without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control and midrange: Bring in Leyline of the Void, Engineered Plague, and Surgical Extraction to disrupt their resources and graveyard. Against combo and mirror: Bring in Rest in Peace, Disrupting Shoal, and additional discard to protect your game plan. Against ramp and artifact-heavy decks: Bring in Ancient Grudge, Disenchant, and artifact hate to slow their acceleration.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard fast mana until you have the full combo package or a clear win condition. Use card draw (Necropotence, Glimpse the Unthinkable, Brainstorm) to dig for pieces while managing your life total and graveyard. If facing graveyard hate, avoid unnecessary graveyard interactions and pivot to direct combo wins or Leyline of the Void.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can win the game or have a backup plan to protect them. Don't overextend into board wipes or sweepers unless you're confident you can win in response. Use counterspells and discard to protect your combo pieces and disrupt opponent's key spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for discard spells to see what they're targeting, and note when opponents tap out for counterspells versus when they're holding up. Pay attention to graveyard interactions—if they're casting Leyline or Rest in Peace, your combo game plan is severely compromised. Track their mana development to anticipate when they can answer your threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Griselbrand (life gain and massive card draw), Emrakul, the Aeons Torn (draw seven and exile permanents), Karn, the Great Creator (Engineered Plague or Leyline of the Void), and Bogardan Hellkite (board sweep and direct damage). Get there by resolving Show and Tell or casting Aluren creatures, then following up with the finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have the full combo package (fast mana + Aluren/Show and Tell + target) or when you can win in a single turn. Commit to the board if you have a clear path to victory and your opponent has no immediate answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you're dead to hate (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Engineered Plague) and have no comeback, when you're facing a locked board with no answers, or when your opponent has stabilized and you're out of resources. Don't drag out games against decks that can out-value you.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells before they can be cast. Cast your combo pieces in response to counterspells, or rely on Aluren's free casting to bypass targeting if they only counter spells. Have multiple targets for Show and Tell to make it difficult to counter everything.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use discard to hit their mana rocks and slow their acceleration. Bring in artifact hate to remove their fast mana. Play around their big spells by holding up counterspells or discard, and aim to win before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use discard to remove early threats and hold up counterspells for key spells. Stabilize with life gain from Griselbrand or sweepers if you're forced to interact. Prioritize combo if possible, but don't be afraid to play defensively to survive the early game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats to force them to use resources. Use discard to remove answers and combo off before they can stabilize. If they rely on graveyard strategies, bring in graveyard hate to disrupt their game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by using discard to hit their combo pieces and card draw to out-draw them. Prioritize graveyard hate if they rely on it, and aim to resolve your combo before they can. Control the board with counterspells and discard to protect your game plan.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your hate cards based on the metagame. Bring in Leyline of the Void, Engineered Plague, and Surgical Extraction against control and midrange. Bring in Rest in Peace, Disrupting Shoal, and artifact hate against combo and ramp. Keep your fast mana and combo pieces consistent, and balance your sideboard to address the most common threats while maintaining your win condition.
notes: Common mistakes include mulliganing too aggressively, overcommitting to the board without a win condition, and ignoring graveyard hate. Aluren bypasses casting costs but not targeting or counterspells, so plan accordingly. Karn can find Engineered Plague or Leyline of the Void, providing a secondary win condition. Always have a backup plan if your primary combo is disrupted.

philosophy: Out-value and out-tempo opponents by leveraging superior card draw, efficient interaction, and Flow State to disrupt game plans or close games with a flurry of free spells. Excels against combo and midrange by disrupting their early game and outlasting control through resource advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 playable lands (preferably duals or fetchable), at least one cantrip (Ponder/Brainstorm), and early interaction (Spell Pierce/Thoughtseize). Keep Flow State only if paired with a land drop and a cantrip to set up. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no plays, 1 land with no cantrips/interaction, or hands lacking any way to interact with the opponent's early game.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Ponder or Brainstorm to dig for answers or Flow State setup. If holding interaction, hold up Spell Pierce or Thoughtseize. Turn 2: Resolve cantrip, play second land, and either apply early pressure with a cheap threat, hold up interaction for opponent's play, or begin casting Flow State if mana allows. Prioritize maintaining card advantage over board presence.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Delver/Sneak & Show): Bring in discard, early interaction, and sweepers. vs Control/Tron: Bring in additional counterspells, graveyard hate, and secondary win conditions. vs Combo: Bring in specific hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, or targeted discard) and counters. vs Mirror: Bring in more counters, discard, and sideboard cards that punish resource trading.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until the opponent commits. Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig for answers or Flow State rather than overcommitting to the board. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary scry/dig if opponent has graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Trade efficiently and avoid overextending. Hold back threats if opponent has sweepers or uncounterable answers. Use interaction to protect your Flow State or key threats, and only commit to board when you have card advantage or opponent is out of interaction.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron's land drops (signaling a massive turn), Sneak & Show's creature package, Delver's flip condition, and Ocelot Pride's midrange threats. Note when opponents hold up mana for counterspells or sweepers, and adjust your sequencing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Flow State (casting multiple free spells to clear the board, disrupt, or win), resolved threats, or card advantage advantage. Set up Flow State by ensuring you have multiple cheap spells in hand and enough mana to cast it.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, opponent is out of interaction, or you can safely cast Flow State to swing the board. Also commit when you can close the game before opponent can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., Tron with multiple artifacts, Sneak & Show with multiple creatures), if you're out of resources and they have sweepers, or if your hand is dead against their strategy.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig around counterspells. Cast Flow State to cast multiple spells, forcing opponent to counter the wrong ones. Use discard to remove counterspells from hand.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and discard to disrupt mana development. Use Flow State to cast cheap removal or counters. Stabilize with sweepers if ramp establishes a board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and discard to slow down the board. Use sweepers and efficient removal to stabilize. Save Flow State for late-game cleanup or to win if they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw and apply pressure with threats. Use Flow State to swing or close games. Force them to use interaction on low-impact spells to exhaust their resources.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use discard to remove key cards, out-draw with Ponder/Brainstorm, and use counters to protect your Flow State or threats. Prioritize card advantage and efficient trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure tempo to a more control/hate-focused game plan. Adjust land count if facing heavy mana bases, bring in format-specific hate for combo and midrange, and increase counterspells/discard against control and aggro. Prioritize flexibility and resource denial over raw tempo.
notes: Flow State is a sorcery, so timing is critical—cast it when you can safely resolve it or when you have enough cheap spells to make it impactful. Ponder and Brainstorm are essential for consistency; never skip them unless you're on the draw and need to stabilize. Avoid overcommitting to the board, as Azorius Tempo wins through resource advantage and interaction, not board presence. Watch for format-specific hate cards and adjust sequencing accordingly.

philosophy: Fast artifact acceleration deck leveraging cheap mana rocks and low-cost threats to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. Excels against slower, resource-dependent archetypes but struggles against fast aggro or hard interaction.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ mana sources (Mox Opal, Lotus Petal, Urza's Bauble, or basic lands) and a turn-1 or turn-2 threat/playable artifact. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no artifacts, or only expensive threats. Prioritize hands that can cast turn-1 spells or accelerate into turn-2 threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Opal/Lotus Petal/Urza's Bauble. If holding multiple artifacts, sacrifice Urza's Bauble for mana to cast a cheap threat or spell. Turn 2: Deploy Arcbound Ravager, Myr Enforcer, or Manamorphose. Sequence artifacts to maximize Opal triggers and generate mana for threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Delver/Ocelot Pride: Bring in artifact hate (Null Rod, Surgical Extraction) and fast interaction (Flusterstorm, Pyroclasm). vs Dimir Tempo/Tron: Bring in more threats (Manamorphose, Myr Enforcer), artifact acceleration (Chrome Mox), and hate (Null Rod, Rest in Peace). vs Sneak and Show: Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), artifact hate, and fast interaction. vs Mirror: Bring in more threats and interaction, keep acceleration.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Don't overextend into sweepers or removal. Use artifacts to generate mana efficiently but keep a reserve for interaction. Manage graveyard carefully if facing graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back if opponent has sweepers or hard removal. Use artifacts to trade into removal or stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Flusterstorm, Ponder/Preordain sequencing), sweepers (Pyroclasm, Wrath), and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Arcbound Ravager with +1/+1 counters, Manamorphose, Myr Enforcer, or artifact combos. Win through combat or artifact synergy.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have multiple artifacts on board, opponent has no sweepers, and you can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to one life, opponent has a board wipe ready, you have no artifacts, and your hand is dead. Also against fast aggro if you can't stabilize.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multiple threats (cantrips, artifact tokens, or split the cast). Don't rely on single big spells. Use artifacts to generate mana for protection or follow-up.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply pressure quickly. Don't let them stabilize. Use artifact hate or fast interaction to disrupt their mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use artifacts to trade, hold up interaction for key threats, and stabilize with removal or sweepers if available.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap threats, use cantrips to dig for answers, and exploit their lack of interaction early.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the artifact race, track their interaction, and out-value them with better sequencing or sideboard cards.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Bring in hate for dominant archetypes, keep acceleration, and adjust threat counts. Generally, bring in more interaction vs control, more pressure vs combo, and artifact hate vs artifact-heavy decks.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without protection. Unique interaction: Mox Opal triggers can be used to pay for costs or generate mana for threats. Watch out for Null Rod and Rest in Peace.

philosophy: A fast, artifact-cheat strategy that wins by assembling lethal combos (Goblin Welder + Arcbound Ravager for board control, or Welder + Smelt-Hound/Thopter Foundry for infinite combat/mana) and cheating threats into play with Aether Vial. Excels against slower, combo-heavy, or midrange decks that lack early interaction or artifact hate.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ mana sources (lands, Mox Opal, Lotus Petal, Chrome Mox) and at least one of Goblin Welder, Aether Vial, or Arcbound Ravager. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no artifacts, or only one mana source without a turn-1 play. Prioritize hands that can cast a spell or activate a mana rock on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lotus Petal or Mox Opal, or cast Goblin Welder/Shardless Agent. Turn 2: Cast Aether Vial, Smelt-Hound, or another artifact. Sequence Opal/Petal to generate extra mana for Vial activation or Welder casting. Prioritize establishing artifact acceleration and Vial availability to cheat threats into play before the opponent can interact.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver, Ocelot): +4 Lightning Helix, +2 Sudden Spoiling, +1 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Aether Vial, -1 Shardless Agent. vs Control/Tron: +3 Spell Pierce, +2 Sudden Spoiling, +2 additional counters/removal, -2 Lotus Petal, -1 Smelt-Hound. vs Sneak & Show/Combo: +2 targeted removal/counters, +1 artifact/graveyard hate, -2 Opal, -1 Petal. vs Mirror: +2 Sudden Spoiling, +2 Lightning Helix, +1 Smelt-Hound, -2 Vial, -1 Agent.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Opal and Petal to accelerate into Vial or Welder. Don't overextend Vial if you lack follow-up artifacts. Track graveyard contents carefully; Welder, Ravager, and Smelt-Hound are vulnerable to graveyard hate but enable infinite combos if assembled. Preserve interaction for key threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Goblin Welder when you can immediately follow up with Arcbound Ravager, Smelt-Hound, or have Aether Vial ready to cheat in a threat. Hold back if the opponent has untapped removal or sweepers. Use Shardless Agent to dig for key pieces or remove threats before they resolve.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Bolt), and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or leave mana open for interaction. Track graveyard recursion and artifact hate to avoid dead commits.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Goblin Welder + Arcbound Ravager (5/5 trample with untap on death), or Welder + Smelt-Hound/Thopter Foundry (infinite combat damage or mana). Assemble by cheating Welder/Vial into play, then sequencing artifacts to trigger abilities and close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when Aether Vial is active, the opponent has no removal/sweepers, or you can safely assemble the combo. Commit when you can swing for lethal or lock the opponent out of the game with infinite combat/mana.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has a hard board wipe, you have no artifacts or mana acceleration, and they are ahead on board. Concede if you are out of resources, facing a locked-out control deck, or have no comeback against a stabilized aggro/combo opponent.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Opal/Petal to cast spells around counters, cheat threats with Aether Vial, and use Spell Pierce/Sudden Spoiling to protect your own spells. Sequence Welder first, then Vial on the following turn to bypass counter windows.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use removal on mana dorks or key lands. Don't let them stabilize; use Vial to cheat out threats before they can ramp out. Disrupt their mana base with targeted removal or counters.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use targeted removal (Helix, Spoiling) and Aether Vial to cheat in blockers. Smelt-Hound can swing for damage. Hold up counters if needed to protect your combo pieces. Stabilize quickly and transition to combo finishers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early before they can stabilize. Use Aether Vial to cheat out threats when they tap out for counterspells. Assemble the Welder combo to bypass control and win quickly. Use counters to protect your own spells when necessary.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with Aether Vial timing, graveyard management, and more interaction. Prioritize casting Welder first, then Vial, and use removal to break their combos. Value engine and artifact synergy will decide the match.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure acceleration/cheat to increased interaction. Against control and combo, prioritize counters and removal to protect your game plan and disrupt theirs. Against aggro, bring in direct damage and blockers to stabilize. Adjust your artifact ratio based on matchup, and always track opponent interaction to time your Vial and Welder plays optimally.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting Goblin Welder without follow-up, forgetting to use Opal/Petal for extra mana, and mis-timing Aether Vial. Unique interactions: Welder + Ravager triggers both ETB and death abilities; Smelt-Hound + Welder creates infinite combat/mana; Aether Vial can target artifacts in the graveyard. Always track opponent removal and sweepers to avoid dead commits. Practice sequencing Opal/Petal to ensure you can cast Vial or Welder on curve even with a poor land draw.

philosophy: Apply relentless early pressure with cheap threats and acceleration while using discard and Chalice of the Void to disrupt the opponent's game plan, aiming to close out games before control or combo strategies can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, Chrome Mox, and at least one of Chalice of the Void, a turn-1/2 threat, or discard. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no Mox or threats, or if you lack any way to play a spell on turn 1 or 2. Prioritize hands that can chain Mox into a threat or Chalice into a disruption piece.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play Chrome Mox, sacrifice it to cast Chalice of the Void (usually on 1 or 2), a turn-1 threat (e.g., Monastery Swiftspear, Goblin Guide), or a discard spell. Turn 2: Play a land, then cast your second threat or discard to remove a key opposing piece. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast your Mox/Chalice/threats on curve without flooding; if you have multiple Moxes, hold one for a potential turn-2 play.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs. Dimir Tempo/Izzet Delver: Bring in additional discard and Chalice on 1/2; consider more direct damage. Vs. Tron/Sneak and Show: Bring in Chalice on 1, discard, and hate cards to disrupt recursion or combo. Vs. Aggro/Midrange: Bring in more threats, lifelink creatures, or spot removal to stabilize early. Vs. Mirror: Bring in discard and Chalice on 1 to disrupt their early game.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Chrome Mox as a one-time acceleration tool—save it for when you need to cast a high-impact spell early. Manage Chalice of the Void by targeting the opponent's most disruptive mana value (usually 1 or 2). Use discard to remove counters, removal, or combo pieces rather than wasting it on irrelevant cards. Keep your graveyard in mind if the opponent plays graveyard-based strategies, but prioritize board presence over graveyard recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats aggressively on turns 1-3 to force interaction. Hold back a threat or two if the opponent has shown sweepers or hard removal. Don't overextend into known board wipes; instead, use discard to remove the wipe from their hand or force them to use it prematurely.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, hard removal, and sweepers. Note when the opponent taps out for a key spell like Thoughtseize or Chalice. Track their graveyard if they play Tron or Sneak and Show, as recursion or combo pieces can swing the game quickly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Win via combat damage with multiple threats, Broadside Bombardiers' triggered ability (which scales with damage dealt), or direct damage spells. Use discard to clear the opponent's hand of blockers or removal, then commit all remaining threats to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, counters, or sweepers in hand or on the board, or when you can safely attack with multiple threats without risking a fatal counterattack. Also commit when you can activate Broadside Bombardiers' ability to deal lethal damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you have no threats, discard, or acceleration left, and the opponent has a clear path to victory. Also concede if you're facing a dead hand against a deck that can stabilize and outvalue you in the late game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Overcommit with threats to force multiple counters, use discard to remove counterspells from hand, and play Chalice on 1 to shut down their disruption. If they counter your Mox, follow up with a land and threat to maintain pressure.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race aggressively and use Chalice on 1 or 2 to disrupt their mana acceleration. Use discard to remove ramp spells or key combo pieces. Don't let them stabilize; pressure them into mistakes before they can cast their finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use discard to remove early threats and hold back mana for spot removal if available. Prioritize blocking or trading efficiently to stabilize, then transition to a midgame pressure strategy. Use Chalice on 1 to disrupt their early game.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply relentless pressure with cheap threats and discard to remove key control pieces. Win before they can stabilize or find their answer cards. Use Chalice on 1 or 2 to disrupt their mana and disruption spells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by trading efficiently, using discard to remove their threats, and leveraging Broadside Bombardiers' scaling ability. Prioritize sequencing your Mox and Chalice to disrupt their early game, and don't overcommit if they have sweepers or removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your Chalice targets based on the matchup (1 vs. control/combo, 2 vs. midrange/aggro). Increase discard density against control and mirror matches to disrupt their hand. Bring in hate cards against combo and recursion strategies. Maintain threat density against aggro and midrange to ensure you can close out games quickly. Always keep at least one Mox and one Chalice in your main deck unless the matchup dictates otherwise.
notes: Chrome Mox is a one-shot, so sequence it carefully to maximize its impact. Chalice of the Void can be dead if the opponent's key spells are at unexpected mana values, so be flexible with your targets. Broadside Bombardiers scales with damage dealt, so use direct damage or discard to remove blockers before attacking. Avoid overextending into known sweepers, and always track the opponent's hand for counters and removal. Remember that Red-Black Stompy wins by out-sping the opponent in the early game, so prioritize speed and disruption over late-game value.

philosophy: Loam Pox is a disruptive midrange/graveyard engine deck that aims to lock opponents out of their game plan through aggressive discard, land disruption, and graveyard hate, while steadily assembling a resilient board of recurring threats. It wins by out-grinding opponents with Field of the Dead/Thespian's Stage recursion or closing out games early with Orcish Bowmasters and Pox effects.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 playable lands, at least one early interaction (Thoughtseize, Pox, or Orcish Bowmasters), and a turn-1 land drop. Keep Mox Diamond if paired with a land for turn-1 casting. Mulligan on 2 lands with no spells, 3+ lands with no early pressure, or hands lacking a turn-1 land drop.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on the opponent's draw step to strip combo pieces, removal, or key answers. If opening with Mox Diamond, play land, Mox Diamond, then Thoughtseize. Turn 2: Cast Orcish Bowmasters or deploy Pox/Liliana depending on hand. Sequence lands to ensure black mana for disruption and green for threats/recursion. Deploy Field of the Dead and Thespian's Stage as soon as you have four or more creature cards in your graveyard.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver, Ocelot Pride): Bring in Rest in Peace, Graveyard Protector, and surgical removal. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir, Tron): Bring in additional discard (Liliana, Inquisition), Pox effects, and Leyline of the Void. vs Combo (Sneak & Show): Bring in Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and graveyard hate. vs Mirror: Bring in extra discard, Pox, and graveyard recursion protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize black mana for disruption and green for threats/recursion; avoid overextending into sweepers. Use Thoughtseize and Liliana to strip key answers and control the board state. Treat your graveyard as a resource for Field of the Dead, but monitor opponent's graveyard hate to avoid dead cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have lock pieces, removal, or a clear path to close. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, graveyard hate, or can stabilize. Use Pox to tax the opponent's board, protect your own, and force unfavorable trades.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline), sweepers (Wrath, Terminus), and combo wincons. Pay attention to opponent's discard targets and hand size to predict their next play. Note when they start drawing extra cards or playing lands, as this signals a shift to their late-game plan.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Field of the Dead paired with Thespian's Stage for recursive board presence, or Orcish Bowmasters with Pox effects for early closure. Use Liliana to mill key creatures or remove opposing threats. Chain Pox effects to tax the opponent's board while your creatures recur.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have lock pieces in play, the opponent lacks sweepers or graveyard hate, and you can stabilize or close the game. Commit when the opponent is at low life and you have a clear path to victory, or when you can force a favorable trade that leaves you with board advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void and you lack removal or alternative win conditions. Concede if you are locked out by control/tempo with no way to break through, or if you have a dead hand with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to strip counterspells before casting threats. Cast multiple threats in a row to force multiple counters, or use Pox to tax their board and make counterspells less efficient. Sequence spells to maximize value even if countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize to strip mana rocks and key ramp spells. Use Pox to tax their board and disrupt their game plan. If they rely on graveyard ramp, bring in Rest in Peace or Surgical Extraction.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early discard to strip threats and stabilize. Deploy Pox to tax the opponent's board and protect your own. Orcish Bowmasters can race or apply pressure while you lock them out. Bring in graveyard hate if they use graveyard synergies.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats to force answers. Use discard to strip key removal and counterspells. Out-grind with graveyard recursion and Field of the Dead. Force them to use resources on your threats while you assemble your win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by prioritizing hand disruption and tracking opponent's graveyard. Control the board with Pox to tax their threats and protect your own. Out-value with better sequencing and graveyard management. Bring in extra discard and Pox effects to dominate the midgame.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline) against combo and control decks, and additional disruption (Liliana, Inquisition) against aggro and midrange. Adjust your land count if you're facing decks that flood or require specific mana. Always carry surgical removal for key threats and ensure your sideboard aligns with the metagame's top archetypes.
notes: Watch for Leyline of the Void in your opening hand and mulligan accordingly if you lack removal. Mox Diamond requires careful mana sequencing to avoid dead mana. Orcish Bowmasters synergizes with discard by enabling early pressure while you lock the opponent out. Field of the Dead needs careful graveyard management to avoid dead cards. Avoid overcommitting to the graveyard if graveyard hate is likely, and always track opponent's hand size to predict their next play.

philosophy: Rapidly fill your graveyard with Cabal Therapy and dredge cards to generate a massive army of creatures or cast a game-winning threat using Lion's Eye Diamond. The deck aims to race faster than midrange and tempo decks while leveraging discard to disrupt control and combo strategies before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with Cabal Therapy, at least one dredge card (Stinkweed Imp, Nighthowler, etc.), and 1-2 lands. Prioritize hands with LED if paired with a dredge card and land. Mulligan pure mana hands, hands with only dredge cards and no lands, or hands with Cabal Therapy but no targets/lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Cabal Therapy to discard an opponent's key spell or card, or self-mill to set up the graveyard. Turn 2: Play a dredge creature or LED. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Cabal Therapy and play a dredge card by turn 2. If opening LED, hold for a turn to ensure you have a dredge card and land to activate it profitably.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control/tempo: Bring in Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and additional discard (Dismember/Inquisition). Against aggro/midrange: Bring in Anger of the Gods, Go for the Throat, and additional threats. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate and fast discard. Against mirror: Bring in extra discard and hate cards like Leyline.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as your library; preserve key dredge cards and LED. Mana is tight, so sequence Cabal Therapy and dredge triggers to maximize value. Never activate LED unless you have a clear path to victory or a necessary discard outlet.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit dredge creatures to apply pressure and force answers, but hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use Cabal Therapy to remove threats before they resolve. Prioritize milling over attacking with small creatures unless you can close the game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Leyline, Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction) and sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods). Note when opponents hold up mana for counterspells or removal, as this dictates whether you should go off with LED or hold back. Track their hand size after Cabal Therapy to gauge their resilience.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lion's Eye Diamond combined with a high-impact threat like Blightsteel Colossus, Phyrexian Obliterator, or a massive Stinkweed Imp/Nighthowler swarm. Sequence Cabal Therapy to discard the threat, then activate LED to cast it or trigger a massive dredge sequence.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have LED, a dredge card, and a land, or when you can cast a win condition that the opponent cannot answer in time. Commit if your opponent has no graveyard hate or sweepers in hand and you can close the game before their next turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are dead to graveyard hate with no way to bypass it, if you have no mana or dredge cards to mill, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., multiple sweepers, artifact lock, or insurmountable value). Also concede if you are drawing dead against a combo that will win on their next turn.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Cabal Therapy to discard counterspells before they resolve. Sequence dredge triggers carefully, as they go on the stack and can be countered. If countered, use LED to generate mana for a backup plan or discard the countered spell to disrupt their hand.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them by prioritizing fast Cabal Therapy and dredge sequences. Discard their ramp spells and early threats. If they outpace you, use LED to go off immediately rather than trying to grind.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Discard their early threats and key removal. Mill quickly to win before they stabilize. Use Cabal Therapy to disrupt their curve and hold back dredge creatures until you can swing for lethal or block efficiently.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early with Cabal Therapy to remove key pieces (sweepers, counters, win conditions). Go off with LED before they can stabilize. If they have graveyard hate, pivot to discarding their hand and racing with creatures.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by sequencing Cabal Therapy to remove their key cards (LED, dredge cards, threats). Value your graveyard resources and force them to overcommit. Use hate cards if sideboarded, and prioritize milling to out-value them in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your discard and hate to the metagame. Against control, prioritize Leyline of the Void and Surgical Extraction to protect your graveyard. Against aggro, add removal and faster threats. Always keep at least one copy of Cabal Therapy and LED, and adjust your land count if you're running a splash. Remember that sideboard cards like Rest in Peace are game-ending against dredge, so track them carefully.
notes: Dredge triggers go on the stack and can be responded to, so don't assume they will resolve. LED is an artifact, so it can be destroyed or tapped for mana if needed. Cabal Therapy is your most versatile card; use it to discard opponent's threats, set up your own graveyard, or mill yourself. Avoid over-milling without a win condition, and always count your opponent's hand size after Cabal Therapy to make informed decisions. Watch for Leyline of the Void in the sideboard, as it can completely shut you down if not addressed.

philosophy: Mono Black Midrange aims to dominate the early board with efficient discard and removal while establishing a resilient, escalating threat curve centered on Nethergoyf and deathtouch creatures like Moonshadow and Dauthi Voidwalker. It wins by out-resourcing opponents through card advantage, leveraging sacrifice synergies, and closing out games with unblockable or unanswerable pressure once the board stabilizes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (Swamps or duals), plus at least one early interaction (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push) or a 1B creature (Moonshadow/Dauthi Voidwalker). Keep hands with Nethergoyf if supported by a land and a discard outlet. Mulligan pure curve decks, hands with 0-1 lands, or hands with only expensive threats and no early board presence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Swamp, cast Thoughtseize on a key non-basic land or early threat, or drop a 1B creature if holding lands. Turn 2: Develop board with a second land, cast Nethergoyf if opponent has sacrificed creatures, or play a cheap deathtouch creature. Prioritize establishing a 2-drop board presence over rushing; Nethergoyf scales best when opponents are forced to sacrifice to your removal or their own effects.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Delver, Sneak & Show): Bring in Surgical Extraction, Rest in Peace, or graveyard hate; add more removal (Fatal Push, Go for the Throat) and discard. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in Liliana, Defiant Grand Master, or additional discard; add more threats like Nethergoyf and Moonshadow. vs Mirror: Side in additional removal and discard to trade efficiently; keep Nethergoyf as a late-game wincon.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana should be kept flexible for 1B spells and 2-drop creatures; avoid flooding with Swamps if you have multiple 1B threats. Use discard to disrupt opponent's mana or key combo pieces. Manage graveyard carefully—Nethergoyf benefits from opponent sacrifices, but you must avoid filling your own graveyard with unplayable cards if facing graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready to answer their interaction or when Nethergoyf is already large. Hold back if facing sweepers or if your hand is light on answers. Prioritize trading deathtouch creatures into their removal to preserve Nethergoyf for late-game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's discard targets (prioritize keeping Nethergoyf and removal). Track their graveyard size and sacrifice outlets. Note if they're playing around your removal with unblockable or hexproof threats. Watch for mana development—if they're ramping, prioritize early disruption.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Nethergoyf is the primary win condition, especially when it reaches 5+ toughness. Moonshadow and Dauthi Voidwalker provide deathtouch pressure that forces trades. Use discard to strip their removal, then swing with Nethergoyf and deathtouch creatures.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, your hand is light on threats but heavy on Nethergoyf/Moonshadow, and you've removed or bypassed their main interaction. Commit when Nethergoyf is large enough to trade through their best removal or when you can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a combo deck that has assembled its win condition and you have no graveyard hate or instant-speed interaction. Concede if you're locked out of the game by a massive board (e.g., Tron with multiple artifacts) and your hand contains only high-cost threats with no removal. Also concede if you're in a dead mirror with no card advantage engine and no way to break the board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Work around it with discard to remove counterspells before they can cast them. Use cheap, efficient threats that can be cast in response to their spells. If they counter your removal, use discard to strip their hand and apply pressure with Nethergoyf, which is hard to counter if cast at instant speed or if they're out of mana.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early disruption (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push) to hit their lands or key ramp spells. Use discard to remove their mana rocks or finishers. Nethergoyf scales well against ramp if they sacrifice creatures to your removal or their own effects.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal (Fatal Push, Go for the Throat) and discard to disrupt their early game. Drop Nethergoyf as soon as possible to apply pressure and force them to sacrifice creatures. Use deathtouch creatures to block efficiently and trade into their best creatures.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their hand with discard to prevent them from setting up sweepers or counters. Use Nethergoyf as a threat that grows over time, forcing them to use multiple removal spells. If they have a sweeper, wait for them to use it, then rebuild with your resilient creature suite.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with better card advantage (discard, lifelink, or graveyard synergies). Prioritize trading efficiently with deathtouch creatures and use Nethergoyf as a late-game wincon. Sideboard in additional removal and discard to control the midgame. Track their hand and graveyard to avoid falling for their traps.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your threat/reaction ratio based on the metagame. Against control/tempo, increase your discard and late-game threats. Against aggro/combo, prioritize hate cards and early interaction. Always keep at least one copy of Nethergoyf and Moonshadow/Dauthi Voidwalker to maintain your core game plan. Adjust land counts if needed to ensure consistency.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board too early, which leaves you vulnerable to sweepers, or failing to use discard effectively to disrupt opponent's key pieces. Remember that Nethergoyf's power is tied to opponent sacrifices, so your removal and their own effects are your best friends. Avoid filling your graveyard unnecessarily if facing graveyard hate. Play around common sideboard cards like Rest in Peace or Surgical Extraction by keeping threats in hand.

philosophy: Selesnya Depths is a combo-control deck that aims to assemble Dark Depths with two Inkwell Elders to create a 13/13 flying unblockable threat, leveraging its Island subtype to bypass non-flying removal. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and tempo archetypes with efficient acceleration and late-game inevitability, while using spot removal and disruption to control the early game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (including fetches or Mox Diamond), Green Sun's Zenith, or early removal. Keep hands with Mox Diamond + fetcher + basic island. Mulligan pure curve decks without lands, GSZ, or early interaction. Prioritize hands that can cast a 1-drop or fetch a basic island on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Mox Diamond or fetch a basic island. Turn 2: Cast Green Sun's Zenith for Inkwell Elder or City of Traitors, or play a 2-drop like Knight of the Reliquary. Sequence: Fetch -> Mox Diamond -> GSZ -> Elders. Protect GSZ with early removal or discard. If opponent plays aggressively, use Swords to Plowshares or Natural Order to stabilize before pursuing the combo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride): Bring in Swords to Plowshares, Natural Order, and Rest in Peace. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in extra removal, additional GSZ/Elders, and graveyard hate if needed. vs Combo (Sneak and Show): Bring in Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment, and extra counterspells/protection. vs Mirror: Bring in additional Swords, Natural Order, and extra combo pieces to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard GSZ and Inkwell Elders for the combo turn. Use Mox Diamond to smooth mana, but remember it requires a basic island or 2 life to untap. Don't overcommit to the board if your combo is incomplete. Treat your graveyard as both fuel for Dark Depths and a liability against hate cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Use spot removal to clear key threats without trading into your own combo pieces. Hold back Elders until you have a clear path or protection. If the opponent has multiple removal spells, delay the combo and apply pressure with early threats or discard to force them out.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Force of Will, Daze, and counterspells in Dimir Tempo. Track when Tron players tap out for Gaea's Cradle or Arcane Signet. Note when Sneak and Show players hold up interaction for Thassa's Oracle or Show and Tell. Pay attention to graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) and adjust your win condition accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Dark Depths + 2 Inkwell Elders = 13/13 unblockable threat. If you only have 1 Elder, use City of Traitors or pay life to remove the remaining ice counters. Once assembled, attack with Dark Depths; it's unblockable by non-flying creatures, making it nearly impossible to block without flying or removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 2+ Elders, Dark Depths, and a way to remove ice counters, or when the opponent has no removal/counters in hand. Commit early if the board is clear and you can close the game before they can rebuild.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if your combo is broken, the opponent has multiple hard counters/removal, and you have no backup plan. Concede if your graveyard is empty, you can't assemble pieces, and the opponent is ahead on board or has established a lock.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Sequence GSZ and Elders around counterspells by using cheap mana or casting in response to their counters. If they counter GSZ, use the remaining mana to cast Elders naturally. Consider holding a Swords to Plowshares to clear their protection spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them with efficient removal and combo inevitability. Use Swords and Natural Order to clear their mana rocks. Pressure with early threats to force them to interact, then combo when they're tapped out.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal, discard, or fast combo. Use Mox Diamond to accelerate into GSZ and Elders. Don't let them establish a board; trade efficiently and close the game before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, then combo. Use your graveyard to your advantage by keeping Dark Depths and Elders in play. Force them to use resources on interaction, then win with the combo when they're out of answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by tracking graveyard and using removal efficiently. Combo first if you have the pieces, or out-value them with Swords and Natural Order. Don't overcommit to the board; preserve your combo pieces and force them to make mistakes.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards control if facing combo-heavy metas, or add more combo pieces if facing control. Adjust land count if you're struggling with mana screw or flood. Prioritize hate cards against specific archetypes, and consider bringing in additional protection for your combo pieces if the meta is heavy on targeted removal.
notes: Dark Depths is a land, so it can be targeted by land destruction; keep a Swords to Plowshares or Natural Order to protect it. Mox Diamond's drawback requires careful mana management; don't overextend without a basic island. GSZ is vulnerable to counters but searches for a creature, so sequence carefully. Don't forget Dark Depths is unblockable by non-flying, which is key to its win condition. Avoid overcommitting to the board before assembling the combo, as midrange decks can out-value you in extended turns.

philosophy: Esper Control aims to stabilize the early game with efficient disruption and card selection, then out-value opponents through superior resource management and late-game threats. It wins by grinding out tempo, combo, and midrange decks while avoiding unfavorable trades.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Ponder, and at least one early interaction (Cabal Therapy, Path to Exile, or a counterspell). Keep any hand with Ponder + 2 lands. Mulligan pure land draws, hands with multiple Ponders but no lands, or hands with only late-game threats and no early answers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, Ponder if possible to set up draws. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Cabal Therapy against combo/midrange, or hold for a threat. Sequence lands carefully to ensure blue mana availability for counterspells and card draw. Prioritize stabilizing plays over aggressive ones.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Izzet Delver): +1 Dispel/Spell Pierce, +1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Cabal Therapy, -1 Ajani. vs Tron/Sneak and Show: +1 Surgical Extraction, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Dispel, -1 Cabal Therapy, -1 Nacatl Pariah. vs Ocelot Pride/Midrange: +1 Path to Exile, +1 Ajani, -1 Cabal Therapy, -1 Ponder.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Ponders for when you need a specific answer or threat. Don't overcommit mana to Ponder if you can't protect it. Manage the graveyard with Cabal Therapy or Surgical Extraction against Tron/Sneak and Show. Keep hand size above 3 when possible.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing Ajani or Nacatl Pariah until you've established a baseline of interaction. Use Cryptic Command or Jace to clear blockers or protect your board. Only commit threats when you have at least one answer to the opponent's likely response.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron's signets/mana rocks, Sneak and Show's graveyard setup, Delver's flip threshold, and Ocelot Pride's mana acceleration. Note if they're holding up blue mana (counterspell) or black mana (Therapy). Track graveyard contents against combo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ajani for lifelink and board presence, Nacatl Pariah for aggressive pressure, and Jace for card advantage. Win by out-resourcing and closing with a board of threats or a planeswalker.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage secured, the opponent has no hard removal, and you can protect your finisher with counterspells or lifegain. Commit when you can swing and still have an answer to their removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life with no interaction against Tron/Sneak and Show, or if you're facing a locked-out Delver/Tempo with no draw spells. Concede if you have a dead hand (only Ponders/late threats) and the opponent is ahead on board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig for answers or threats that can't be countered. Cast threats in response to counterspells, or use Cryptic Command to bounce their counter source. Don't overextend into known counter suites.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early Cabal Therapy to disrupt mana dorks/Tron. Surgical Extraction to remove key permanents. Path to Exile to trade efficiently. Don't let them stabilize; pressure early.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (Therapy, Bolt, Path). Use Ajani for lifegain and board presence. Hold back on Ponders until you stabilize. Use Cryptic Command to bounce key threats and draw.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw (Ponder, Brainstorm, Jace). Pressure with threats they can't efficiently remove. Use Cryptic Command to disrupt their mana or protect your board. Win the card advantage war.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Prioritize Ponders/Brainstorms and Jace. Use Cryptic Command to bounce their threats or protect yours. Cabal Therapy to disrupt their hand. Win by out-drawing and out-resolving.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure control to matchup-specific strategies. Against combo/ramp, bring in hate cards and early disruption. Against aggro/midrange, bring in efficient removal and lifegain. Always keep Ponder and key counterspells. Adjust your play style to be more proactive or reactive based on the sideboard.
notes: Ponder is your best card; never cast it blindly. Cabal Therapy is versatile but can be dead against control; know when to hold it. Ajani's lifelink is crucial against aggressive decks. Cryptic Command is a win-more card in some matchups but essential in others. Watch for Tron's 5-mana turn and Sneak and Show's graveyard recursion.

philosophy: This Izzet ramp-combo deck aims to accelerate mana, establish a 4+ power nonland permanent to cast Etali, Primal Conqueror at a massive discount, and leverage Trumpeting Carnosaur’s death trigger to chain into additional threats. It wins by overwhelming midrange and control decks before they can stabilize, while leveraging speed and recursion against tempo and combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (prioritizing duals/acceleration like Chrome Mox, Mox Opal, Arcane Signet, or basic Islands/Plains) plus at least one way to cast a 4+ power creature or ramp spell. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only low-cost spells and no acceleration or 4+ power threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast acceleration or a cantrip. Turn 2: Cast a 4+ power creature (e.g., Karn, Glimmerpoint Stag, or Carnosaur itself if mana allows) or ramp into it. Sequence lands to ensure U/R availability for counterspells or removal if needed, but prioritize casting threats/ramp to set up the Etali discount.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Dimir Tempo/Izzet Delver: +2 Flusterstorm, +2 Lightning Axe; -2 Trumpeting Carnosaur, -1 Etali. vs Tron/Ocelot Pride: +2 Surgical Extraction, +2 Abrade; -2 lands. vs Sneak and Show: +2 Red Elemental Blast, +2 Pyroclasm; -2 ramp spells. vs Mirror: +2 Flusterstorm, +2 Lightning Axe; -2 lands.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve counterspells for key threats or combo pieces. Use graveyard recursion efficiently—don't dump Carnosaur unless you have a follow-up plan or can protect it. Cantrip to maintain hand size and dig for answers or finishers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use Etali’s haste to pressure, but be mindful of board wipes and graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), counterspells (Counterspell, Flusterstorm), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Null Rod). Note when opponents tap out for removal or counters to identify windows for your combo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Etali, Primal Conqueror attacking twice with trample/flying, or a chain of Carnosaur deaths triggering into a lethal threat. Use cantrips to dig for the final piece and close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have mana open for protection, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can close the game in 1-2 turns. Commit when you can safely cast Etali and follow up with a threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to a single land, have no answers to an established board, or the opponent has locked you out with graveyard hate/sweepers and you can’t rebuild. Concede if you’re drawing dead against a locked combo or control deck.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split the cast (cast one, respond to counter), or use protection/enlightened. Cantrip into answers and pressure with multiple threats to force counter trade-offs.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on their mana sources, and sideboard in hate bears. Don't overextend into their late-game finishers; stabilize and close the game quickly.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early removal, stabilize with counterspells, and don't overcommit to big threats until you have board control. Use cantrips to find answers and keep pressure on.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use cantrips to find answers, and sideboard in direct damage. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades, protect your finishers, and use counterspells to protect your own plays. Sideboard in hate for their recursion and prioritize graveyard interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust land counts based on matchup (more basics vs control, more duals vs combo). Prioritize bringing in hate cards against combo and control, and removal against aggro. Keep the core Etali/Carnosaur engine intact unless facing graveyard hate, in which case pivot to direct damage and early pressure.
notes: Avoid casting Carnosaur without a follow-up plan. Etali’s discount requires a 4+ power nonland permanent on the battlefield—Carnosaur itself can enable this. Watch for Sneak and Show or combo decks that can go off before you stabilize. Practice sequencing mana to ensure you can cast both spells in the same turn if needed. Remember that graveyard hate is your primary weakness; carry sideboard hate or play around it.

philosophy: A fast combo-aggro deck that leverages Food Chain and Goblin Lackey to cast high-impact goblins for free, generating immediate board presence and card advantage. It aims to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize, winning against control and combo decks by racing them or out-valueing them with recursive threats and pump spells.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (duals/lands that fetch), Goblin Lackey, Food Chain, or a 2-drop (Piledriver/Chieftain/Rabblemaster). Prioritize hands that can execute a Turn 1 Lackey into a Turn 2 free cast. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no combo pieces or threats, or hands with only 1 land and no early plays. Against control-heavy fields, keep hands with Disfigure or Hexing Squelcher.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Goblin Lackey. Turn 2: Play land, cast Food Chain, sacrifice Lackey to cast Goblin Piledriver or Chieftain. If you have a pump spell or Rabblemaster, hold it for Turn 2 or 3 to maximize value. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast the free spell on Turn 2. If you draw a 2-drop in hand, you can cast it for free on Turn 2. Always prioritize establishing a board that forces unfavorable trades or goes off before the opponent can react.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Dimir Tempo/Izzet Delver: Bring in Disfigure, Hexing Squelcher, Scuttling Doom Engine. Against Sneak and Show: Bring in Disfigure, Surgical Extraction, Goblin Bomb, Scuttling Doom Engine. Against Ocelot Pride Midrange: Bring in Scuttling Doom Engine, Hexing Squelcher, Disfigure. Against Aggro: Bring in Disfigure, Hexing Squelcher, Goblin Bomb. Against Mirror: Bring in Disfigure, Scuttling Doom Engine, Hexing Squelcher. Reduce lands if your mulligan rate suffers post-sideboard.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Do not overcommit to Food Chain without a target or protection. Use Hexing Squelcher to protect key threats or recur Goblin Welder. Manage your graveyard carefully against hate, but generally keep it stocked with Lackey/Chain/Welder. Use lands efficiently; do not untap with excess mana if you can cast a threat or cast Hexing Squelcher for {1}.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a way to protect them or when the board is clear. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment, etc.) or hard counters. Use Goblin Bomb to clear blockers or deal direct damage. Prioritize establishing a board that forces unfavorable trades or goes off before the opponent can stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note if the opponent is playing around your combo (holding up removal/counters). Track their mana and hand size to know when to go off. Pay attention to whether they are playing around your pump spells or their ability to answer recursive threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Goblin Piledriver with Rabblemaster/Bomb, Scuttling Doom Engine, or a swarm of goblins with pump spells. Get there by chaining Lackey/Chain, then using Welder/Squelcher to recur threats, or pumping Rabblemaster to lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no sweepers, or you're ahead in the race. Go all-in with pump spells and bombs. If the opponent has no interaction left, commit your entire hand and board to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Tron with no interaction, or a combo that goes off before you can stabilize), or if you're dead to the board (e.g., opponent has multiple sweepers and you have no removal). Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent is ahead in the race.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple targets (Lackey into Chain into Piledriver), or cast threats in response to counters. Use Hexing Squelcher to protect key spells. Sometimes you need to go off at instant speed or use pump spells to overcome counters. If they counter your combo, pivot to aggressive goblin swarms or Scuttling Doom Engine.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them. Use Goblin Bomb to deal direct damage. Use Disfigure to remove their ramp creatures. Do not let them stabilize; pressure early and go off as soon as you have a lethal threat.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Hexing Squelcher to protect your threats, Disfigure to remove key aggro creatures, and Goblin Bomb to deal damage. You can also go off quickly to win before they kill you. Do not overextend into sweepers; hold back interaction for their key threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure early, use Disfigure to remove their answers, and go off with multiple threats. Use Hexing Squelcher to protect your combo pieces. Do not let them stabilize; keep the pressure on and go off at instant speed when they show vulnerability.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race. Prioritize card advantage and protection. Use Disfigure to remove their threats, Hexing Squelcher to protect yours, and Scuttling Doom Engine to recur. Track their graveyard and hand size. Win the interaction war by holding up Disfigure for their key threats and going off when they are tapped out.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Generally, bring in more interaction (Disfigure, Hexing Squelcher) against control/combo, and more bombs (Scuttling Doom Engine) against midrange. Reduce lands if your mulligan rate suffers, but keep the mana base consistent. Always have a plan for graveyard hate and sweepers. In mirror matches, prioritize Disfigure and Hexing Squelcher to control the board and protect your combo pieces.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting to Food Chain without a target, forgetting to protect your combo pieces, mismanaging the graveyard, not tracking opponent's hand size. Unique interactions: Hexing Squelcher can protect Goblin Lackey or Food Chain from removal. Scuttling Doom Engine can recur Goblin Welder for infinite value if you have enough goblins. Remember that Goblin Piledriver's power is based on goblins you control, not just the ones you cast. Always be ready to pivot to an aggressive plan if your combo is disrupted.

philosophy: The deck aims to out-resource opponents through relentless card selection, milling, and recursive value engines, leveraging Flow State to cast multiple low-cost spells per turn and Cephalid Illusionist to maintain hand advantage. It wins by overwhelming tempo and midrange strategies with unanswerable card advantage and out-grinding control/combo decks through superior sequencing and graveyard recursion.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize fetch/dual lands for WUB consistency), Ponder or Flow State, and at least one early game piece (Cephalid Illusionist or a cheap cantrip). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only high-cost spells, or no way to interact/develop on turn 1-2. Keep any hand with Ponder + 2 lands as it provides immediate setup.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Ponder if possible to filter into key pieces or lands. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Cephalid Illusionist or a cheap cantrip, then follow up with Flow State if you have multiple spells with CMC < 3 in hand. Sequence lands to ensure WUB mana by turn 3, prioritizing utility lands that can enter untapped or fetch basics.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Dimir Tempo/Izzet Delver: Bring in Flusterstorm, Thoughtseize, Dismember. vs Tron: Bring in Thoughtseize, Dismember, Leyline of the Void. vs Sneak and Show: Bring in Flusterstorm, Thoughtseize, Leyline of the Void. vs Ocelot Pride Midrange: Bring in Dismember, Thoughtseize, Flusterstorm. Always keep Ponder and Flow State as they are core to the deck's engine.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Flow State for turns where you can cast 3+ spells with CMC < 3. Use Ponder to dig for key pieces or lands rather than spending it on filler. Don't overcommit mana if you can't follow up; mill strategically to set up recursion or win conditions while preserving interaction for critical turns.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have card advantage in hand or can protect them with counterspells/removal. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy interaction. Use milling to disrupt opponent's graveyard-dependent decks and set up your own recursion targets. Protect Flow State with counters or discard when possible.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Supreme Verdict), counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents hold up mana for multiple turns (likely holding interaction). Track their graveyard size if they play graveyard synergies, and monitor their library size if they're running mill or combo win conditions.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cephalid Illusionist + Flow State combos, milling the opponent's library to zero, or recursive value engines that out-value the board. Get there by stabilizing the board, maintaining card advantage, and setting up the graveyard for recursion or lethal damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Flow State on the stack, multiple cheap spells in hand, and the opponent has no sweepers or interaction. Also commit when you can mill the opponent's library to zero or when you have a guaranteed lethal combo. Don't hesitate to cast Flow State if you can cast 3+ spells and the opponent has no counterspells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a dead hand, the opponent has established a dominant board with sweepers/removal, or you're in a matchup where your game plan is completely neutralized (e.g., heavy graveyard hate with no sideboard answers). Also concede if you're out of cards and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Flow State to cast multiple spells, making it hard to counter all. Use Ponder to dig for uncounterable threats or protection. Sequence spells to force counters on less important ones, and save counterspells for their key interaction. Consider casting Flow State in response to their counterspell to maximize value.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use Thoughtseize to steal key ramp pieces, and use Dismember to remove their mana sources. Out-value them with card draw and mill strategically to disrupt their late-game plan. Don't let them stabilize; keep the pressure on with cheap threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early interaction (Flusterstorm, Dismember), then pivot to value engines. Don't overextend; keep blockers or removal ready. Use Ponder to dig for answers and maintain hand size. Once stabilized, transition to milling and recursion to out-grind them.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap threats, use Flow State to cast multiple spells before they can respond. Disrupt their hand with Thoughtseize. Out-grind them with card advantage and mill strategically to disrupt their late-game plan. Don't let them stabilize; keep the pressure on with cheap threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Use Thoughtseize to strip their Flow State or key cantrips. Prioritize keeping lands and interaction. Win by out-drawing and out-recursing. Sequence Ponder to find the right pieces and use Flow State to cast multiple spells when the opponent has no counterspells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against control, bring in more hand disruption and uncounterable threats. Against aggro, prioritize early removal and life gain. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and additional counters. Always keep Ponder and Flow State as they are core to the deck's engine. Adjust your land base if needed to ensure WUB consistency against specific matchups.
notes: Flow State is your most powerful card but requires careful sequencing; don't cast it unless you have a clear plan to cast 3+ spells with CMC < 3. Ponder is essential for consistency; never skip it unless you're on the play and need to develop. Watch out for Leyline of the Void or Rest in Peace as they can shut down your recursion. Practice your mana base to ensure WUB consistency, and always prioritize card selection over raw power in the early game.

philosophy: Azorius Stoneblade is a control-midrange hybrid that stabilizes early with efficient interaction and card advantage, then transitions to a value engine using Stoneforge Mystic to find Sword of Feast and Famine and Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student to dominate the midgame and close out games with equipment threats or Tamiyo’s exile effects. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on specific spells or slow game plans, leveraging disruption and card selection to out-value opponents.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize basics or fetchable duals), a turn-1 land, and at least one of: Stoneforge Mystic, Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student, Stifle, or efficient interaction (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares, Counterspell). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands with no spells, or hands consisting solely of counterspells/removal without threats or Stoneforge. Prioritize keeping hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1, or two lands and a spell on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip (Opt/Opt) or cheap interaction if threatened, or play a 1-drop if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, then cast Stoneforge Mystic or Tamiyo if possible. If opponent plays a threat, use spot removal or counterspell to stabilize. If they pass, develop board with Tamiyo or equip Sword of Feast and Famine to a threat. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/finding basic Plains/Islands early to ensure consistent casting of WU spells; don't overextend into duals if you're vulnerable to sweepers or need immediate color fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver, Ocelot Pride): +1 Stifle, +1 Path to Exile, +1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Tamiyo, -1 Sword of Feast and Famine, -1 Counterspell. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir, Tron): +1 Counterspell, +1 Mana Leak, +1 Stifle, -1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Sword, -1 Tamiyo. vs Combo (Sneak and Show): +2 Stifle, +1 Counterspell, +1 Path, -1 Tamiyo, -1 Sword, -1 Lightning Bolt. vs Mirror: +1 Swords to Plowshares, +1 Stifle, -1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Sword.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve counterspells for key combo pieces or game-winning threats; use spot removal on early pressure. Tamiyo’s loyalty should be spent on card draw (+1) when ahead, or exile/removal (-2/-3) when behind or facing problematic permanents. Sword of Feast and Famine is a win condition and removal tool—equip it to your best threat or use it to exile a key permanent. Don't overcommit mana into Tamiyo if you can't protect it; keep a counterspell or Stifle in hand as insurance.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction to answer their response or when you're ahead in cards. Hold back on playing Tamiyo or Stoneforge if the opponent has sweepers or mass removal in hand. Use Stifle preemptively against combo decks rather than waiting to react. Prioritize board presence over greedy card advantage if you're at 15 life or below.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo decks playing Show and Tell, Delver of Secrets, or Arcane Signet/Chalice of the Void. Track opponent's hand size and discard effects; if they've Thoughtseized or Duress, expect a specific game plan. Note when they play cheap interaction—they're likely setting up a midgame threat or combo. Pay attention to Tamiyo's exile zone; if they're trying to exile your Sword or Mystic, they're targeting your engine.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sword of Feast and Famine equipped to a 4/4 or larger threat, Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student (especially with +1 card draw to find the sword or a finisher), and a well-timed Stifle to protect your win condition. Sometimes a single Sword of Feast and Famine on a 3/3 with trample/first strike is enough to close out games.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a Sword equipped, Tamiyo on the board, and at least one counterspell or Stifle to protect your board. Also commit when the opponent has no removal or sweepers in hand and you're ahead in life. If you've stabilized and have a clear path to damage, stop holding back and start attacking with equipped threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're at 5 life or below with no interaction, no threats, and the opponent has a clear win condition (e.g., Tron with 7 mana, Sneak and Show creature in play, or Delver with Inverter of Truth). Also concede if you're in a dead mirror with no card draw and the opponent has a Sword equipped. If your sideboard plan didn't come together and you're consistently losing to their specific game plan, it's time to tap out.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Stifle to counter their counterspells or key spells. Cast threats at end step or when they have no mana. Use Tamiyo's +1 to draw into answers or finishers. Don't overcommit to a single threat; attack with multiple small threats if possible.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use spot removal and counterspells to disrupt their mana development. Stifle on Arcane Signet, Chalice of the Void, or key ramp spells. Tamiyo is excellent here—exile their mana rocks or threats. Don't let them hit 7 mana; apply pressure with equipped threats to force them to interact.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize spot removal and counterspells on early threats. Use Tamiyo to protect your board and draw cards. Stifle is crucial against Delver or other combo-aggro decks. Don't overextend into sweepers; stabilize at 10-12 life and start closing out games with Sword of Feast and Famine.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Tamiyo and card draw. Use Stifle to protect your threats from sweepers or counters. Attack with equipped threats to force them to use removal. Don't let them stabilize; apply consistent pressure and use your interaction efficiently.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Use Tamiyo to draw into more cards. Stifle their counterspells or key spells. Prioritize keeping Sword of Feast and Famine in play. Use spot removal efficiently and don't overcommit to a single threat. Track their hand and use counterspells to protect your win conditions.
