sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a control-heavy shell to a more interactive or aggressive stance depending on the matchup. Against combo and aggro, prioritize disruption (Stifle, spot removal) and reduce card draw. Against control and midrange, increase counterspells and protection, and keep Tamiyo as your primary value engine. Always track opponent's sideboard cards and adjust your interaction accordingly—don't overcommit to Stifle if they've maindeck sweepers, and vice versa.
notes: Common mistakes include overplaying Tamiyo without protection, using Sword of Feast and Famine as mere removal instead of a win condition, and hoarding counterspells until it's too late. Remember that Stoneforge Mystic is a 3-mana 2/2—don't attack with it unless you're sure it's safe. Tamiyo's -2 ability can exile your own threats if you're not careful; always check the exile zone. Stifle is a 1-mana instant—use it to counter key spells rather than just combo pieces. Finally, this deck thrives on patience; don't force plays when you can out-value the opponent in the midgame.

philosophy: Ruby Storm is an explosive combo deck that leverages cheap red spells and Ruby Medallion to generate massive mana and storm count, aiming to win in a single turn or through recursive extra turns with Jeska's Will. It outpaces slower, resource-heavy archetypes by assembling its win condition before opponents can stabilize or interact.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 fetchable/untapped lands, Ruby Medallion, and at least 2-3 cheap red spells (Seething Song, Lightning Bolt, etc.). Keep Jeska's Will only if paired with mana acceleration or draw. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple Ruby Medallions, or expensive spells without acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap red spell if available to start storm count and generate mana. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Ruby Medallion if possible, and continue sequencing cheap spells to build mana and storm. Prioritize untapped lands and fetching basics to maximize Ruby Medallion synergy.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Sneak & Show, Ocelot Pride): Bring in Lightning Axe, Flame Slash, and Pyroclasm for board control. Vs Control/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in additional Jeska's Will, extra Seething Songs, and spot removal to protect the combo. Vs Combo: Bring in additional recursion and win conditions. Vs Mirror: Bring in extra Jeska's Will and cheap spells to out-value sequencing.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Track storm count meticulously; every spell cast this turn increases your win condition's power. Manage mana to ensure you can cast your combo turn without leaving yourself vulnerable. Use Jeska's Will to recycle key pieces or generate extra turns, but avoid overcommitting to recursion if you can win outright.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting expensive spells early unless you have a clear path to combo. Commit threats only when you can protect them or when you're ready to go for the win. Use spot removal to clear key blockers or interaction, but prioritize assembling your combo over trading.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard, or board wipes. Note when opponents tap out for interaction or hold up mana. Track graveyard contents if they have recursion. Monitor mana acceleration from Tron or ramp decks, and watch for Delver/Tempo threats that can disrupt your sequencing.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-storm win conditions (e.g., Tendrils of Agony, Firemind's Research) or Jeska's Will chains that cast multiple win conditions or generate extra turns. Win by casting a high-storm spell when you have enough count, or by using Jeska's Will to bypass interaction and close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough storm count to win, or when you can safely cast Jeska's Will to generate extra turns/recursion. Commit when the opponent has no interaction left, is tapped out, or when you can protect your combo with spot removal or timing.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land, have no draw spells, and the opponent has a clear path to win. Concede if you're facing a hard counter to your combo with no sideboard answers, or if you're out of resources and the opponent is ahead on board with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Jeska's Will to cast spells in response to counters, or chain cheap spells to out-value counters. Cast win conditions at the end of the opponent's turn or when they have no mana. Use recursion to bypass counters and win before they can respond.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap spells to disrupt their mana base. Use spot removal to kill key mana sources. Go for the combo quickly before they stabilize, and use Jeska's Will to generate extra turns if needed to close the game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use spot removal (Lightning Axe, Flame Slash) to clear threats and stabilize. Don't overextend; focus on assembling the combo. Use Jeska's Will to generate extra turns if you're behind on board, and prioritize survival over aggression.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap spells to force interaction and exhaust their counterspells. Use Jeska's Will to out-value their removal and generate extra turns. Go for the combo when they tap out for counterspells or when they have no interaction left.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Out-value them with better sequencing and storm count management. Use Jeska's Will to generate extra turns or recursion. Prioritize protecting your combo and use spot removal to clear their threats. Win by going off first or by out-recursing with Jeska's Will chains.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize adding interaction (spot removal, discard if available), extra recursion (Jeska's Will), and win condition consistency. Against control, bring in additional draw and protection. Against aggro, bring in board wipes or spot removal. Always keep Ruby Medallion and Jeska's Will in the main deck, and adjust based on metagame trends.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to board wipes, forgetting to count storm, mismanaging mana for Jeska's Will recursion, and not tracking opponent interaction. Unique interactions: Jeska's Will can be cast from the graveyard with certain effects, allowing extra turns. Ruby Medallion makes red spells cheaper, enabling more storm count. Always prioritize untapped lands for Ruby Medallion synergy, and remember that Jeska's Will can be used to cast multiple win conditions in a single turn if sequenced correctly.

philosophy: Sultai Tempo disrupts the opponent's early game with efficient spot removal and hand interaction, then establishes a board presence with high-impact, low-cost threats while out-drawing them through card selection. It excels against combo and aggressive decks that rely on uninterrupted development, while struggling against highly resilient midrange or heavy control strategies that can out-value its fragile early threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land, a turn-1 disruption or removal (e.g., Fatal Push, Thoughtseize), and a turn-2 threat or card draw. Mulligan hands with two or more lands, or hands lacking a turn-1 play and a turn-2 threat/draw. Prioritize keeping hands that can cast Ponder or Brainstorm on turn 2 to dig for consistency. Ensure at least two sources of colored mana for early spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast disruption (Fatal Push on aggressive threats, Thoughtseize on key combo pieces or removal). Turn 2: Play a land, cast Ponder/Brainstorm to filter into a threat or removal, then play a threat or another disruption. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/shock lands or duals that enter untapped if possible, but don't delay casting turn-1 disruption for a fetch. Always keep a land drop available for turn 2.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo: Bring in more spot removal, discard, and early interaction (e.g., Fatal Push, Thoughtseize, Surgical Extraction). vs Control/Ramp: Bring in more card draw, resilient threats, and hate cards (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, additional draw spells). vs Mirror: Bring in additional disruption and card advantage, plus hate for opposing threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve hand for key turns; don't overcommit to board if opponent has unanswered interaction. Use Ponder/Brainstorm to find answers to immediate threats or finishers. Manage graveyard by using Surgical Extraction or Rest in Peace if opponent relies on it. Don't overextend into sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal for their interaction or when they've blown through answers. Hold back if they have potential sweepers or if you're below 10 life. Trade efficiently: use removal on their most impactful threat, even if it's a 'small' creature, to maintain board control.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), counterspells, and graveyard recursion. Note when they tap out for a single spell; that's your window to push damage or play a free threat. Track their hand size after discard spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact mid-game threats (e.g., 4/4 or 5/5 creatures, or a combination of smaller threats that outvalue their blockers). Win by out-resourcing them through card draw and efficient removal, then closing with unblockable or resilient threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, they have no sweepers, and you can trade efficiently. Also commit when you're at low life and need to race, but only if your threats are faster than theirs.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land, have no removal, and they have a board of unblockable threats or a combo win condition. Also concede if you're consistently drawing dead hands against a resilient midrange deck with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig for alternative paths or cheap threats. Cast spells in response to their counterspells if you have protection or can trade up. Don't overcommit to a single spell; spread threats across turns.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use early disruption to slow their mana development. Prioritize removal on their mana rocks or early threats. Bring in hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline) if they rely on graveyard or specific engines.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and discard. Don't overextend; trade efficiently. Use your early interaction to buy time for your threats to come online.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats and discard their hand. Force them to use removal on low-impact creatures. Out-value them with card draw and resilient threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the hand war. Prioritize Thoughtseize/brainstorm to find answers. Trade threats efficiently. Control the board with removal. The player who manages hand and board better wins.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against slower decks, increase card draw and resilient threats. Against faster decks, increase early interaction and discard. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and consider bringing in basic lands if needed.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without considering sweepers. Unique interaction: Ponder/Brainstorm can be used to dig for specific answers or threats, but don't cast them just to cast them; always have a plan. Graveyard hate is crucial against recursion decks. Always track opponent's hand size after discard spells.

philosophy: Dimir Ninjas is a tempo-value archetype that leverages flash, combat tricks, and unblockable damage to disrupt opponents while steadily trading up in resources. It aims to out-value midrange and control decks by turning every combat into a favorable exchange, while using hand disruption to neutralize combo and aggressive strategies before they stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a disruption spell (Thoughtseize/Duress), and a Ninja or combat trick. Mulligan to lands if you have 0-1, or to disruption/Ninjas if you have 2+ lands. Hands with only creatures and no lands or tricks are poor.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Duress on key threats or combo pieces. Turn 2: Play a land, flash in a Ninja if the opponent has no removal, or hold disruption for their turn. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast disruption on turn 1 and a Ninja/trick on turn 2.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro/Combo (Delver, S&S): Bring in more disruption (Duress, Thoughtseize), targeted removal (Tasigur's Contempt, Drown in the Loch), and early blockers. Vs Control/Midrange (Tempo, Tron, Ocelot): Bring in more value engines, extra Ninjas, and card draw. Vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and hand disruption to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve hand disruption for critical turns. Don't overcommit to the board if you lack tricks. Use combat tricks to trade up favorably. Manage mana carefully to maintain flash priority for opponent's turns.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or tricks to protect them, or when the board is clear. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Flash in Ninjas to probe for answers before committing fully.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Drown in the Loch), and combo win conditions. Note when the opponent taps out for a big spell or holds up mana for protection.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Unblocked Ninjas with combat tricks, value engines like Kagemaro, or direct damage. Win by trading favorably and closing with unblockable damage or card advantage engines.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have hand control, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can consistently trade up. Commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns or when you've out-valued them enough to weather a counterattack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has a hard board wipe, you have no removal or tricks, and they are ahead on board. Also concede if you are facing a combo that has already resolved its win condition or if you are completely out of resources.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats, flash in during the opponent's turn, and rely on unblockable damage to bypass counters. Use discard to remove counterspells from hand before they can be cast.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early disruption to hit lands or key spells. Flash in threats to pressure before they ramp out. Use discard to delay their game plan and stabilize with efficient trades.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early disruption, then use Ninjas to trade efficiently. Don't overextend; focus on surviving turns 3-4 and out-value them with combat tricks and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with flash threats and use tricks to bypass removal. Out-value them with card advantage and don't let them stabilize with sweepers. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by discarding their tricks and Ninjas. Trade favorably, sequence your plays to maximize value, and target their hand disruption first. Control the board with better trick usage and flash timing.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw disruption to a balanced mix of targeted removal and value engines. Against control and midrange, prioritize card advantage and late-game finishers. Against aggro and combo, double down on early disruption and early-game creatures. Always keep at least one hate card or land destruction if the format supports it.
notes: Note that Yuriko is Commander-only, Orcish Bowmasters is not a Ninja, and Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student is not a real card; base your decklist on legal Dimir Ninjas like Ninja of the Deep Hours, Kagemaro, Shuriken, and Kusari-Gama. Flash priority is crucial, so always keep mana open for opponent's turns. Combat tricks are your primary value engine; never attack without them unless you are racing or probing. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection, as Ninjas are vulnerable to sweepers and targeted removal.

philosophy: Stiflenought is a control-tempo archetype that leverages targeted counters, graveyard hate, and discard pressure to neutralize opponent resources before deploying Phyrexian Dreadnought as an unanswerable win condition. It excels against combo and midrange strategies that rely on recursion or high-cost spells, while struggling against fast aggro and heavy artifact/ramp decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (prioritizing fetch/shock or duals if available), Daze, and Nulldrifter. Keep hands with Daze + a land + a discard outlet. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, single lands, or only Dreadnought without discard outlets or interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. If opponent casts a spell, Daze it. If they don't, hold for Nulldrifter on turn 2. Turn 2: Drop Nulldrifter if possible, or play a land and hold Daze. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Nulldrifter on turn 2 or Daze on turn 1. Prioritize untapped lands that enter quickly.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2 Path to Exile, +1 Surgical Extraction, -2 Nulldrifter. vs Control/Combo: +2 Nulldrifter, +1 Daze, -2 Dreadnought. vs Mirror: +2 Nulldrifter, +1 Daze, -2 Dreadnought.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Daze is your primary interaction; use it on key spells, not just anything. Nulldrifter shuts down recursion, so don't tap it for attacks unless necessary. Dreadnought requires discarding; manage your hand to ensure you have enough cards to cast it without leaving yourself defenseless. Graveyard is your friend for Dreadnought but an enemy for recursion; use Nulldrifter to protect your board.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Nulldrifter is a tempo play that disrupts opponent's game plan. Commit it early to shut down recursion. Dreadnought is a finisher; only cast it when you can protect it or when the board is clear enough to trade. Hold back Daze for game-ending threats or to protect your Dreadnought.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard recursion (e.g., Reanimate, Flashback), artifact abilities (Nulldrifter handles this), and counterspells. Key tell: Opponent tapping out on turn 3-4 with a graveyard full of cards usually means they're setting up a combo or midrange win.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phyrexian Dreadnought is the primary win condition. Cast it when you have board control, opponent has no removal, or you can protect it with Nulldrifter. Secondary win: Nulldrifter can win by attrition if opponent relies on abilities.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Nulldrifter on board, opponent's graveyard is empty or irrelevant, and you have enough mana to cast Dreadnought. Also commit when opponent has no removal or counterspells left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no early interaction, if you're out of mana and hand against a control deck with a locked board, or if opponent has multiple removal spells for Dreadnought and you have no way to protect it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Daze to counter their counters. Cast Dreadnought in response to their removal, or use Nulldrifter to shut down their ability-based answers. Sequence spells to force counterspells on non-critical threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Nulldrifter is key to stopping artifact ramp. Use Daze to counter their mana rocks or finishers. Dreadnought can outvalue their early game if you survive turn 3.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Mulligan for early interaction (Daze). Use Nulldrifter to stop token generators or pump spells. Dreadnought is too slow; focus on surviving until turn 4-5 with Nulldrifter and Daze.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with Nulldrifter's disruption. Dreadnought is hard to answer if you discard efficiently. Use Daze to protect your threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Nulldrifter is king. Trade Nulldrifters if necessary. Daze war of attrition. Dreadnought wins if you can discard efficiently and protect it.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Generally, bring in more interaction against control/combo, more removal against aggro, and keep Nulldrifter/Daze as staples. Dreadnought is situational; side it out against fast decks.
notes: Dreadnought's discard cost means you must manage your hand carefully. Nulldrifter stops all abilities, including your own if you have any. Daze's life payment is a double-edged sword; don't life-pay unless necessary. Common mistake: Casting Dreadnought without board control, leading to easy removal. Unique interaction: Nulldrifter + Dreadnought is a classic "unblockable/unanswerable" threat if opponent relies on abilities or counters.

philosophy: Dimir Death’s Shadow is a life-loss-powered aggro-control hybrid that uses discard to protect a massively scaling threat, aiming to out-value and out-pressure control, tempo, and midrange decks while stabilizing against aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands, at least one Thoughtseize, and two sources of life loss (e.g., Dark Confidant, Moonshadow, Blood Crypt, or similar). Keep hands with Death’s Shadow + 2+ life loss outlets + discard. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no life loss outlets, or only one land with no early interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize (target removal, ramp, or early threat). Turn 2: Play a land, cast Dark Confidant or Moonshadow to establish life loss. If Death’s Shadow is in hand and you have 2+ life loss sources, cast it on turn 2 or 3. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast key spells at instant speed when needed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2/0 Surgical Strike, +1/1 Drown in the Loch, +1/1 Go Blank. vs Control/Tempo: +2/0 Surgical Strike, +2/0 Drown in the Loch, +1/1 Thoughtseize. vs Combo: +2/0 Rest in Peace (or equivalent graveyard hate), +1/1 Go Blank. vs Mirror: +2/0 Surgical Strike, +1/1 Drown in the Loch, +1/1 Go Blank.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize life loss outlets over card draw once Death’s Shadow is on board. Use Thoughtseize to remove opponent’s interaction rather than redundant threats. Manage your life total carefully—never drop below 1 unless you can immediately stabilize or win.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Death’s Shadow only when you have consistent life loss (2+ sources) and opponent has no immediate sweepers or targeted removal in hand. Hold back if they show signs of a board wipe or heavy interaction. Use discard to disrupt their development rather than trading creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for threats versus holding up interaction, and track their life total to calculate Death’s Shadow’s power accurately.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Death’s Shadow (often 13+ power), Moonshadow, or a late-game Dark Confidant. Close the game by attacking with Shadow while using discard to remove blockers or protection, or by out-valuing them with card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 2+ life loss sources, opponent has no sweepers or removal in hand, and you can reasonably close the game in 1–2 turns. Also commit when you can discard their only answer to Shadow.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if opponent has a hard lock (e.g., exile effects, empty-the-board + graveyard hate), you’re at 1 life with no life loss outlets or answers, or you’re facing a combo that will resolve before you can stabilize.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells. Cast Shadow at end step or when they’re tapped out. Leverage Moonshadow’s abilities to protect your plays or value out their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseize for mana dorks, ramp spells, or extra lands. Apply early pressure to force them to interact before they stabilize. Use Surgical Strike to remove key threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early Thoughtseize to remove early threats or life gain. Use Moonshadow or Confidant to stabilize and drain them. Surgical Strike key creatures. Avoid overextending into combat traps.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Shadow to force interaction. Use discard to remove removal, sweepers, or win conditions. Out-value them with card advantage from Confidant/Moonshadow and close the game before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by prioritizing life loss outlets and Shadow. Surgical Strike their Shadow or key interaction. Go Blank to disrupt their life loss engine. Track life totals carefully to out-value them in combat.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift toward a more control-oriented game plan by prioritizing hate cards and interaction. Adjust land counts if the metagame demands more basics or duals. Focus on protecting Shadow, disrupting opponent interaction, and adapting to the specific threats you face.
notes: Common mistakes include casting Shadow too early without enough life loss, overcommitting to Dark Confidant without protection, and mismanaging your life total. Moonshadow can serve as both a life loss outlet and a protective tool—use it flexibly. Always calculate your life total before combat to avoid accidental loss. Format legality of specific cards may vary, so adjust land and spell counts accordingly.

philosophy: A hybrid midrange-aggro strategy that leverages Green Sun's Zenith to efficiently deploy high-impact threats and disruption, while using Cabal Therapy to control the early game and Veteran Explorer to close out combat. It aims to out-value opponents through superior card quality and hand disruption, winning against control, combo, and midrange decks by dictating the pace of the game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Green Sun's Zenith, Cabal Therapy, and at least one early threat (Tarmogoyf, Scavenging Ooze, Veteran Explorer, or Bloodbraid Elf). Prioritize hands that can cast a 1-mana spell on turn 1 and a 2-mana spell on turn 2. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of Therapy without threats, or hands lacking early interaction/pressure.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Cabal Therapy targeting opponent's hand (discard a key disruption or combo piece) or cast a 1-mana threat like Tarmogoyf/Scavenging Ooze. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Green Sun's Zenith to tutor for a 2-3 mana threat (Veteran Explorer, Bloodbraid Elf, or Thalia's Guardian) or additional disruption. Sequence lands to ensure double-colored mana availability for Zenith and threats; fetch lands should be used to find shock lands or duals that enter untapped if possible.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control/combo: Bring in Rest in Peace, Thalia's Guardian, Surgical Extraction, and additional discard (Thoughtseize/Inquisition). Against aggro: Bring in Rest in Peace, Thalia's Guardian, Surgical Extraction, and discard. Against mirror: Bring in additional discard, combat tricks (Lightning Axe, Giant Growth), and hate bears. Remove Green Sun's Zenith, Cabal Therapy, and redundant threats as needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Green Sun's Zenith as a premium resource; only cast it when you can guarantee a game-winning or game-swinging threat. Use Cabal Therapy early to disrupt opponent's hand, saving it for key turns. Manage your graveyard by avoiding unnecessary self-mill and using Surgical Extraction if needed. Keep a hand of 3-4 cards to maintain flexibility against disruption.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no sweepers or has exhausted their removal. Hold back if they have potential board wipes (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods) or multiple targeted removal spells. Use Veteran Explorer's pump ability to trade favorably or push lethal. Don't overextend into known sweepers; play one threat at a time to bait removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Force of Will, Daze, Spell Pierce) and targeted removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Axe). Track opponent's hand size and discarded cards to anticipate their game plan. Note if they're playing lands that suggest ramp (Tron) or combo (Sneak and Show). Pay attention to when they tap out for spells, indicating vulnerability to pressure.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Veteran Explorer (often pumped to 3/3 or 4/4), Tarmogoyf, and Bloodbraid Elf. Secure victory by applying consistent pressure, using combat tricks to bypass removal, and leveraging Green Sun's Zenith to find a late-game finisher if needed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no sweepers, has used their removal on earlier threats, or is at low life. Commit multiple threats if you can trade favorably or if you have combat tricks to protect your board. Push for lethal with Veteran Explorer's pump ability when opponent has no blockers or removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face multiple sweepers with no removal or disruption to break them, if you're out of resources and opponent is stabilizing with a board wipe, or if you're facing a fast combo with no interaction. Also concede if you're on the draw against a control deck that has locked you out with counterspells and you have no way to break through.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple threats to force through, cast Green Sun's Zenith on your turn to bypass counters, and use Cabal Therapy to discard counterspells. If possible, tutor for Rest in Peace or Thalia's Guardian to lock them out. Use combat tricks to trade favorably if a threat is countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their mana acceleration. Use Cabal Therapy to discard mana dorks or key ramp spells. Bring in Rest in Peace and Thalia's Guardian to neutralize their advantage. Use Green Sun's Zenith to find hate bears or additional disruption.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early threats to race, Cabal Therapy to disrupt key creatures, and Green Sun's Zenith to find Rest in Peace or Thalia's Guardian. Play defensively, holding back threats to block efficiently. Use Veteran Explorer's pump ability to trade favorably in combat. Don't overcommit if they have multiple small creatures.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats to force them to use removal or counterspells. Use Cabal Therapy to disrupt their hand, targeting counterspells and key spells. Use Green Sun's Zenith to find answers or finishers. Push for damage when they tap out for spells, and use combat tricks to bypass removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is key; use Cabal Therapy to disrupt their Green Sun's Zenith or key threats. Track their hand for combat tricks and discard them. Use Veteran Explorer's pump ability to trade favorably. Don't overextend; play one threat at a time to bait removal. Use Green Sun's Zenith to find a superior threat or hate bear when they commit.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize hate cards against control and combo (Rest in Peace, Thalia's Guardian, Surgical Extraction). Against aggro, focus on disruption and early threats. In the mirror, bring in additional discard and combat tricks to win hand and combat wars. Remove redundant cards like extra Green Sun's Zenith or Cabal Therapy if the meta shifts. Always keep at least one Zenith and one Therapy unless facing a specific hate deck.
notes: Green Sun's Zenith is a one-sided tutor, so know your topdecks and what you're looking for. Cabal Therapy's downside is manageable early but can be a liability late; use it wisely. Veteran Explorer's pump ability is crucial in combat and should be saved for favorable trades or lethal. Avoid overcommitting to the board without assessing sweepers. Practice hand management to avoid being out of resources against control. Remember that Nic Fit is a value engine; don't rush plays that don't advance your game plan.

philosophy: Dimir Control seeks to disrupt the opponent’s early game with targeted discard, stabilize through efficient counterspells and spot removal, and close out games by out-grinding opponents with card advantage engines. It excels against combo and midrange archetypes by shutting down their game plans and leveraging late-game spell density to overwhelm them.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Thoughtseize, and a mix of removal/counterspells. Drop to 6 if you have 4+ lands with no early interaction, or 3+ draw spells with zero answers. Prioritize keeping hands that can cast Thoughtseize on turn 1 and have a land to cast it on.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Thoughtseize against Tron (target Lotus Cobra/Ancient Tomb), Sneak and Show (target Phyrexian Revoker or graveyard), or Ocelot Pride (target mana accelerants). Turn 2: Play a land, then cast Brainstorm or a spot removal/counter. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Thoughtseize on turn 1, then develop your mana base. Use Brainstorm to dig for answers or set up your graveyard for Snapcaster Mage/Flow State.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Delver/Ocelot): +4 Daze/Spell Pierce, +3 Fatal Push/Go for the Throat, -2 Flow State. Against Control/Tron: +3 Rest in Peace/Leyline of the Void, +2 Snapcaster Mage, +2 Flow State, -2 removal. Against Combo (Sneak): +3 Leyline of the Void/Rest in Peace, +2 Surgical Extraction, +2 counterspells, -2 discard. Against Mirror: +2 counterspells, +1 graveyard hate, adjust based on sideboard games.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between interaction and threat development. Avoid overcommitting to Flow State unless you have a clear win condition or protection. Use Brainstorm/Preordain to cycle into answers or set up your graveyard for Snapcaster Mage. Track opponent’s graveyard for combo pieces and Snapcaster targets.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing large threats if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use counterspells to protect key spells (Flow State, Snapcaster, or finishers) and disrupt opponent’s combos. Trade efficiently: use spot removal on early threats, counterspells on game-ending spells, and discard to disrupt hand integrity.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron’s mana rock drops, Sneak and Show’s graveyard setup, Delver’s flip condition, and Ocelot’s mana acceleration. Key tells include opponents playing multiple lands without casting spells, casting discard to protect combo pieces, or holding up mana for sweepers/counterspells. Track their graveyard for Phyrexian Revoker, Snapcaster, or combo enablers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Flow State (storm count > 10) is the primary win condition, often followed by Snapcaster Mage to replay key spells or draw into game-ending answers. Efficient threats like Thoughtseize into a board wipe or spot removal can also close out games if the opponent is low on resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough spells in hand/graveyard to cast Flow State, have protection (counterspell/removal), and the opponent has no clear answer. Also commit when you can safely cast Snapcaster Mage to replay a key spell or draw into a win.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re at 1 life with no answers, the opponent has a guaranteed win condition (e.g., Tron with 10 mana, Sneak with graveyard ready), or you’re completely out of resources in a bad matchup. Don’t bluff Flow State without a clear path to win or protection.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells to split the stack, cast Flow State in response to their counter, or use Thoughtseize to remove their counterspells. Brainstorm to dig for answers or protection. If they have heavy counter magic, pivot to discard and spot removal to grind them down.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early disruption with Thoughtseize to target mana rocks or key creatures. Use spot removal for early threats and counterspells for finishers. Flow State can out-value ramp if you have enough spells, but prioritize shutting down their early acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early discard, spot removal, and counterspells. Don’t overcommit to Flow State early; focus on surviving until you can stabilize. Once stabilized, close out games with threats or Flow State if you have enough spells.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with card draw and Thoughtseize to disrupt their hand. Protect your win conditions with counterspells and spot removal. Flow State can out-value control if you can cast it safely, but prioritize trading resources efficiently and tracking their hand for sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by targeting their counterspells, discard, and key threats. Protect your Flow State and Snapcaster Mage with counterspells. Track their graveyard for Snapcaster targets and use counterspells to protect your key spells. Sideboard games often come down to who manages resources and graveyard better.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite based on the metagame. Against control and combo, prioritize graveyard hate and counterspells to disrupt their game plans. Against aggro, bring in spot removal and early interaction to stabilize. Keep Thoughtseize and Brainstorm as staples, and adjust your land count if you’re consistently mana-screwed or flooding. Always track opponent’s sideboard cards to anticipate their adjustments and adapt your play accordingly.
notes: Flow State is highly vulnerable to discard and counterspells; never cast it blindly without protection or a clear win condition. Brainstorm and Preordain are crucial for setting up your graveyard and digging for answers. Mind the graveyard for Snapcaster Mage and opponent combo pieces. Common mistake: Overcommitting to Flow State without enough spells or protection, leading to dead mana and lost games. Always track opponent’s hand and graveyard to make informed decisions.

philosophy: Mono-blue Merfolk leverages Aether Vial for tempo and mana efficiency while flooding the board with synergistic threats to overwhelm opponents. It wins by racing midrange, disrupting combo, and out-scaling control through relentless pressure and conditional counterspells.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, Aether Vial, and at least two 1-mana Merfolk or Vodalian Hexcatcher. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands, no Vial, or only expensive lords without early threats. Prioritize hands that can cast Vial or a 1-drop on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Aether Vial or a 1-mana Merfolk. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another 1-drop or use Vial to put a Lord or Hexcatcher into play. Sequence lands to ensure blue mana is available for Hexcatcher and Vial activation.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Dimir Tempo/Tron: Bring in additional counterspells, graveyard hate, and faster clock threats. vs Sneak and Show: Bring in graveyard hate, extra counters, and removal. vs Izzet Delver/Ocelot Pride: Bring in early interaction, more lords, and sweepers. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and additional lords to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Aether Vial as a tempo engine; sequence it to protect threats or bypass counterspells. Don't overcommit hand resources—trade cheap Merfolk for value and save interaction for key spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have Vial or Hexcatcher ready to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, heavy counterspells, or can stabilize with a single play.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Dimir's counterspells and removal, Tron's mana rocks and finishers, Sneak & Show's graveyard combo, Delver's flip threats, and Ocelot's midrange value. Key tells: opponent tapping for multiple colors, graveyard activity, or holding up mana past turn 3.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lord of Atlantis with a full board of Merfolk, or a Vial-sent Hexcatcher/Lord into combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have board presence, opponent is at low life, or you've disrupted their key interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing Tron with no interaction and they have 3+ mana rocks, or if you're down to a dead hand against Sneak & Show with no graveyard hate.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Aether Vial to cast threats in response to counterspells, or sequence around them. Use Hexcatcher if you have another Merfolk.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use counterspells to hit their mana rocks or finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with counterspells and early lords. Don't overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Vial-sent threats. Use Hexcatcher to protect your key spells. Out-value them with card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize lords and interaction. Vial into lords to break stalemates.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw aggression to interaction and value. Add more counterspells against control, more pressure against combo, and more lords/removal against midrange. Adjust land count if needed.
notes: Aether Vial synergy is key; practice sequencing. Don't overcommit to the board without protection. Watch for graveyard hate in combo matchups. Lord of Atlantis is a clock and a tool; use its tap ability strategically.

philosophy: Mono-white Mystic Forge Combo aims to generate exponential mana and card advantage by repeatedly cycling artifacts through Mystic Forge and The One Ring, out-resourcing opponents through artifact synergy and card selection. It wins by assembling a win condition (commander damage, combat, or a specific finisher) after stabilizing and drawing through the deck.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one mana artifact (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or similar), and Mystic Forge or The One Ring. Mulligan if you have no artifact acceleration, no way to untap artifacts, or more than 3 lands with zero early plays. Prioritize hands that can cast Mystic Forge by turn 2-3 or The One Ring by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap mana artifact. Turn 2: Cast Mystic Forge if possible, or another mana rock. Turn 3-4: Cast The One Ring and begin cycling artifacts. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast key pieces without flooding. If you draw The One Ring early, prioritize casting it to start generating mana and cycling your hand.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro: Bring in protection spells, early removal, and board wipes. Against Control/Ramp: Bring in artifact destruction, counters, and faster win conditions. Against Tempo/Mirror: Bring in additional card draw, protection, and redundant combo pieces. Against Combo: Bring in hate cards, graveyard hate, and board wipes. Always maintain a balance between interaction and combo density.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always keep enough untapped for interaction. Use Mystic Forge to cycle your hand and find answers or win conditions. Don't overcommit to drawing if you need to hold up mana for Kozilek's Command or protection. Prioritize untapping artifacts for The One Ring's draw trigger when you have excess mana.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to the win condition or can protect them with protection spells. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or targeted removal. Use Kozilek's Command to bounce threats or remove key permanents. Avoid overextending into known sweepers; wait for opponents to commit resources first.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, etc.), targeted removal on your combo pieces, and opponent's mana development. Note if they're playing counterspells or artifact hate. Track how many cards they have in hand and whether they're going for a specific combo. Pay attention to when they tap out for interaction versus when they're holding up for protection.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Commander damage, combat via a large creature, or a specific artifact combo (e.g., infinite mana into a win condition). Assemble The One Ring + Mystic Forge to draw your deck and find the win. Use Kozilek's Command to bounce blockers or remove key defenders.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to protect your win condition, have removed key blockers, or when opponents are tapped out. If you can generate infinite mana/turns, commit immediately. Don't wait for perfect conditions if you can close out the game before opponents can respond.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (Stax, empty the Warrens, etc.), have no way to break it, or are facing a combo that will win before you can assemble yours. Also concede if your hand is dead against a specific matchup (e.g., no answers to a fast aggro) and you have no comeback plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats if possible, or cast spells during your opponent's turn. Use Kozilek's Command to bounce counterspells or protect your combo pieces. Overload with multiple threats to force them to spend multiple counters. Prioritize casting Mystic Forge and The One Ring first to establish card advantage.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use artifact destruction, and race them with your own acceleration. Don't let them stabilize; use Kozilek's Command to remove key ramp pieces. Prioritize finding your win condition quickly rather than engaging in a long game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction, protection spells, and board wipes. Use Kozilek's Command to bounce creatures. Stabilize with card draw and a fast win condition. Don't overcommit to drawing cards if you need to hold up for removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats, use Kozilek's Command to bypass counterspells, and assemble your combo quickly. Don't give them time to answer; go off as soon as possible. Use The One Ring to draw through their answers and find your win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value card advantage and resource denial. Use Kozilek's Command to bounce their combo pieces. Prioritize protecting your own Mystic Forge and One Ring. Win by out-resourcing and finding the win condition first. Track their hand size and mana to know when they're vulnerable to targeted removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, bring in more interaction and faster win conditions. Against aggro, prioritize protection and early removal. In mirrors, bring in redundant combo pieces and hate cards. Always keep a balance between interaction and combo density, and ensure you have answers to the most common threats in your specific playgroup or tournament environment.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to drawing cards without securing the win condition, forgetting to untap artifacts for Kozilek's Command, and not tracking opponent's mana. Unique interactions: The One Ring can be used with Mystic Forge to draw multiple cards per turn. Kozilek's Command's bounce mode can be used to recycle artifacts for additional triggers. Always be mindful of timing and stack management when cycling artifacts. If playing in a format with sideboards, prioritize keeping your core combo pieces while swapping in matchup-specific hate.

philosophy: 8-Cast is a fast mana and cantrip engine designed to cast a high-impact win condition or combo off as early as possible, typically by turn 3-4. It aims to outpace resource-heavy and control-oriented decks by generating explosive mana and drawing into its finisher before opponents can stabilize or answer.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with at least two fast mana sources (Lotus Petal, Mox Opal, Chrome Mox, etc.), one cantrip (Thoughtcast, Ponder, etc.), and one land. Keep hands with a win condition plus two mana sources. Mulligan hands with three or more lands, hands with only fast mana and no cantrips/land, or hands with a win condition but no way to cast it by turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and a fast mana piece, or two fast mana pieces if you have a land in hand. Turn 2: Cast a cantrip to dig for your win condition or additional acceleration. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your win condition by turn 3-4, prioritizing untapped lands that support your mana base. If you draw into a win condition early, consider holding up interaction if the meta favors disruption.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (e.g., Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride): Bring in countermagic, discard spells, and fast removal to stabilize. Against Control (e.g., Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in additional cantrips, card advantage, and protection spells to protect your combo. Against Combo (e.g., Sneak and Show): Bring in graveyard hate, discard, and disruption to disrupt their game plan. Against Mirror: Bring in additional disruption, card draw, and specific hate to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize drawing into your win condition over developing a wide board. Manage your graveyard carefully if your win condition is vulnerable to discard or sweepers. Keep mana open for protection or interaction when facing disruptive opponents.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can close the game in one turn. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered interaction or a sweeper in play. Use cantrips to scout for the right moment to go off.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and discard spells. Note when the opponent taps out for interaction, as this is your window to cast your win condition. Track graveyard contents if your win condition is vulnerable to graveyard hate.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Your win condition (typically a high-impact spell or combo like Empty the Warrens or a Storm finisher). Accelerate mana with fast sources and cantrip to draw into it, then cast it when you have protection or the opponent has no interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast your win condition, can protect it (or the opponent has no counters), and you can close the game in one turn. Also commit when the opponent is tapped out for interaction or has no sweepers in play.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you are down to a dead hand with no way to cast your win condition, the opponent has a hard lock (e.g., mass discard, counterspells, or a sweeper), or you are facing a matchup where you have less than a 10% win rate post-sideboard with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple targets or cheap alternatives to your win condition. Cantrip into protection spells or use redundant acceleration to cast around counters. If possible, go off in response to their counterspells to force a trade.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use discard or counters to remove key ramp pieces. If they stabilize, out-grind them with card advantage and protect your win condition.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with countermagic, discard, or fast removal to survive the early turns. Once stabilized, pivot to your combo or win condition. Do not overcommit to board development at the expense of your game plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with cantrips and card advantage to dig through their interaction. Protect your win condition with counters or discard. If they have a sweeper, wait for them to use it on a threat, then go off.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by out-drawing and out-disrupting. Protect your win condition with counters or discard. Use disruption to remove their key pieces. Prioritize drawing into your win condition over developing a board.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to the specific matchups in your metagame. Generally, increase disruption and protection against control and combo, add interaction and stabilization tools against aggro, and keep the core fast mana and cantrip engine intact. Avoid overloading the sideboard with situational cards that dilute your primary game plan.
notes: Common mistakes include mulliganing too aggressively (losing your fast mana engine), overcommitting to the board instead of digging for your win condition, and mismanaging fast mana sequencing (e.g., playing a land before a fast mana piece when you need to cast a cantrip). Unique interactions: Thoughtcast synergizes with cheap spells to draw cards and generate mana, while Mox Opal requires artifacts to function, so ensure your mana base supports it. Always track the opponent's graveyard and hand size to anticipate their interaction.

philosophy: Stax is a resource-denial archetype that taxes opponents' mana and card advantage while maintaining its own through cheap artifacts and recursion. It aims to grind out attrition-based wins against acceleration, combo, and tempo decks by forcing them into impossible resource deficits and out-valuing them over the long game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches if applicable), plus at least one tax effect (Trinisphere, Thalia, Winter Orb) and a mana source (Mox Diamond, Grim Monolith, or basic lands). Prioritize hands that can establish a tax floor by turn 2-3. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only expensive spells without acceleration, or hands missing both tax effects and mana rocks.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Diamond or Grim Monolith if mana allows. Turn 2: Drop Trinisphere or Thalia if available; otherwise, continue mana development. If you have Grim Monolith, consider tapping it for mana on turn 2 to cast a second tax effect or a disruption piece. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your tax effects by turn 2-3 without stalling.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Tron/Sneak and Show: Bring in Rest in Peace, Wasteland, and additional tax effects. Against Dimir Tempo/Izzet Delver: Bring in Thalia, Winter Orb, and artifact hate if needed. Against Ocelot Pride Midrange: Bring in Rest in Peace, Wasteland, and additional disruption. Against mirror: Bring in additional tax effects and hate pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your most valuable resource. Use Grim Monolith strategically to cast multiple tax effects or disruption in a single turn. Mox Diamond requires careful sequencing to avoid dead mana. Keep cards in hand to respond to key threats, but don't hoard if you can stabilize. Graveyard is a resource for you (via fetch lands or recursion) but a liability for opponents (Rest in Peace).
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have established a tax floor. Hold back if you can't protect your key pieces (Trinisphere/Thalia). Prioritize keeping your tax effects on the board over aggressive creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana acceleration (Tron, Sneak and Show), card draw engines (Dimir Tempo, Ocelot Pride), and graveyard recursion. Key tells: Opponent tapping extra lands, casting spells from exile/graveyard, or holding up mana for counterspells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Out-grinding via tax effects, or using Knight of the Reliquary to fetch a land for mana, or casting a large spell with Grim Monolith. Sometimes a resilient creature like Knight of the Reliquary or a win con like Empty the Warrens (if in the list) closes it out.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you've established a permanent tax floor (Trinisphere + Thalia/Winter Orb) and the opponent has no way to remove your key pieces.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a combo deck that can win before turn 4 and you have no disruption, or if you're dead to hate pieces (Ensnaring Bridge, Artifact Mutation) with no way to recover.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target tax effects. Cast spells in response to counterspells if possible, or use mana rocks to cast around them. Trinisphere makes counterspells expensive.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Rest in Peace, Wasteland, and Winter Orb are key. Tax their mana with Trinisphere/Thalia. Don't let them establish a mana base.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Thalia and Winter Orb slow them down. Use Wasteland to disrupt their land base. Out-grind with resilient artifacts.
threat_responses.against_control: Trinisphere and Thalia make their removal/counterspells too expensive. Use Grim Monolith to cast multiple spells in a turn. Out-value them with card advantage denial.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race to establish tax effects. Prioritize keeping Trinisphere and Thalia. Use Wasteland to disrupt their mana. Win by having more resilient artifacts or better sequencing.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your hate pieces to the metagame. Against combo/ramp, prioritize Rest in Peace and Wasteland. Against control/tempo, bring in Thalia and Winter Orb. Always keep at least one tax effect and a mana source. Adjust based on opponent's specific strategy.
notes: Stax is a fragile deck if your key pieces are removed. Protect Trinisphere and Thalia at all costs. Mox Diamond's sacrifice cost can be a liability in slow games. Grim Monolith requires careful mana management. Don't overcommit to attacking; Stax wins by attrition. Common mistake: Tapping Grim Monolith for mana too early without a plan. Always have a win condition or a way to close out the game.

philosophy: This four-color Jund variant operates as a flexible midrange engine, leveraging Green Sun’s Zenith to cheat mana on key threats while using Ignoble Hierarch for mana fixing, sacrifice outlets, and deathtouch removal. It aims to out-value opponents through card advantage, scalable threats like Scythecat Cub, and precise disruption, excelling against combo and control decks that overcommit early.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetch/shock sources), Green Sun’s Zenith paired with 2-3 mana, Ignoble Hierarch, or early threats (1-2 mana creatures). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of the same land type without fetches, or hands lacking early interaction/mana sources. Prioritize hands that can play a land, cast Hierarch or a 2-drop, and have a plan for turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Ignoble Hierarch if possible, or hold for disruption. Turn 2: Fetch lands for shock lands, play a second land, cast a 2-drop or use Hierarch sacrifice for mana to cast Green Sun’s Zenith targeting a 3-mana threat. Sequence lands to ensure double-green or green-black availability for GSZ.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Delver): +2 Lightning Strike, +2 Dismember, -2 Green Sun’s Zenith, -1 Scythecat Cub. vs Control (Dimir Tempo): +2 Dispel, +1 Thoughtseize, -2 Green Sun’s Zenith, -1 Scythecat Cub. vs Combo (Tron, Sneak & Show): +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Engineered Explosives, -2 Scythecat Cub, -1 Green Sun’s Zenith. vs Midrange (Ocelot Pride): +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Dispel, -2 Green Sun’s Zenith.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Green Sun’s Zenith as a tempo investment; cast it when you can follow up with a threat or removal. Use Ignoble Hierarch’s sacrifice for mana when you need to cast a spell or break through, but avoid sacrificing it prematurely if it’s your only removal. Track graveyard resources for GSZ and opponent’s recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or disruption ready to answer their response. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered interaction or if you’re facing a combo deck that needs to be disrupted first. Use Hierarch’s deathtouch to trade efficiently.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for key spells (e.g., Tron’s finishers, Sneak & Show’s combo pieces). Track their removal suite to know when GSZ will be answered.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Scythecat Cub with multiple +1/+1 counters, Green Sun’s Zenith targeting a late-game threat, and board-sweeper-resistant creatures. Win by out-resourcing through disruption and value spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent is tapped out, or you’ve removed their key interaction. Commit when Scythecat Cub is on the board with multiple creatures, or when you can cast GSZ into a threat they can’t answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a locked combo deck with no disruption, if your hand is dead against control (no threats/removal), or if you’ve lost your mana base to hate cards. Concede if you’re down to one card and the opponent has a sweeper or finisher.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Green Sun’s Zenith on your turn when they’re tapped out, or cast it in response to their removal. Use Ignoble Hierarch’s sacrifice to cast threats at instant speed if needed. Rely on hand disruption to remove counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Disrupt their mana sources early with Thoughtseize or Hierarch sacrifice. Use removal to kill their early threats, then close the game with scalable creatures.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal and disruption over GSZ early. Use Ignoble Hierarch to trade efficiently, and hold back Scythecat Cub until you stabilize. Sideboard in more efficient removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and disrupt their hand. Use GSZ to put threats on the board when they’re low on resources. Out-value them with card advantage and removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through better hand disruption, superior late-game threats, and precise mana sequencing. Use Thoughtseize to remove their GSZ or key threats, and out-grind with Scythecat Cub scaling.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a midrange grind to a more interactive or aggressive stance depending on the meta. Against combo/control, prioritize disruption and hate cards. Against aggro, streamline your curve and add efficient removal. Always keep Green Sun’s Zenith if you’re facing decks that can’t answer 3-mana threats, but drop it against decks that exploit your mana base or graveyard.
notes: Green Sun’s Zenith is a sorcery, so timing is critical—cast it when you can follow up or when the opponent can’t respond. Ignoble Hierarch’s sacrifice is a double-edged sword; use it for mana when necessary, but preserve it for deathtouch removal. Four-color mana requires careful land sequencing; fetch lands should target shock lands to avoid flooding. Avoid overcommitting to Scythecat Cub early without board support, as it’s vulnerable to sweepers.

philosophy: A tempo-control hybrid that leverages early token generation and precise damage to stabilize against aggressive and combo strategies, while using card selection to out-value control and midrange opponents. It aims to win by out-resourcing the midgame and closing out games with Orcish Bowmasters pressure or incremental Saproling damage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing basics for UBG mana), Brainstorm, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (Up the Beanstalk, Orcish Bowmasters, or early removal). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only non-basic lands and no cantrips/removal. Prioritize hands that can stabilize or apply early pressure.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Orcish Bowmasters if possible, or hold for a turn-2 Up the Beanstalk. Turn 2: Cast Up the Beanstalk to fetch a land and drop a Saproling, or use Brainstorm to dig for answers/land. Sequence lands carefully to ensure UBG mana base works (e.g., Island, Swamp, Forest sequencing).
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver, Bowmasters mirror): +1 discard, +1 direct damage, -1 control spell. vs Control (Dimir, Tron): +1 card draw, +1 counterspell/interaction, -1 early pressure. vs Combo (Sneak & Show): +1 graveyard hate, +1 specific hate (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline), -1 generic removal. vs Midrange (Ocelot Pride): +1 board sweep, +1 targeted removal, -1 token generators.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Brainstorm sparingly to find answers or land when mana is tight. Preserve Up the Beanstalk for critical turns to fix mana or generate a blocker. Don't overcommit Saprolings if the board is unstable; they are chump blockers and damage sources.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction ready or when the opponent has no removal. Use Orcish Bowmasters to trade with key creatures or finish the opponent. Hold back on casting Up the Beanstalk if you can stabilize with removal/counters.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron's signets/lands, Sneak & Show's graveyard setup, Delver's flip condition, and Ocelot Pride's mana acceleration. Key tells: Opponent playing multiple lands without spells suggests Tron or ramp. Opponent casting discard early suggests Delver or midrange. Opponent holding up mana on your turn suggests counters or sweepers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Orcish Bowmasters trading into key creatures and dealing 1 damage per turn, combined with Saproling tokens for incremental damage. Brainstorm to find the final answer or land to cast a win condition.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, the opponent is low on life (under 10), or you've removed their primary interaction. Commit Bowmasters and tokens to pressure, using Brainstorm to ensure you don't run out of resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be answered, if you're down to one life with no interaction and the opponent has a win condition in play, or if you're in a dead matchup with no comeback tools (e.g., Tron with empty graveyard and no sweepers).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Brainstorm to dig around counters, cast Up the Beanstalk in response to counters if it's a key play, or use cheap, uncounterable threats. Sequence spells to force counters on non-critical plays.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to stop mana acceleration. Use removal on key ramp creatures. Up the Beanstalk helps fix mana to cast answers faster.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and chump blockers. Use Orcish Bowmasters to trade with key attackers. Don't overextend; focus on surviving to turn 4-5.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply incremental pressure with tokens and Bowmasters. Use Brainstorm to find answers to their control spells. Out-value them by generating card advantage with Up the Beanstalk.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use Brainstorm to find the right answer. Prioritize board control and track interaction. Win by out-resourcing and closing out with Bowmasters pressure when they run out of answers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from tempo to control against combo and control decks, bringing in hate cards and card advantage. Against aggro, prioritize early interaction and direct damage. Adjust land counts if needed, and ensure you have enough answers for the specific format. Always track opponent's sideboarded cards and adapt your play order accordingly.
notes: Orcish Bowmasters is a double-edged sword; it can hit you or your own creatures if not careful. Up the Beanstalk's token is fragile but crucial for mana fixing and blocking. Brainstorm is your lifeline; use it to dig for answers, not just to draw cards. Watch for format-specific bans/restrictions if playing in a specific league. Common mistake: Overusing Up the Beanstalk early without a plan, or misplacing Bowmasters damage.

philosophy: A four-color control deck that stabilizes the early game with flexible counterspells and removal, then accelerates into a massive, growing Yorion, Sky Nomad or other game-ending threats using ramp spells. It wins by out-resourcing opponents through card advantage and precise interaction, excelling against combo and midrange decks that rely on specific key pieces.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one fetch/shock or dual land) and 2-3 spells. Prioritize hands with Force of Negation, Flow State, or early removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares, Abrade) alongside ramp (Growth Spiral, Beast Within, Kodama's Reach). Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only removal and no interaction, or hands with multiple copies of the same spell without land support.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, potentially cast a cheap ramp spell or hold for interaction. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a ramp spell or early interaction. If opening with duals/shocks, sequence to ensure colored mana for Force of Negation (U) and Flow State (U/W) or removal. Prioritize establishing board control over committing threats early.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride Midrange): Bring in more cheap removal (Abrade, Beast Within, Lightning Strike) and discard/hand disruption; reduce counterspells. Against control/midrange (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Keep counters, bring in card advantage (Teferi, Hero of Dominaria, Karn, the Great Creator) and artifact hate. Against combo (Sneak and Show): Bring in Force of Negation, Flow State, and specific hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment if applicable). Generally, sideboard out redundant counters against aggro, bring in more removal/hate against control/combo.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Force of Negation and Flow State for high-impact threats or combo pieces. Use ramp spells to accelerate into Yorion or card draw engines. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary discard or mill, but be aware of opponent's graveyard strategies. Keep a hand of 3-4 cards to maintain flexibility.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Yorion or other threats only when you have sufficient interaction to protect them or when the board is clear. Hold back counterspells for key threats (e.g., Tron's Arcane Signet, Sneak's Goblin Guide, Delver's Inverter). Use removal efficiently to trade 1-for-1 or 1-for-2.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development, hand size, and graveyard usage. Key tells: Opponent holding up mana on turn 2-3 likely has a counterspell or early threat. Opponent casting multiple ramp spells signals a combo or Tron strategy. Opponent discarding or milling suggests a control or midrange grind.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Yorion, Sky Nomad (often 5/5 or larger with +1/+1 counters from spells cast), Teferi, Hero of Dominaria, Karn, the Great Creator. Get there by ramping into them, then using removal/counters to clear the board and protect the finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to casting Yorion/finisher, opponent has no interaction left, or you can protect your threat with counterspells/removal. Also commit when you have card advantage and the opponent is low on resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that outpaces your stabilization, if you're dead to the board (e.g., multiple Tron pieces online with no removal), or if you have no answers to the opponent's win condition and no way to close the game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Flow State and Force of Negation to counter their counters. Cast spells in response to their counters, use cheap threats to force counters, then follow up with bigger threats. Rely on card advantage to out-grind.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use Force of Negation/Flow State on key ramp spells or finishers. Use removal on early threats. Don't let them get ahead; stabilize quickly and out-value them with card draw.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use cheap removal (Abrade, Beast Within, Path) and counterspells to stall. Ramp into Yorion or Teferi for late-game stability. Don't overcommit to counterspells; use removal for efficiency.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw (Teferi, Karn, Flow State). Use Force of Negation to protect your threats. Pressure with Yorion once you have card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Respect counterspells. Use Flow State to dig for answers. Win the card advantage war with Teferi/Karn. Use Force of Negation on their threats. Play conservatively early, then out-value them mid/late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite based on the metagame. Against aggro, reduce counterspells and increase cheap removal/hand disruption. Against control/combo, keep counters and bring in card advantage and hate cards. Always ensure your mana base can support the sideboard cards (e.g., dual lands for WUrg). Prioritize flexibility and card advantage in longer games.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting to counterspells early, failing to ramp into Yorion/finishers, mismanaging mana for colored requirements, not tracking opponent's graveyard/hand size. Unique interactions: Flow State can be used to dig for answers or cast multiple spells in a turn. Force of Negation can counter non-creature spells, making it excellent against combo. Yorion grows with every spell you cast, so ramp and interaction count toward its size. Always be mindful of the beanstalk mechanic every spell you cast makes your finisher bigger.

philosophy: Necrodominance is a fast, explosive Grixis combo deck that aims to assemble a game-winning threat via Beseech the Mirror and fast mana before opponents can stabilize. It wins by going off on turn 2 or 3, outpacing tempo and midrange archetypes while leveraging disruption to protect its fragile early game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with Beseech the Mirror plus two fast mana sources (Chrome Mox, Lotus Petal, or fetch/shock equivalents). Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one fast mana source if you have draw spells. Mulligan on 4+ lands with no fast mana, 3+ lands with no land fetchers, or hands with only one land and no acceleration. Prioritize hands that can cast Beseech by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lotus Petal or Chrome Mox. Turn 2: Use fast mana to cast Beseech the Mirror, then fetch your win condition (e.g., Griselbrand, Phyrexian Negator, or Bogardan Hellkite). If you lack Beseech, develop your mana base, cast draw spells, and look for alternative combo pieces or disruption. Always prioritize untapping fast mana to maximize your second-turn go-off potential.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro/combo (Sneak and Show, Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride Midrange): Bring in spot removal, discard, and early interaction to disrupt their early game. Against control/ramp (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in additional card advantage, anti-ramp hate, and alternative win conditions that are harder to answer. Against mirror: Bring in extra disruption, protection spells, and discard to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat fast mana as a one-time resource; conserve lands for casting Beseech and your win condition. Avoid overcommitting to the stack if you lack follow-up. Use draw spells to cycle into answers or combo pieces, and keep your graveyard clean unless your build specifically synergizes with it. Prioritize hand integrity over board presence in the early turns.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, hard counters, or significant interaction on the stack. Commit to going off only when you have a clear path to victory or when the opponent is tapped out with no removal. If you establish a resilient threat, apply pressure but keep a backup plan in hand to protect your win condition.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (especially in Dimir Tempo) and note when opponents tap out for disruption versus board presence. Track when Tron players hit their lands and when Sneak and Show players hold up mana for protection. Pay attention to discard targets and prioritize protecting your fast mana and Beseech from early hand disruption.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Beseech the Mirror fetching a game-ending threat like Griselbrand, Phyrexian Negator, or Bogardan Hellkite, enabled by Chrome Mox and Lotus Petal. Once the win condition is on the battlefield, attack with it or use its abilities to close the game immediately.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have two or more fast mana sources in play, Beseech in hand, and the opponent has no interaction on the stack or is tapped out. Also commit when you've established a resilient threat and the opponent lacks sweepers or hard removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're on the draw against a locked-out control deck with no draw spells or interaction, or if you're facing a fast aggro combo with no win condition in hand and your life total is critically low. Also concede if your entire game plan is countered or removed with no backup resources.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats if available, or layer Beseech with discard and spot removal to clear the way. If countered, pivot to a backup plan using draw spells to find alternative win conditions or disruption. Never overcommit to casting Beseech if you know the opponent has a hard counter and no follow-up.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use discard to hit their mana rocks or key lands. Go off before they stabilize, and prioritize hands with interaction. If they stabilize, use card advantage to out-value them and find a win condition that bypasses their ramp.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize interaction and discard to remove key threats. Stabilize with spot removal and hold back on going off until you're at a safe life total or have established a resilient board presence. Don't overextend into their combat tricks or protection spells.
threat_responses.against_control: Outlast them with card advantage and discard to remove key answers. Go off when they're tapped out or out of resources, and use Beseech to fetch threats that are difficult for control to answer. If they have sweepers, wait for them to use them on your early threats before going off.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing discard, protecting your combo pieces, and going off first. Sideboard in additional disruption and protection, and track when your opponent is holding up mana for interaction versus developing their board. Force them to make mistakes by applying early pressure and punishing misplays.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo game plan to a hybrid control-combo strategy. Bring in interaction against control and tempo, and hate cards against combo and aggro.
