Adjust your land count if the metagame shifts toward slower games, and prioritize keeping hands with interaction plus combo pieces. Always have a backup plan ready for when your primary win condition is answered.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to casting Beseech the Mirror when you lack follow-up or face hard counters. Always keep a backup plan in hand. Fast mana is fragile; protect it from discard and spot removal. Beseech can fetch lands, so ensure your mana base can support your win condition. Adapt to local rules and format restrictions, as this archetype is highly sensitive to banned/restricted lists and format-specific mechanics.

philosophy: Jeskai Tempo aims to control the board through efficient interaction and card advantage, out-valuing opponents in the midgame before closing out games with resilient threats or spell-slinging. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on specific pieces or long games, while struggling against fast aggro and heavy ramp if unanswered.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or fixing), Ponder or another card-draw engine, and at least one turn-1 or turn-2 interaction (bounce/removal) or a 2-drop threat. Mulligan flood, mana screw, or hands with only high-cost spells without fixing or early answers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land; Ponder if opening with 3+ lands or no early plays to dig for a turn-2 threat or interaction. Turn 2: Cast a 2-drop threat or Flow State/Tamiyo if mana allows, or hold up interaction to protect your board. Sequence lands to ensure WU or UW availability, and use Ponder to smooth out mana or find answers to early aggression.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro: Bring in cheap removal, +1/+1 counters, and direct damage to stabilize early. Vs Control/Tempo: Add more card draw, hatebears, and additional bounce to win interaction wars. Vs Combo (Sneak & Show): Include artifact/graveyard hate, discard, or specific lock pieces. Vs Tron/Ramp: Bring in enchantment/artifact removal, additional interaction, and stabilization tools to slow their acceleration.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until threats are established or opponents commit to plays. Use Ponder, Flow State, and Tamiyo to cycle into answers or threats, but avoid over-digging early. Manage your graveyard carefully if opponents run graveyard hate, and don't overextend into known sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if they have sweepers, heavy removal, or combo pieces in play. Use bounce effects to reset key permanents, protect your own threats, or disrupt opponent's mana development.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or leave mana open for interaction. Track graveyard contents against combo decks, and monitor land drops to predict ramp or Tron lock potential.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Resilient creatures, Flow State/Tamiyo bounce loops, or direct damage. Win by out-valuing in card advantage, protecting a key threat, or closing out with a board state that opponents cannot clear efficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers or interaction, or you can protect your threat with bounce/removal. Commit when you've disrupted their win condition and can pressure them into a losing position.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, the opponent has a guaranteed win condition (e.g., combo off, Tron lock), or you have no interaction against their board state. Concede early if you've exhausted your resources and cannot stabilize or close the game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ponder to dig for answers, cast threats in response to counterspells, or use bounce to protect your own spells. Don't overcommit to one threat; spread your investment and force them to counter multiple spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to slow their acceleration. Use bounce/removal to reset key permanents and avoid inefficient trades. Wait for them to overextend or tap out for big spells before committing your own threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize first with cheap removal and bounce. Avoid digging for card advantage early; focus on surviving the initial assault. Use +1/+1 counters if available, and transition to value once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with threats and use card advantage to out-value them. Bounce their key permanents to disrupt their game plan, and force them to spend resources on interaction rather than executing their own plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war by holding up counterspells/bounce for their threats. Use Ponder to find answers and out-value them in card advantage. Win by out-resolving, protecting your threats, and forcing them to overcommit into your interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Bring in hate cards for combo/ramp, more interaction for control/tempo, and stabilization tools for aggro. Keep core card draw and bounce effects, and adjust land count if facing heavy ramp or fast aggro. Prioritize flexibility and answer diversity to handle diverse threats.
notes: Common mistakes include over-digging with Ponder early and flooding out, or using Flow State/Tamiyo bounce on your own creatures unnecessarily. Always track opponent's mana and graveyard, as Jeskai Tempo relies on precise timing and interaction efficiency. The synergy between Tamiyo and Flow State is key for card advantage and board control, so sequence them to maximize value without overcommitting.

philosophy: A fast-mana Grixis combo deck that assembles a self-referential engine (Grim Monolith, The One Ring, Transmute Artifact) to generate massive mana in a single turn, then casts a game-winning spell or combo finisher. It aims to out-accelerate midrange/control strategies and race aggressive decks before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetchable or shock lands if format-legal) plus at least one core combo piece (Grim Monolith, The One Ring, or Transmute Artifact). Keep hands with early interaction (Thoughtseize, Inquisition, Duress) that support the combo. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple non-basic lands without fetch support, or hands containing only combo pieces with no mana or interaction to protect them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or early interaction to disrupt the opponent or dig for combo pieces. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Transmute Artifact to find a mana rock (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or similar), or cast The One Ring. If you have Grim Monolith, play it on turn 2 when possible. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast pieces on curve without tapping out prematurely.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Sneak and Show, Izzet Delver): Bring in countermagic (Spell Pierce, Daze), discard (Thoughtseize, Duress), and spot removal (Lightning Helix, Go for the Throat). Against control/midrange (Dimir Tempo, Ocelot Pride, Tron): Bring in more hand disruption (Inquisition, Surgical Extraction), combo protection (Force of Will, Mana Leak), and additional win conditions. Against mirror: Bring in discard and countermagic to disrupt their engine assembly.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize assembling the mana engine over spending mana on non-essential spells. Use Transmute Artifact strategically to find the most efficient mana rock for your current board state. Manage your graveyard carefully, as graveyard hate is common; keep backup plans ready. Hold cards in hand to disrupt opponent key plays while preserving combo pieces for your turn.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to combo off or when you can stabilize against aggro. Hold back combo pieces if the opponent has significant disruption (counterspells, hate cards) and you lack answers. Use early interaction to clear the board or disrupt their game plan before going for the win.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Force of Will, Spell Pierce, Daze), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction), and board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods). Pay attention to when the opponent taps out for a key spell or leaves mana open for protection. Track their graveyard if they play graveyard-dependent strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The primary win condition is assembling Grim Monolith + The One Ring + a mana rock (via Transmute Artifact) to generate 10+ mana, then casting a game-winning spell or combo finisher. Secondary finishers include using the mana to cast multiple disruptive spells or combo off with alternative win conditions if the primary plan is disrupted.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have assembled the core mana engine, have cleared or bypassed the opponent's disruption, and have a clear path to casting your win condition. Commit when you can generate enough mana to cast your win condition and still have protection (e.g., counterspells, discard, or instant-speed protection).
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard lock (e.g., Rest in Peace + Leyline of the Void, or a combo that cannot be disrupted), if you have no way to assemble your engine, or if you are down to a single card against a deck that can win on the spot. Also concede if you are out of resources and the opponent has stabilized against aggro or control.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple casting steps (cast pieces on different turns), use cheap interaction (Thoughtseize, Inquisition) to remove counterspells from hand, or use protection spells (Force of Will, Spell Pierce) to protect your combo. If possible, cast your win condition in response to their counterspells or use free spells to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them by going off as soon as possible. Use early interaction to disrupt their mana development (Thoughtseize, Inquisition). If they outpace you, use spot removal or discard to slow them down, then try to combo off before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and spot removal to stabilize. Use discard to remove key threats. If you cannot stabilize, go off as fast as possible. Use countermagic to protect your combo pieces from removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with early interaction and discard to disrupt their hand. Use the mana engine to cast multiple spells in a turn, overwhelming their resources. If they have a hard lock, use disruption to remove key pieces or go off around it.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the mirror by going off first. Use discard to remove their combo pieces or interaction. Use countermagic to protect your engine. Prioritize assembling the mana engine faster and casting your win condition before they can respond.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite based on the metagame. Against control/midrange, increase discard and countermagic to disrupt their plans. Against aggro, add more spot removal and early interaction. Always keep at least one copy of your core combo pieces in the main deck. Post-sideboard, be more aggressive with your early game plays and less tolerant of bad draws. Use sideboard cards to disrupt opponent key strategies while protecting your own engine.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the mana engine without a clear win condition, failing to track opponent disruption, and not using Transmute Artifact optimally. Unique interactions: The One Ring can be tapped for mana even if you control other artifacts, and it can be used to pay for costs. Grim Monolith requires you to control no other artifacts, so sequence carefully. Transmute Artifact can find any artifact, so prioritize mana rocks but also consider utility artifacts if needed. Always be aware of the 'artifact tax' and plan your turns accordingly.

philosophy: Dimir Reanimator is a discard-driven combo-control deck that disrupts early game plans, protects its graveyard, and swings the board with high-impact threats pulled from the graveyard. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and control decks through sudden, protected board states, while out-grinding tempo and aggressive archetypes by stabilizing into game-ending threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (prioritizing fetch/shock or duals), at least one Thoughtseize, and at least one reanimation spell or mana acceleration. Keep hands with 3+ lands if they include Thoughtseize and a reanimation spell. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no spells, hands with only reanimation spells and no mana/discard, or hands with zero interaction against aggro/tempo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible to hit key early threats or mana rocks. Turn 2: Develop mana, potentially cast a second Thoughtseize or a cheap threat. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetch/shock lands to enter untapped, then basics to enable shock lands. Ensure you can cast Thoughtseize on turn 1 or 2 to disrupt Tron, Sneak and Show, or Izzet Delver before they stabilize.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Ocelot Pride, Izzet Delver): +2 Graveyard Hate (Rest in Peace/Surgical Extraction), +2 Counterspell/Flashback removal, -1 Reanimate, -1 Threat. vs Control (Dimir Tempo, Tron): +1 Extra Reanimation spell, +1 Card Draw, -1 Discard. vs Combo: +1 Graveyard Hate, +1 Counterspell, -2 Threats. vs Mirror: +1 Extra Protection/Counterspell, +1 Graveyard Hate, -1 Threat.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve graveyard resources; avoid overcommitting to one threat if multiple reanimation targets are available. Keep mana open for protection (Counterspell, Flashback, or instant-speed discard). Trade discard for card advantage when ahead, and use Atraxa, Grand Unifier's ETB to generate mana/graveyard value when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or when the board is clear. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers (Wrath of God, Toxic Deluge) or graveyard hate in play. Use reanimation to trade up (e.g., reanimate a 4-drop to kill a 2-drop, or swing for lethal).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), sweepers, and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or hold up mana for protection. Track how many cards they have in hand vs graveyard to predict reanimation targets.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Atraxa, Grand Unifier (enters with multiple value triggers, including reanimating a permanent from graveyard and generating mana/graveyard value), or high-impact reanimated threats (e.g., Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath, Teferi, Hero of Dominaria, or format-specific win conditions). Win by swinging with multiple threats or using Atraxa's ETB to put a game-winning permanent onto the battlefield.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have lethal damage in the graveyard, when the opponent has no removal/hate, or when you can protect your reanimation spell. Commit when you can trade up or when the opponent is at low life total.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, the opponent has active graveyard hate, you have no reanimation spells, and you can't stabilize against their board. Also concede against combo decks that win on turn 3-4 if you can't disrupt them, or against Tron if they have 5+ mana and no way to interact.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split your reanimation targets, or cast reanimation spells in response to their counterspells. Use flashback or alternative costs if available. Force them to use multiple counterspells by threatening multiple angles.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize to hit their mana rocks. Stabilize with a single reanimated threat, then close out the game before they can cast their win conditions. Don't let them get to 5+ mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize discard to hit their early threats. Use a single reanimated blocker or removal spell to stabilize, then transition to control. Don't overcommit to reanimation early; focus on surviving to turn 3-4.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and reanimation. Trade discard for their key pieces. Use Atraxa or a big threat to pressure their life total and force mistakes. Don't let them stabilize with sweepers by threatening multiple reanimation targets.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Protect your graveyard with hate. Use reanimation to trade up. Prioritize keeping hands with multiple threats and protection. Force them to use counterspells on non-lethal threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo/control shell to a more resilient midrange/reanimator hybrid. Increase graveyard hate and counterspells against control/combo, and add more early interaction against aggro. Adjust your sideboard based on metagame shifts, but always keep at least one reanimation spell and a discard outlet. Prioritize cards that protect your graveyard or provide alternative win conditions.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to reanimation without protection, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. Unique interaction: Atraxa, Grand Unifier's ETB can search for a basic land, put it tapped, then put it into the graveyard, potentially fueling your reanimation engine or triggering landfall effects. Always check the graveyard for value before casting reanimation. Watch for Leyline of the Void in the sideboard of control decks.

philosophy: Esper Vial is a tempo-control hybrid that uses Aether Vial to efficiently deploy a mix of cheap threats and spot removal, aiming to out-value opponents through flexible mana acceleration and card advantage while controlling the board until Yorion, Sky Nomad closes out games. It excels against control and midrange decks that overcommit to single resources, and punishes combo decks that lack immediate interaction.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Aether Vial, and at least one 1-2 CMC creature or removal spell. Keep hands that can play a land and a 1-drop or Vial on turn 1. Mulligan hands with Vial but no creatures/removal, 4+ lands with no early plays, or hands lacking interaction against known tempo/combo threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Aether Vial or a 1-drop creature. Turn 2: Play land, cast a second land or cheap creature/removal. If you have Vial + a cheap creature, Vial the creature on turn 2 or 3 to establish board presence while preserving mana for interaction. Prioritize developing a defensive wall of threats while holding up spot removal or counters for key metagame threats like Delver or Tron engines.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver/Sneak & Show): Bring in additional spot removal, counters, and early blockers. vs Control/Tron: Bring in more card draw, late-game finishers, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counters, and early pressure to disrupt assembly. vs Mirror: Bring in extra value engines, targeted removal, and additional card advantage to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Aether Vial to cast creatures from hand when you have excess mana, effectively converting mana into tempo. Avoid overextending into known sweepers or heavy removal. Balance hand size by using Yorion triggers to refill, and manage your graveyard carefully if the format features graveyard hate. Prioritize keeping interaction for the opponent's win conditions rather than burning it on low-impact threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if they have sweepers, counters, or heavy removal in hand. Use Vial to deploy blockers or favorable trades in response to opponent attacks. Maintain board parity until you can safely deploy Yorion or a game-winning threat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or engines. Track graveyard activity if relevant. Pay attention to Tron/Drake mana bases and Sneak & Show hand disruption. Adjust your Vial timing based on their available interaction and board state.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Yorion, Sky Nomad is the primary closer. Cast it to draw a card and potentially cheat a game-winning permanent onto the battlefield. Secondary finishers include mid-4 CMC creatures Vial'd into play or late-game value engines that out-resource the opponent.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, sufficient interaction to protect Yorion, and the opponent is low on resources or has no sweepers. Commit when you can Vial multiple threats to close the game or when the opponent is tapped out for a key spell. Also commit when you can force a favorable trade that leaves you ahead in board state.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Concede if you're facing a locked-down Tron or Sneak & Show with no interaction, or if you're dead to the board with no way to rebuild. Fold when you're out of resources, the opponent has a proven win condition, and you have no path to stabilize or close the game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Vial to cast creatures when opponents are tapped out or after they've used counters. Deploy cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (flying, hexproof, or multiple targets). Save interaction for their key spells and avoid overcommitting to value when they have counterspells in hand.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures and Vial threats to force trades before they stabilize. Don't let them reach their late-game power level; pressure them into mistakes and close the game before they can deploy their engines.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and blockers. Use Vial to deploy efficient blockers or removal. Don't overcommit to value; stabilize first, then close. Track their hand for sweepers or removal and adjust your threat deployment accordingly.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage and efficient Vial plays. Use Yorion to draw into answers and pressure with Vial'd threats to force mistakes. Don't let them stabilize with sweepers; apply consistent pressure and close the game before they can draw into their win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card advantage and efficient Vial plays. Target their key threats with removal and control the board to force them to overextend. Track their hand for interaction and adjust your threat deployment accordingly. Win the late game by out-valuing them with Yorion and superior resource management.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame shifts. Bring in more interaction vs tempo/aggro, more value/combo hate vs control/combo, and additional card advantage vs mirror. Keep Aether Vial as the core engine. Adjust land counts if needed to optimize for speed or resilience. Prioritize cards that synergize with Vial or provide late-game stability, and always keep at least one copy of your primary finishers.
notes: Common mistakes include overusing Vial on low-impact creatures, forgetting to hold up interaction, and mismanaging Yorion timing. Remember that creatures Vial'd into play enter tapped, so plan your turns accordingly. Synergize Yorion with cheap threats to maximize the 'put a permanent from hand' trigger. Always track the opponent's hand for sweepers and counters, and adjust your Vial timing to exploit their tapped-out turns. In Unknown format, adapt quickly to meta shifts and prioritize flexibility over rigid game plans.

philosophy: Grixis Tempo seeks to disrupt the opponent's game plan while establishing board control through efficient threats and card selection, then closing out the game with Delver of Secrets and Flow State. It wins by out-tempoing and out-resourcing opponents, leveraging disruption and card advantage to outlast midrange, control, and combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks), Ponder, early disruption (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), and Delver/Flow State. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no spells, 0-1 lands with no cantrips, or hands lacking a turn-1 play. Prioritize hands that can Ponder into a land or cast disruption on turn one.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Ponder into a land or cast Thoughtseize/Inquisition on a key threat or combo piece. Turn 2: Drop Delver if it's a two-drop, or cast Flow State if ahead, or play a shock land/mana source. Sequence mana carefully to keep options open and avoid tapping out unnecessarily.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Dimir Tempo, Izzet Delver, Ocelot Pride): +2/4 Counterspell, +2/4 Discard, +2/4 Spot Removal. vs Control/Tron: +2/4 Graveyard Hate, +2/4 Disruption, +2/4 Finishers. vs Sneak and Show: +2/4 Graveyard Hate, +2/4 Discard, +2/4 Board Wipes. vs Mirror: +2/4 Disruption, +2/4 Counters, +2/4 Threat Upgrades.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Ponder to find lands or key spells; avoid overcommitting to the board. Manage your graveyard carefully—Flow State needs it, but be wary of hate. Keep hand size up with cantrips and discard to maintain card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Delver when you have removal for their threats or they're tapped out. Hold back if they have sweepers or counters. Use disruption to protect your board and force unfavorable trades.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Damnation), counterspells, graveyard hate, and combo pieces. Note when they tap out for big spells or when they're low on resources. Track their graveyard for Flow State or Sneak and Show threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Delver of Secrets (Aetherborn), Flow State, efficient removal, and card advantage engines. Win by out-resourcing and closing with a lethal threat or combo.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, they're tapped out, or you've removed their key answers. Commit with Delver + Flow State + removal to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, they have a guaranteed win condition (e.g., Tron with mana rocks, Sneak and Show with Unearth), or you're completely out of answers to their strategy.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ponder to dig for answers, cast threats at end step, or chain multiple threats. Flow State can bypass counters if cast from the graveyard. Sideboard in cheaper or uncounterable threats if needed.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early disruption is key. Thoughtseize/Inquisition to hit mana dorks or key rocks. Spot removal on ramp threats. Don't overextend; out-tempo them with efficient plays.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Countermagic, spot removal, and discard to slow them down. Use Delver to race or stabilize. Flow State to close out if they can't answer it.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with threats, use disruption to hit their answers, and win the card advantage war with Ponder and efficient spells. Outlast them with better late-game threats and graveyard synergy.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the disruption war. Out-Ponder, out-discard, and out-tempo. Sideboard for more counters and disruption. Track their hand and graveyard to predict their plays.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Generally, bring in more disruption and counters vs fast decks, more graveyard hate vs combo, and more threats vs control. Keep the core game plan intact but adapt to specific threats. Prioritize hands with sideboard cards in play.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board, mismanaging mana, ignoring graveyard hate, and Pondering into dead cards. Unique interactions: Flow State synergy with Delver, Ponder for consistency, and disruption timing. Always track their graveyard and hand size to predict their moves.

philosophy: Sultai Depths is a control/graveyard archetype that uses fast mana and card selection to stabilize the early game, then overwhelms opponents with a massive graveyard-fueled threat like Undercity Regent. It excels in longer games against control and midrange decks, while relying on precise early sequencing and discard to disrupt tempo and combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including a fetch or shockland), at least one fast mana source (Lotus Petal, Elvish Spirit Guide, or Crop Rotation), and a removal or discard spell. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no fast mana, or only threats with no support. Keep hands that can play a land and cast a cantrip/removal on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lotus Petal/Elvish Spirit Guide, and use Crop Rotation on a fetch or basic to find a shockland or Depths. Cast Thoughtseize/Duress to disrupt. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast removal (Fatal Push/Flaw) or dig for Undercity Regent. Prioritize establishing a stable mana base and graveyard resources. If facing aggro, hold up removal for turn 1/2 threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Tempo (Delver, Ocelot): Bring in +1/+1 counters, more removal, discard, and Tormod's Crypt. vs Control (Dimir, Tron): Bring in graveyard hate, additional card draw, protection spells, and more discard. vs Combo (Sneak and Show): Bring in graveyard hate, discard, fast mana to race, and artifact hate. vs Mirror: Bring in additional removal, discard, and protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve fast mana for critical turns. Use Crop Rotation to fix mana or find Depths. Manage your graveyard carefully—avoid unnecessary sac outlets unless generating immediate value. Prioritize card advantage to out-resource control decks.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Undercity Regent only when you can protect it or when the board is clear. Use discard to remove key threats before they resolve. Hold back on overcommitting if the opponent has unanswered interaction or a sweeper in hand.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and graveyard hate. Note if the opponent is playing around Depths (hold up interaction). Track their graveyard size for Gravecrawler/Regent triggers and monitor their mana development for Tron or combo turns.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Undercity Regent (often 10+ power) or Gravecrawler. Win by deploying Regent with protection, or by out-graveyarding with Gravecrawler + card draw.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have a clear path to attack, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can protect your threat with discard/removal. Go all-in if you're ahead on board and the opponent is out of interactive resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to a single threat with no protection against a known sweeper, or if the opponent has established a lock (e.g., Tron with full mana rocks, or Sneak and Show with an infinite combo). Fold if you have no way to interact and are losing life total.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast threats in response to their interaction. Use Depths to tutor around counters. Overcommit with multiple threats if possible to force a trade.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Disrupt their mana with discard and removal. Use fast mana to race or stabilize. Sideboard in land destruction or hate. Prioritize removing their mana rocks early.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early discard and cheap removal to stall. Prioritize survival over value. Sideboard in more removal and discard. Play conservatively until you can deploy a Regent.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with card draw and discard. Protect your threat. Use Depths to find answers. Pressure with early threats to force mistakes and exhaust their hand.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Protect your Regent. Use Depths to tutor for the right threat. Prioritize graveyard advantage and track their interaction to time your attacks.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control, prioritize graveyard hate and card advantage. Against aggro/tempo, increase removal and discard. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and fast mana to race. Always keep at least one copy of Depths and fast mana. Adjust land counts if needed for specific matchups, and consider bringing in additional protection if the meta is heavy on sweepers.
notes: Depths is a key tutor; don't overextend without it. Crop Rotation is versatile; use it to find shocklands or Depths, not just basics. Watch for Tron's mana rocks; early discard is crucial. Gravecrawler scales with graveyard size; don't sac creatures unnecessarily. Common mistake: Overcommitting early without protection, leading to a sweeper wipe. Fast mana sequencing is critical; Lotus Petal + Elvish Spirit Guide can enable turn-2 threats.

philosophy: A resilient midrange-control hybrid that wins by out-resourcing opponents through card advantage (Yorion), efficient disruption (Solitude, removal), and escalating threats (Batterskull, Serra Ascendant). It excels against combo and control by stabilizing early, leveraging graveyard hate, and closing games with equipment or flying threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a turn-1 play (Thalia's Lieutenant, early removal, or Solitude), and a way to cast your 4-drops. Keep Stoneforge Mystic if you have a land and can cast it for 2 mana. Keep Yorion if you have 3+ lands or a way to accelerate it. Mulligan pure land, pure removal, or hands with no early interaction/land base.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Thalia's Lieutenant or a removal spell if possible. If opening Stoneforge Mystic, play land, cast Mystic, tap for mana, search for Batterskull or a threat, and equip if mana allows. Turn 2: Develop board with Thalia, equip Batterskull, or cast Yorion if ahead. Prioritize playing lands to ensure consistent casting of 4-drops without stalling.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo (Sneak & Show): +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Leyline of Punishment, +1 Path to Exile, -2 Serra Ascendant, -1 Yorion. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir): +1 Solitude, +1 Swords to Plowshares, +1 Batterskull, -2 Rest in Peace, -1 Leyline. vs Midrange (Ocelot): +1 Thalia's Lieutenant, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Batterskull, -2 Serra Ascendant, -1 Yorion. vs Tron: +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Leyline, +1 Solitude, -2 Serra Ascendant, -1 Yorion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve removal for key threats (Tron artifacts, Sneak & Show creatures, Dimir counterspells). Don't overcommit Yorion unless you can protect it or have a clear path to close. Use Stoneforge Mystic to find Batterskull early for board control, or save it for Serra Ascendant later. Manage your own graveyard carefully to avoid feeding Serra Ascendant prematurely or enabling opponent strategies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the board is clear. Hold back Yorion if facing board wipes or heavy removal. Use Solitude to answer problematic permanents (planeswalkers, artifacts, or resilient creatures). Prioritize trading efficiently with Thalia's Lieutenant and removal to maintain board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Dimir), artifact acceleration (Tron), graveyard recursion (Sneak & Show), and early aggressive threats (Delver, Ocelot). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up interaction. Track graveyard contents if they play graveyard strategies, and adjust your hate spells accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Batterskull-equipped Serra Ascendant, Yorion with flying threats, or a fully equipped Knight of the Reliquary. Close the game by attacking with Batterskull's lifelink and trample, or flying over blockers with Yorion's +1/+1 counters.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage in hand, removal to answer their last interaction, and a clear path to damage. Commit Yorion when you can protect it or when you're ahead on the board and they lack board wipes.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no removal, if your hand is dead against a fast combo, or if you're down to one life and have no way to break through their board. Also concede if you're facing a graveyard strategy and have no hate in hand or sideboard.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Solitude to answer their counterspells or key permanents. Play around counters by casting threats at end step or using instant-speed interaction. Don't overcommit Yorion into a locked board; trade efficiently and wait for them to blink or tap out.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thalia and early threats. Use Solitude to remove their ramp sources or finishers. Play Rest in Peace to disrupt their graveyard and force them to interact with your board instead of accelerating.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Thalia's Lieutenant, early removal, and Solitude. Don't overextend into board wipes. Use Batterskull to stabilize and gain life. Prioritize surviving turn 3-4 before committing to a clock.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and equipment. Use Solitude to answer their removal or planeswalkers. Close the game quickly with Batterskull or Serra Ascendant before they can stabilize. Don't let them draw you out of the game; maintain board presence.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with Yorion, better sequencing of Stoneforge Mystic, and more efficient removal. Prioritize trading creatures and using Solitude to answer their key threats. Control the board with Thalia and equipment, and force them to make the first mistake.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Bring in graveyard hate vs combo/recursion, more removal vs control, and more early interaction vs aggro. Generally, keep Yorion and Stoneforge Mystic, but swap out high-cost finishers like Serra Ascendant for more interactive pieces. Always have a plan to answer the opponent's primary win condition, and don't be afraid to sideboard out your own finishers if the matchup demands a control-oriented approach.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting Yorion without protection, failing to play Rest in Peace/Leyline when needed, missequencing Stoneforge Mystic (finding Batterskull too late), and ignoring the graveyard. Unique interactions: Solitude can target any permanent, making it excellent against artifacts and planeswalkers. Yorion's +1/+1 counters stack with equipment. Serra Ascendant requires 10+ cards in graveyard, so manage your own graveyard carefully to avoid feeding it prematurely or enabling opponent strategies.

philosophy: Orzhov Stoneblade is a midrange-control hybrid that stabilizes early with efficient removal and card advantage, then pivots to a lethal game plan using Stoneforge Mystic to equip Sword of Feast and Famine to an efficient threat, or closes out games with Yorion, Sky Nomad. It excels against tempo and combo decks by out-resourcing them and punishes slower decks by applying consistent, unblockable pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetch/shock or basics), Stoneforge Mystic, and at least one removal spell (Solitude, Path, or Swords). Keep hands with 2+ lands and a threat/removal combo. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only removal and no threats/land, or hands with 3+ lands and no early plays. Against fast decks like Izzet Delver or Sneak & Show, prioritize hands with early interaction and a turn-2 play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. If you have Stoneforge Mystic, play it. Turn 2: If you have a land and a removal/threat, play it. If you have Stoneforge Mystic, activate to get Sword of Feast and Famine, equip to a Knight of the Reliquary or 2/2 threat. If you have Yorion, consider holding for later unless you need the protection. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/shock lands if available; otherwise, play basics to ensure colored mana for removal and Mystic.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Sneak & Show, Izzet Delver): Bring in more removal (Solitude, Path), and consider discard or hatebears. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Tron): Bring in more card advantage (Yorion, additional removal), and graveyard hate if needed. vs Combo: Bring in specific hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline if applicable, or additional removal). vs Mirror: Bring in additional removal and threat protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Solitude efficiently (save for key threats or to protect Yorion/SFM). Don't overcommit removal on small creatures if you can stabilize with board presence. Manage graveyard by using Solitude to exile or by playing Rest in Peace if needed. Keep hand size healthy; discard non-essentials if flooded.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal to protect them or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if you suspect a sweep or if you need to stabilize first. Prioritize trading efficiently; don't overextend into sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Dimir Tempo), ramp spells (Tron), combo pieces (Sneak & Show, Izzet Delver), and midrange threats (Ocelot Pride). Key tells: Opponent tapping for multiple mana, holding up removal, or playing non-basic lands.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sword of Feast and Famine equipped to a Knight of the Reliquary or 2/2 threat, or Yorion, Sky Nomad attacking with evasion and lifelink. Get there by stabilizing early, finding equipment, and trading efficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a lethal equipped threat, when the opponent has no sweepers, or when you can trade up favorably. Also commit when you need to race a combo deck.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards with no removal or threats, if you're facing a locked-out combo deck with no hate, or if you're completely out of resources against a dominant midrange/control deck.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Solitude to exile threats instead of targeting them. Attack with multiple threats to force multiple counters. Use Sword of Feast and Famine's deathtouch to trade efficiently.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal to kill key ramp creatures. Don't overextend into their late-game finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and early threats. Use Yorion to protect your board and gain life. Don't overcommit to equipment if you can win with efficient trades.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with threats and removal. Use Solitude to remove their key spells. Force them to use resources on your threats while you build a board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use Solitude efficiently to remove their threats. Prioritize finding Sword of Feast and Famine first. Trade up favorably and don't overextend.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your removal and threat ratio based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize removal and early interaction. Against slower decks, bring in more card advantage and late-game finishers. Always keep at least one copy of Solitude and Stoneforge Mystic in your main deck. Adjust your land base if needed to handle specific mana requirements.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Solitude on small creatures instead of saving it for key threats or to protect Yorion. Another mistake: Forgetting to equip Sword of Feast and Famine immediately after fetching it. Unique interaction: Yorion can protect your board from sweepers if you sacrifice a creature, but be mindful of your own removal targets. Always track the opponent's graveyard if they run graveyard-based strategies.

philosophy: 4c Depths is a tempo-control archetype that leverages delve mechanics to cast resilient threats at reduced costs while using discard and counterspells to disrupt the opponent’s game plan. It aims to out-value and out-tempo midrange, combo, and control decks by controlling the early board and closing out games with efficient, graveyard-fueled threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands (prioritizing fetch/shock or duals that can produce WUBG), a turn-1 play (land, Orcish Bowmasters, or Delver), and at least one disruption spell (Thoughtseize, Brainstorm, or a counterspell). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, hands with only removal and no threats/land, or hands missing colored sources for your key spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Orcish Bowmasters or Delver of Secrets, or cast Thoughtseize on a key threat. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast Brainstorm to filter into threats or answers, or play a second land and cast a delve creature (Tarmogoyf, Cryptbreaker). Sequence lands to ensure you can produce the necessary colors for removal and delve costs while maintaining a steady curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Dimir Tempo, Izzet Delver): In Graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), extra removal (Lightning Bolt, Go for the Throat), and life gain. Out: Delve creatures, extra discard. vs Control/Tron: In more pressure (Delver, Tarmogoyf), card advantage (Brainstorm, Dark Confidant), and targeted removal. Out: Graveyard hate, excess discard. vs Combo: In counterspells (Spellstutter Sprite, Spell Snare), early pressure, and graveyard hate. Out: Midrange threats. vs Mirror: In more counterspells, discard, and targeted removal. Out: Extra land and generic threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Brainstorm to filter into threats or counterspells based on board state. Manage your graveyard carefully—delve creatures want to be cast from it, but avoid overcommitting to it if the opponent has graveyard hate. Use Liliana of the Veil or Dark Confidant to maintain card advantage while monitoring your life total.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or counterspells to protect them and the opponent has no sweepers. Hold back if they have untapped mana for counters or a known sweeper in hand. Use counterspells on game-winning threats or key combo pieces rather than cheap spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate, sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for counterspells. Track their graveyard size to anticipate delve threats or recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cryptbreaker, Tarmogoyf, and Delver of Secrets (Inferno Titan/Insectile Aberration). Win by establishing board presence, using delve to cast them cheaply, and protecting them with counterspells or removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and they have no sweepers or graveyard hate in play. Commit when you can close the game in 1–2 turns or when you’ve forced them to use their answers on multiple threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to 1 life with no threats, facing a Tron/Combo deck with a guaranteed win condition, or if your hand is completely dead against their specific hate (e.g., Rest in Peace with no recursion). Also concede if you’re out of resources and they have a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Brainstorm to find cheaper threats or counterspells. Cast spells in response to their counters or when they’re tapped out. Use discard to remove their counterspells, and prioritize casting threats when they have no mana to respond. Delve doesn’t bypass counterspells, so timing and card advantage are key.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use removal on their mana rocks and early threats. If they get ahead, use counterspells on their win conditions and stabilize with removal. Don’t let them establish a massive mana base; disrupt their acceleration early.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early threats and removal. Use counterspells on key damage sources and discard their early plays. Don’t overcommit to the board; stabilize with removal and close out with delve creatures. Track their life total and adjust your pressure accordingly.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap threats and discard their answers. Use Brainstorm to dig for threats and force them to use resources on multiple targets. Play around sweepers by committing threats gradually. Force them to make difficult choices between answering threats and developing their board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win on card advantage and better sequencing. Use discard to remove their counterspells and key threats. Hold up counterspells for their key plays and prioritize graveyard advantage. Track their hand and adjust your timing to outmaneuver them in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a tempo/control shell to a more focused game plan based on the metagame. Against hate, bring in recursion or alternative win conditions. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and life gain. Always track what they kept and adjust your sideboard strategy accordingly, keeping your core game plan intact while adapting to specific threats.
notes: Delve creatures are vulnerable to graveyard hate; always have a plan B like direct damage or early pressure. Brainstorm is crucial for consistency—use it to find answers or threats based on board state. Orcish Bowmasters is a fragile but effective early threat; protect it or use it to trade into their removal. Avoid overextending into sweepers and always track the opponent’s graveyard to anticipate delve threats or recursion.

philosophy: This deck aims to close games in 3-5 turns by dealing 10 poison counters through fast mana and early infect threats. It wins by outpacing control and combo decks, leveraging discard and protection to maintain uninterrupted pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, at least one 1-drop (Glistener Elf, Noble Hierarch, or Legolas's Quick Reflexes), and a pump spell or discard. Mulligan if you have zero 1-drops, three or more lands, or only 2-drops with no acceleration. You need a turn-1 playable threat or mana source to execute the game plan.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Noble Hierarch or Glistener Elf. If you have Legolas's Quick Reflexes, play it for immediate haste and +1/+1. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Kongming's Confidant or Sylvan Caryatid, or pump your 1-drop. Attack with infect creatures immediately, prioritizing the face for poison. Sequence mana to ensure you can cast threats and answers on curve without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Tron and Sneak & Show: Bring in Rest in Peace, Gaddock Teeg, and Nihil Spellbomb to disrupt their permanents and graveyard. Vs Dimir Tempo and Izzet Delver: Bring in Swan Song, Counterspell, and discard to protect your threats and remove key answers. Vs Ocelot Pride Midrange: Bring in targeted removal and counterspells to handle their counters and stabilize. Vs mirror: Bring in additional hate and pump spells to win the race.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource for threats and answers, not for storing value. Keep a pump spell or counterspell in hand to protect your poison strategy. Avoid overcommitting to the board if you lack answers. Your graveyard is generally a liability against hate, so prioritize board presence over recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit early threats to apply poison pressure and force difficult blocks. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, hard removal, or counterspells they can activate. Use pump spells to break through blockers or secure lethal. Never attack with your last threat unless you're confident you can close the game or have a backup plan.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron's mana rocks, Sneak & Show's graveyard, Delver's flip condition, Ocelot Pride's counters, and Dimir Tempo's counterspells. Key tells include tapping for multiple colors, drawing extra cards, protecting specific permanents, or holding up mana on your turn. Adjust your aggression based on their available answers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Win by dealing 10 poison counters through combat. Achieve this by pumping infect creatures, attacking with multiple threats, or using pump spells to break through blockers. Ensure you have enough damage to close the game before the opponent can stabilize or race you back.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have two or more infect creatures, a pump spell, and the opponent has no sweepers or hard removal. Also commit when you're at 5+ poison and the opponent is at low life or has no answers. Use discard to remove key answers before attacking.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're at 10+ poison and the opponent has a sweeper, hard removal, or a win condition you can't race. Also concede if you're dead to the draw (no lands, no threats, no answers) or if the matchup is mathematically unwinnable due to hate cards. Don't bluff through known answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells before casting threats. Cast threats in response to counterspells if possible, or use pump spells to make threats too large to counter. Attack with multiple threats to force multiple counterspells. If they have only cheap counters, pump your creatures to bypass them.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Deploy hate cards like Rest in Peace and Gaddock Teeg early to disrupt their mana. Apply pressure before they stabilize. Use targeted removal on key mana sources. If they ramp past your hate, use counterspells and pump spells to race or stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use removal and Path to Exile on early threats to stabilize. Hold counterspells for their finishers. You can often race aggro decks if you stabilize and apply poison pressure. Don't overextend into their combat tricks or sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure and avoid letting them stabilize. Use discard to remove key answers and protection spells. Pump threats to go over their removal. Attack with multiple threats to force difficult decisions. If they have a sweeper, hold back and wait for a better turn.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race to 10 poison. Prioritize faster mana, better pump spells, and hate cards against each other's threats. Win by out-resourcing, applying poison faster, and using discard to remove their answers. Track their poison counters and adjust your aggression accordingly.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw aggression to a balanced mix of pressure and disruption. Bring in hate cards for combo and ramp, counterspells for control, and targeted removal for aggro. Adjust your land count if necessary (typically 22-23 lands) to ensure consistency. Prioritize answers that protect your poison strategy and disrupt the opponent's game plan.
notes: Common mistakes include overextending into sweepers, forgetting to count poison counters, and mismanaging pump spells. Always track the opponent's life total versus your poison damage. Legolas's Quick Reflexes can be pumped for immediate lethal, so sequence it carefully. Fast mana enables turn-2 poison, but don't sacrifice consistency for speed. Watch for opponent's mana development and adjust your aggression based on their available answers.

philosophy: Jeskai Stoneblade is a control-oriented midrange deck that stabilizes the early game with efficient removal and card filtering, then transitions to a late-game win condition by using Stoneforge Mystic to find Emrakul, the Aeons Torn or Umezawa’s Jitte. It wins by out-grinding opponents through card advantage, protecting its finisher, and leveraging Emrakul’s game-swinging abilities to close out games against control, combo, and midrange strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a turn-1 interaction (Path to Exile, Go for the Throat, or a counterspell) or a Ponder/Brainstorm into a land. Keep hands with Stoneforge Mystic if you have a land and a way to protect it or cast it on turn 2. Mulligan pure curve decks, hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or hands with multiple Ponders/Brainstorms but no lands. Ideal opening hand: 1-2 lands, 1-2 spells (interaction/filtering), possibly SFM.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a Ponder or Brainstorm to dig for interaction or SFM. If you have a land and interaction, hold for potential threats. Turn 2: Cast Stoneforge Mystic if you have a land and a way to protect it or cast it. If you have a land and a threat/interaction, play a land and cast a spell. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast SFM on turn 2 or 3 while maintaining mana for removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Tron/Sneak & Show: Bring in Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment, Surgical Extraction, or discard effects. Against Izzet Delver/Ocelot Pride: Bring in additional counterspells (Daze, Spell Snare), fast removal (Lightning Strike, Go for the Throat), and graveyard hate. Against Dimir Tempo/Mirror: Bring in additional card draw (Opt, Ponder), counterspells, and protection spells (Swords to Plowshares, Path to Exile). Against Aggro: Bring in fast removal, discard, and potentially Leyline of Punishment.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize card advantage; never cast Ponder/Brainstorm if you can avoid it, but use them to dig for answers or SFM. Manage mana to ensure you can cast SFM on turn 2/3 while keeping open mana for removal. Protect your graveyard from hate effects, but don't overcommit to protecting it if it means losing the board.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have sufficient interaction to protect them. Hold back removal for key threats (Tron combo pieces, Sneak & Show creatures, Delver). Use SFM to find Emrakul when the board is clear or when you have protection ready.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Tron's mana rocks, Sneak & Show's graveyard setup, Delver's flip triggers, and Ocelot Pride's midrange threats. Note when opponents are holding up mana for counterspells or removal. Track graveyard contents for potential Sneak & Show or Emrakul recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emrakul, the Aeons Torn is the primary win condition; its enter-the-battlefield trigger and ongoing effect dominate the board. Umezawa’s Jitte is a secondary finisher for closing out games against control or midrange.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Emrakul on the board with protection, or when you've stabilized and have enough resources to trade up. Commit Jitte when you can attack safely or when you have removal to clear blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo (Sneak & Show) without sideboard hate and have no interaction, or if you're in a control mirror with no card advantage and the opponent has established a dominant board. Concede if you have a dead hand (multiple Ponders/Brainstorms with no lands or answers) and no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig for answers or protection. Cast SFM and threats in response to counterspells if possible. Use cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter. Consider sideboarded counterspells or discard to disrupt their hand.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize hate bears (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment) to shut down their mana acceleration. Use efficient removal to deal with their threats before they can stabilize. Protect Emrakul from their combo finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early interaction (Path, Go for the Throat, Lightning Strike). Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig for answers. Once stabilized, find Emrakul or Jitte to close out the game. Sideboard in additional fast removal and discard.
threat_responses.against_control: Race for card advantage and protect your finisher. Use SFM to find Emrakul when the board is clear. Apply pressure with Jitte if Emrakul is vulnerable. Out-value them with card draw and efficient removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Use Ponder/Brainstorm to dig deeper. Protect your SFM and Emrakul with counterspells or discard. Trade efficiently and prioritize graveyard hate if recursion is a threat.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite based on the metagame. Against combo and control, prioritize hate bears, counterspells, and discard to disrupt their game plan. Against aggro and midrange, bring in fast removal and protection spells. Always ensure your sideboard plan aligns with your primary win condition; if Emrakul is vulnerable, consider Jitte or additional protection. Maintain a balance between hate and interaction to avoid running out of resources.
notes: Ponder and Brainstorm are essential for consistency; never mulligan hands with them unless you have no lands or interaction. Protect Stoneforge Mystic at all costs; if it dies, your game plan stalls. Emrakul’s enter-the-battlefield trigger is a free card draw and board wipe; use it to reset the board when you’re behind. Be mindful of graveyard hate; if Rest in Peace is on the board, pivot to Jitte or other non-graveyard-dependent win conditions. Common mistake: Overcommitting to Ponder/Brainstorm without a plan, or failing to protect Emrakul from targeted removal.

philosophy: A high-impact control/combo deck that leverages superior mana acceleration (Cloudpost + Darksteel Citadel + Moxen) to cast game-warping spells like Kozilek's Command and Karn, the Great Creator. It aims to out-value and out-resource opponents through card advantage and flexible, high-impact spells, winning against midrange, combo, and control decks that rely on permanents or board presence.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ mana sources (Cloudpost, Darksteel Citadel, Moxen, or fetches). Keep hands with at least one Turn 1 play and a Turn 2 spell (Kozilek's Command, Karn, or a threat). Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no early mana, or only late-game spells with no acceleration. Hands with 1 land and 2+ Moxen are keepers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Mox or Cloudpost/Darksteel Citadel. If you have two mana rocks, play them. Turn 2: If you have 3+ mana, cast Kozilek's Command or Karn. Sequence Cloudpost carefully—it enters tapped, so you need another mana source to cast spells the turn it hits. Darksteel Citadel is crucial for hexproof and consistent mana.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Tron/Sneak & Show: Bring in Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, or Disrupting Shoal. Against Dimir Tempo/Izzet Delver: Bring in Spell Pierce, Force of Will, or Negate. Against Ocelot Pride/Midrange: Bring in Field of Ruin, additional counters, or artifact hate. Generally, sideboard in more interaction and hate based on the top 3 matchups.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your biggest asset but also your vulnerability. Don't overextend into hate. Use Moxen strategically to enable spells when you need them. Keep Cloudpost in play for late-game mana, but be aware of land destruction. Manage hand size—Kozilek's Command can draw cards, so use it to refill when you're low.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can answer their interaction. Hold back Kozilek's Command if they have a lethal threat on the board and you can't answer it. Use Karn to exile key permanents or find answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for land destruction (Wasteland, Field of Ruin), artifact hate (Rest in Peace, Null Rod), and combo win conditions. Pay attention to when they tap out for interaction vs. when they're holding up counters. Track their graveyard for Sneak & Show or Delver strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kozilek's Command (destroy all nonbasic lands, draw cards, or deal damage), Karn's ultimate (exile target permanent, then draw cards), or Thundertrap Trainer. Usually, it's a combination of card advantage and a game-ending spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have enough mana to cast your win condition and have answered or ignored their interaction. When they're at low life and you can close the game. When you've established board control and they have no answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're locked out of your mana base (multiple Wastelands/Field of Ruins), have no answers to their combo, or are facing a matchup where your game plan is completely neutralized (e.g., heavy artifact hate with no way to protect Citadel).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek's Command's split modes or Karn's abilities to bypass counters. Cast spells in response to their counters, or use mana rocks to cast multiple spells in a turn. Sometimes you can't beat counters, so focus on out-value and protecting your win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with Moxen and Cloudpost. Use Kozilek's Command to destroy their mana rocks or nonbasics. Karn can exile their key ramp pieces. Force them to use resources on you while you set up.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use counterspells (Force of Will, Spell Pierce) to stall. Use Kozilek's Command to bounce or deal damage. Cloudpost's late-game mana will outscale them if you survive. Don't overcommit to early threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and flexible spells. Use Karn to exile their key permanents. Force them to use resources on you while you set up your win condition. Be patient and wait for them to blink.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race for card advantage and protection. Prioritize protecting your Cloudpost/Citadel. Use Kozilek's Command to destroy their nonbasics. Sideboard in more hate or counterspells. Win by out-resourcing and forcing them to make mistakes.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Bring in more hate against permanents (Rest in Peace, Disrupting Shoal), more counters against spell-heavy decks, and additional protection for your mana base. Generally, increase your interaction count and add specific hate for the top 3 matchups. Consider bringing in additional land destruction or artifact hate depending on prevalence.
notes: Cloudpost enters tapped, so sequence carefully. Darksteel Citadel is your best land for consistency and hexproof. Kozilek's Command is incredibly flexible—use the right mode for the situation. Don't overcommit to one spell; preserve resources. Thundertrap Trainer likely benefits from your mana base—use it to pressure or combo. Common mistake: playing Cloudpost without a way to cast spells the turn it hits, or ignoring land destruction. Always track their graveyard and hand for Sneak & Show or Delver strategies.

philosophy: A fast, disruptive control-combo hybrid that uses fast mana and Trinisphere to lock opponents out of resources while developing a board or casting game-ending threats. It aims to outpace tempo and combo decks by applying early pressure and denying the opponent's ability to interact.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands or 1 land + 2 fast mana sources, plus a turn-1 or turn-2 play. Mulligan hands with no fast mana, no lands, or multiple low-impact cards. Prioritize hands that can cast Trinisphere by turn 2 or 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lotus Petal or Chrome Mox. Turn 2: Use fast mana to cast Trinisphere if possible, or develop board with early threats. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit Trinisphere's mana tax while maintaining your own acceleration.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Ocelot Pride): Bring in board wipes, removal, and artifact hate. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir Tempo, Izzet Delver): Bring in counterspell hate, additional fast mana, and threat upgrades. vs Combo (Sneak and Show): Bring in graveyard hate, additional Trinisphere copies, and artifact destruction. vs Mirror: Bring in additional disruption and threat density.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve fast mana for key turns (Trinisphere, big threats). Don't overextend into removal. Use Chrome Mox for color fixing or extra mana for spells. Manage hand size to avoid flooding.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can follow up. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard counters. Use Trinisphere to force unfavorable trades or lock out key spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), hard counters (Force of Will, Daze), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents are holding up mana for interaction vs. developing their board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large threats (e.g., Krenko, Goblin Warboss; or combo pieces like Goblin Bombardment + Krenko), or Trinisphere lock combined with board presence. Win by overwhelming the opponent's mana denial or out-resourcing them.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Trinisphere on the board, the opponent is locked out of key spells, and you have a clear path to lethal or a win condition. Also commit when you can protect your threats or have removal for their answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of mana by Trinisphere (if they have it), have no removal for their board, and the opponent is on a combo or aggressive plan you can't stabilize against. Also concede if you're drawing dead cards into a locked board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips, multiple threats, or cost reducers to overcome counters. Play around key counters by holding up mana or using uncounterable threats. Consider artifact destruction if counters are tied to artifacts.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on key ramp pieces. Trinisphere can lock them out if they overcommit to mana rocks.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal, board wipes, or early threats. Use Trinisphere to slow their mana development. Prioritize surviving turn 1-3 to set up your game plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and cantrips to find answers. Use Trinisphere to lock out their interaction. Out-value them with card advantage and efficient threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize keeping Trinisphere online. Use disruption to break their mana base. Out-value them with better sequencing and threat density.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw acceleration to targeted disruption and protection. Add more removal for specific threats, increase artifact hate against combo, and adjust land counts for consistency. Prioritize hands that can support the new game plan.
notes: Trinisphere is a double-edged sword; be mindful of your own mana development. Fast mana can lead to flooding, so balance land and spell ratios. Watch for opponent's sideboard cards that target your strategy. Common mistake: overcommitting to Trinisphere without follow-up threats.

philosophy: Mono-Black Pox is a control-aggro hybrid that wins by combining hand disruption, targeted removal, and poison counters. It aims to disrupt the opponent's early game while steadily applying poison pressure, eventually closing out games with Bloodghast or a fully poisoned opponent. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on casting multiple spells or have fragile boards.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, or 3+ lands paired with a Thoughtseize or Inquisition. Always keep a hand containing Pox + a land. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, or 3+ lands with no disruption or Pox. Prioritize hands that can cast Pox by turn 2 or 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play Swamp, cast Thoughtseize or Inquisition if possible. Turn 2: Play Swamp, cast Pox if you have 2 mana, or cast another disruption spell. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Pox by turn 2 or 3. If opponent plays a creature, Thoughtseize it or kill it with Pox/Sinkhole.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Delver, Ocelot Pride): Bring in Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze, Surgical Extraction, and additional spot removal. vs Control/Tempo (Dimir, Tron): Bring in extra Thoughtseize/Inquisition, Bloodghast, Liliana of the Veil, and more removal. vs Combo (Sneak & Show): Bring in Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze, Surgical Extraction, and hand disruption to strip key pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is tight; always prioritize casting Pox to enable your own creatures and disable opponent's. Use Thoughtseize/Inquisition to strip key combo pieces or removal. Manage graveyard carefully; Pox and Bloodghast synergize, but opponent graveyard hate matters.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or Pox ready to answer their answer. Don't overextend into sweepers or spot removal. Use Pox to turn opponent's creatures into 1/1s, making them vulnerable to your removal or poison.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Toxic Deluge), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace), and combo win conditions. Note when they tap out for a counter or removal, as that's your window to cast Pox or attack.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Bloodghast (fueled by Pox and graveyard), Liliana of the Veil (draw + removal), or simply attacking with poisoned creatures. Once opponent is at 5-7 poison counters, commit all creatures.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have board control, opponent has no sweepers/hate in hand, and you can safely attack with 2+ creatures. Also commit when you can cast Pox on their only blocker.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a fast combo that can't be answered by your disruption, or if you're completely locked out by Rest in Peace + no creatures in hand/graveyard. Also concede if you're at 1 life with no blockers and they have a sweeper/removal in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Don't overcommit to expensive spells. Use cheap disruption (Thoughtseize, Inquisition) and Pox (instant speed). Attack with small creatures to force counters, then cast Pox or follow up.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Hand disruption is key. Thoughtseize their mana rocks. Pox their big threats. Don't trade creatures; use spot removal and poison to close out before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early Thoughtseize/Inquisition to strip key threats. Pox to turn their board into 1/1s. Use removal efficiently. Don't overextend; stabilize with blockers and Pox, then poison them out.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply steady poison pressure. Use Thoughtseize to strip their removal/sweepers. Cast Pox to enable your creatures. Force them to use resources on your threats while you grind them down with poison and card advantage (Bloodghast/Liliana).
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a game of resource attrition and graveyard management. Prioritize keeping your graveyard safe. Use Thoughtseize to strip their Pox or Bloodghast. Trade removal efficiently. Win by out-grinding their graveyard and applying poison faster.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure disruption to a mix of disruption and graveyard hate against combo/aggro. Against control, bring in more card advantage and finishers. Always keep at least one copy of Thoughtseize and Pox. Adjust land count if needed (usually 24-26). Prioritize answers to the top metagame threats (Rest in Peace vs Sneak & Show, more removal vs Delver).
notes: Note: Orcish Bowmasters is a Red/Black card and not part of Mono-Black Pox; this guidance assumes the standard Mono-Black Pox list (Thoughtseize, Inquisition, Pox, Bloodghast, Liliana, Sinkhole). Pox is your most important card—always prioritize casting it over attacking. Be mindful of your own graveyard; Pox and Bloodghast rely on it, but opponent hate can shut you down. Track opponent's hand size and resources to know when to go all-in.

philosophy: A fast tempo-combo hybrid that aims to establish an early aerial threat with Delver of Secrets while using cheap disruption and card advantage to out-resource slower strategies. It excels at racing combo and midrange decks while leveraging efficient interaction to control the board.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with Delver of Secrets, Flow State, and Mishra's Bauble. Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably shock lands or duals), 1-2 cantrips (Opt, Ponder, Brainstorm), and 1-2 removal spells. Mulligan flood, hands with 3+ non-basic lands and no early plays, or hands lacking Delver/Flow State/Bauble unless they contain multiple cantrips and removal to dig for the combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then cast Mishra's Bauble if you have no land, or hold it for tempo. Turn 2: Cast Flow State (if Bauble is on the battlefield) or play a land and cast Flow State. Turn 3: Cast Delver of Secrets and attack. Sequence mana carefully, prioritizing shock lands to maintain mana availability for interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2 Lightning Helix, +2 Go for the Throat, +1 Abrupt Decay; -2 Flow State, -1 Mishra's Bauble. vs Control/Tron: +2 Abrupt Decay, +2 Lightning Helix, +1 Thoughtseize/Duress; -2 Flow State. vs Sneak & Show: +2 Rest in Peace, +1 Leyline of Punishment, +2 Abrupt Decay; -2 Flow State, -1 Mishra's Bauble. vs Mirror: +2 Lightning Helix, +2 Go for the Throat, +1 Abrupt Decay; -2 Flow State.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use cantrips to maintain hand size and dig for threats or answers. Avoid overcommitting mana to Flow State without a clear path to Delver. Protect your graveyard, as Delver's flip condition and Flow State's value depend on it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Delver when the opponent lacks removal or has no effective blockers. Use cheap removal to eliminate key threats before they can stabilize. Hold back interaction if the opponent is on a combo or control plan that requires specific pieces to disrupt.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Remand, Spell Pierce, Essence Scatter), sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment). Track opponent's hand size and graveyard to anticipate disruption or combo turns.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Delver of Secrets (flipped) is the primary win condition, supported by Lightning Helix or Go for the Throat to clear blockers or finish the game. Secondary finishers include late-game threats like Tarmogoyf or efficient removal to clear the path.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, no blockers, or is below 10 life. Commit when you have card advantage and the opponent is out of resources to interact.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when facing heavy sweepers, graveyard hate, or when you are behind on board with no way to clear it. Concede if you are drawing dead cards or facing a combo that has already stabilized.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Mishra's Bauble to bait counterspells, then cast Flow State in response. Dig for answers with cantrips and cast cheap spells to pressure through interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to force interaction before they ramp out. Use cheap removal and discard to disrupt mana dorks and key spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and cantrips to dig for answers. Use Delver to trade efficiently and pressure the opponent's life total.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Delver and force interaction. Use removal on key spells and dig for threats to out-value their card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by using removal efficiently and tracking the opponent's hand. Win with better sequencing, card advantage, and by forcing the opponent to overcommit.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a combo-tempo game plan to a more interactive tempo strategy. Increase removal and hate cards to handle specific matchups, reduce Flow State and Bauble if facing graveyard hate or disruption, and adjust land count to ensure consistent mana for interaction. Prioritize hands with early plays and interaction over combo pieces.
notes: Delver's flip condition is crucial; remember to flip it when you cast a noncreature spell. Flow State is a combo piece but also a cantrip, so use it to dig for threats or answers. Mishra's Bauble is essential for tempo and can be used to bait counterspells or protect your hand. Common mistakes include overcommitting mana on Flow State without a plan, forgetting to flip Delver, and underestimating graveyard hate. Watch for Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment, and other graveyard disruption.
